### .lina — 2012-04-16T17:26:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-how-i-feel.628/post-15578>

ok so, the moussaka recipe, just approximately:

first, slice up two aubergine in fingerthick slices that you place on paper

and add salt generously on top to make them sweat out excess liquid.

then fry up 500 grams of minced meat with cinnamon, oregano, cumin,

and black pepper to taste. add a dash of cream to make it sauce-y.

when that is done, wipe the salt off the aubergines, (panfry them if you want

to but it's a matter of taste, we used them raw) and then you layer the following

in an oven pan: aubergine, meat sauce, cheese, aubergine, meat sauce, **cheese**.

bake in oven about as hot and long as you would a lasagna  it's pretty simple.

you can also just do the meatsauce, stuff it in a pitted paprika, cheese ontop, bake. yum!

### .lina — 2012-04-17T09:09:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-how-i-feel.628/post-15830>

by the way, a miracle happened on sunday afternoon.

my mother phoned me up, positively _chirping_ with delight, to tell me that now that my dad has finished his VLCD thing and lost 10 kilos, their natural follow-up is to start eating the way i do, cutting the carbs and eating good healthy fats.

she said she used to have a completely distorted picture of what this meant and assumed it was only bacon and butter all day long, but after me having been eating like this for a year now, and showing her photos of what my meals look like, and treating her to delicious dinners, she's super enthusiastic about low carbing! she had even lost weight while visiting me for easter, and we ate GOOD and drank a lot of delicious wines - she was so impressed she kept lowcarbing whn she got home, and lost 2 more kilos on her own.

so she said she had baked a really delicous carb-free bread, and was about to make a batch of that fake pasta i posted the recipe of here earlier  she was so cute in her gleefulness, you could tell she was really happy about this commitment, which made ME super happy

AND she said that my older sister, who has always been unable to gain weight and always had horrible stomach problems, has started talking really well about lowcarbing to her friends after having seen the change in me, and might be open to testing it herself to finally be able to eat without stomach pains. THIS impressed me the most, because she too has been very suspicious towards this "fat diet" before, but my mom said she's gonna bake some extra bread for her and introduce her to some good dishes to show her it's all good.

it feels like i kinda won the lottery!!! i couldn't be more proud of my dear ones

### FF_BC — 2012-04-17T11:59:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-how-i-feel.628/post-15841>

> **.lina;15636 wrote:**
>
> i just did this weekend as my very first time, and i am not sure if i did everything right because i felt kinda crap
>
>
>
> i went from harmonic, happy, energized, "regular" (ahem), and satiated
>
> to brainfogged, lethargic, headachey, constipated, grumpy and craving junkfood.
>
>
>
> and to crown it all, i _gained weight! XD
>
>
>
> did i do it right? i had my last meal at 8pm and first meal the next day at noon.
>
> ate regular sized portions of lunch and dinner, no snacking inbetween (despite gruesome cravings).
>
> _

_

It can be hard the first times, all that ghrelin telling you to eat just because you use to. Did you drink any coffee or tea? That really helps to keep away the hunger  It's just the first few times that the fasting is hard, keep trying! Once you've done a 24h fast, a 16h is nothing ;D Also, physical activity is great to keep hunger away, if I get very hungry I will drop down and do some pushups, that helps

For me the real breakthrough was when I realized that hunger is _not_ the body telling you to eat, it's the body telling you to start looking_ for food, there's a BIG difference!

### .lina — 2012-04-17T14:19:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-how-i-feel.628/post-15849>

i did have coffee for "breakfast" and found that i actually CAN drink it without adding

some milk substitute, which was good to know, i've been gagging at black coffee before.

but yeah i'll give it another go sometime, it's just hard to pep myself for it when i've gotten

my best weightloss results from regular 3 lowcarb meals a day and a glass or two of wine,

all while feeling absolutely awesome and energetic  the choice seems easier then, hehe.

still, i am gonna stay openminded towards it and maybe it'll feel better at a later stage.

thanks for the support though!

well we did anyway, i just took a sun chair and bossed my better half around while he built the planters to my liking  and then he read on some app that there might be frost coming later this week... oh boy. but it was SO warm today, i'm hoping for forecast changes.

anyway, here's the goods and the goodies for your ocular pleasure;

chuck of ox with a creamy mushroom soup and some red onion

### .lina — 2012-05-09T14:10:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-how-i-feel.628/post-22515>

haha! thank you all. i have been a bit tied up to make more serious efforts at snapping pics of food,

but i will have some new soon. all in all i have had a good week, and stuck to my food, workouts and CT.

infact, i started my day today (had t get up at 5am!) with ice strapped to the small of my back after

some all too vigorous gardening yesterday  the cold felt good and relieving. last night i did my facedunks

with one cat sticking his head next to mine to drink the cold water, and one on my shoulders, purring.

lovely to see the "family" engaging themselves in my activities... ;D

until i have more food photos, here are pics i snapped of my boyfriend, first pics from two years ago at a

standard swedish diet, and then pics in the same clothes from a few weeks ago, after a year of (non-strict)

LCHF and since a few months he's started doing the cold showers thing as well.

he went from 87 kilos (192 lbs) in february 2011 to 65 (143 lbs) in february 2012, and has started

working on regaining some weight in form of muscles. which also seems to come easy to him,

i'm not jealous at all yes i am

but hey, i'm at a new record low weight again, i had to replace my favourite jeans with a size smaller, yay!

### .lina — 2012-08-05T14:40:28+0300

thank you!  i am doing well, just have so much time outdoors during the summer,

and less computer time - which is also nice. my weight had a bit of a plateau but i

manage my diet pretty well and things seem to be budging again. i am working way

more seafood into my diet, and we are gonna build a new wooden deck for the back

yard and then i will set up our big inflatable pool and resume CT:ing properly.

i need more vacation ;D the summer arrived lately here in sweden and now the sun

is shining but i have to sit in an office all day. but the days are long and we get some

nice summery evenings and dinners in the garden.

speaking of garden, my zucchinis are finally coming in after i gave them more dirt! YAY!

such pleasure in growing your own food. maybe i should dig a pond and fish my own dinners...

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

and the recipe for the fish soup / stew, my dears:

\- whatever fish/seafood you want - i had salmon, cod, mussles and crayfish-tails.

\- some veggies of choice - i had leek, squash, fresh mushrooms, half an onion and some yellow bellpepper.

\- a lemon.

\- sambal oelek (or curry).

\- lime- or lemonpepper.

\- 100g philadelphia or other cream cheese (optional if paleo, i guess).

\- 3dl heavy cream or coconut milk.

first fry up the onion, mushrooms and leek in butter.

then add the diced bellpepper and squash.

add the cream and cream cheese (or just coconutmilk).

add the spices, sambal oelek (or curry), and the juice of half a lemon.

dice and add the fish, let stew. (i added the mussles now)

when the fish has gotten whiter in the flesh, take the pot off the stove and stir in the shrimp or crayfish.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-07T10:38:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-how-i-feel.628/post-49942>

Hi .lina I also grow zucchini in my garden - they are a never fail produce! They grow so prolifically and like you say one plant will be growing 5 at once and in different sizes. I try not let them grow too big as the skin gets tough when they are big. But they are a great vegetable to have chopped into stir frys, grated in quiche (and hidden from the children who don't like them much!), and they are delicious fried up with onions and tomatoes - chunky style. But also something I tried recently - slice them lengthways and use as a substitute for lasagne sheets in lasagne.

I have just read through you blog for the first time and its so inspiring! I will copy some of the recipes out. Thank you so much!

### .lina — 2013-04-29T15:08:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-how-i-feel.628/post-85119>

hi everybody! i've been in working mode all winter and haven't been very active online (save for facebook, heh).
i'm gonna try and kick into gear with my food photography again, looking through my thread here has inspired me

last summer was really crappy in regards of growing my own vegetables, but you wouldn't believe what a winter
we had here in Sweden - we were waist-deep in snow for months upon months, and only first NOW we're seeing signs
of spring arriving... finally! some vitamin D \*not\* in pill-shape ;D

my seedlings are sprouting like mad at home, and the champions of the garden will once again be zucchini,
followed by brussel sprouts and kale. fingers crossed the frost and bugs and "killer slugs" (yeah) will stay away.
but it'll still be about a month before it's hot enough outside to plant it out - right now the only things growing
in my garden are moss and tadpoles...

anyway, nice to be back  see ya around!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-how-i-feel.628/post-91270>

thank you all sooo much  you're too sweet!!!

some updates!

i have started physiotherapy for my back, been at the gym about 2-3 times a week for about 45 minutes,
doing muscle strengthening exercises. it's really fun to see yourself getting stronger! i started in may and
back then i could do 20 reps of 31,5 kilos on one leg on the leg press machine, and now i do 58,5 : D

last week i started experimenting with the (in Sweden very) popular 5:2 fasting, and found i could breeze
by a whole day on just water and coffee with coconut oil. i even work out more efficiently during fasting,
which is a nice revelation, as i usually feel kind of tired and stuffed with food. so now i will synchronize my
fasting days with my workout days.

some pics!

people at work ooh and aaah over my lunchboxes lately, when i harvest whatever grows in my garden, to add to my proteins:

the rooftop garden delivers! colleagues were really happy to have a taste of our own produce:

my kale is going craaaazy!

# Thread: Are Leaky Gut, Silent Reflux, and Toe Nail Fungus Related?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 37
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### curves64 — 2012-04-03T23:34:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/post-10960>

Dr. Kruse. I am new here and am amazed at the plethora of relevant information. Every post seems to lead me to a deeper understanding of how to best optimize my health. By way of background, i am a 54-year-old female, 5'4", 128 pounds, and gain weight easily. I can't say I'm new to paleo as I was diagnosed with a wheat allergy in my 30s, and have limited (not eliminated) carbs ever since. I recently saw an ENT for a low level chronic sore throat, and was diagnosed with **Silent Reflux. I have also had persistent **toe nail fungus** for over 20 years. After reading your blog, I am wondering if both the Silent Reflux and toe nail fungus are related to Leaky Gut**? I did start the LR and CT a week ago and already notice I am not hungry between meals and sleep more soundly. I would be most appreciative if you would let me know your thoughts on the toe nail fungus. Thanks again!

### Jim — 2012-04-04T00:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/post-10971>

curves64,

I have a leaky gut and toe nail fungus. I believe my leaky gut is much better (cured?) and the toe nail fungus seems to be getting better. I believe that eating paleo (i.e. staying away from grains) and CT have helped both conditions. I don't have reflux so I can't comment on that personally but my wife had it bad for the longest time. She took Prilosec all the time. We didn't know anything about paleo or CT at the time but she lost weight via a low carb diet and that seemed to do the trick. So given her history I would say yes to the reflux too.

Good luck!

### curves64 — 2012-04-04T00:23:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/post-10989>

Thank you Jim! Can you tell me specifically which toe nail fungus symptoms have improved by eating paleo and doing CT? After over 20 years of aggressive and unsuccessful treatments, I want to relish in your success!

### Jim — 2012-04-04T20:38:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/post-11392>

Well it ain't over yet. I have it on two toes on my right foot. I use an anti-fungal on it more regularly now and it seems as though it is not regenerating in the lower part of the nail. So I am thinking that as the nail grows out, the remaining fungus will move with it. Not sure if it's the lack of carbs, the topical anti-fungal or the cold that is working but something is preventing expansion.

Chris Kresser has some info on it.

How to prevent spending the last 10 years of your life in a diaper and a wheelchair [http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-spending-the-last-10-years-of-your-life-in-a-diaper-and-a-wheelchair](http://http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-spending-the-last-10-years-of-your-life-in-a-diaper-and-a-wheelchair)

### Jane — 2012-08-24T21:00:41+0300

I had toenail fungus for years that nothing could touch, not even anti-fungals internally and externally. Somebody told me to put Vics Vapo Rub on it. I used it every day for a month and the fungus disappeared. That was a year ago and the fungus has never come back!

grapefruit seed extract - paint it on the toes and put about 20 drops in 32oz of water - also put tree tree oil, and coconut oil on it too...

remember most antifungals are derived from flouride... think diflucan... while it works, it effs up your semicondutors....

I've tried everything I've ever read on the internet, any doctor has every told me, any medication (which I wouldn't take now but way back then I was desperate) including lamisil which gave me a total body rash. I do think it's related to GERD because the only time it cleared up was when was on HCG back in 2012. I know it's a message from my body that somethings not right but what exactly?

Her toes are all good! To be honest I had forgotten about it....so its been a while since its reared its ugly head. Since moving we have seen all things fungal leave our bodies. Nails and candida gone for the whole family and it couldnt be diet because our diets are all different. Its change of zip code. crazy stuff.

Try taking your toes outside for an ice bath and sunshine....use the cold to strengthen the UV and let the UV kill that shit

### Curves — 2015-10-23T01:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/post-180199>

@[cantweight](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1283/) and @[JMO](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/403/) you have given me hope!

When I lived in SF, I hoped that all the ocean swimming I was doing would clear it. But, no matter how much I played in the cold water the fungus persisted. Of course, my exposure to nnEMF was waaaay higher then.

BUT, that was then and this is now. Then,, I didn't understand the power of the sun and surface chemistry but now I do.

So, time to take your advice and kill that shit!" I put a black light at my feet level on my desk for when I can't get outside and will do intermittent "toe dunks." to increase the UV power with cold. Thanks so much!!!!

### Curves — 2015-10-29T17:04:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/post-180714>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=180674)
>
> Surprise!!!!

My biohack of this 25 year old toe nail fungus is this: (1) to get outside whenever possible and soak up the UV/IR even if cloudy (2) when having to be indoors, use a UV bulb under my my desk at toe level; (3) toe dunks or ice occasionally (4) get 20 minutes of red light therapy 5x a week at the local tanning salon (my whole body likes this one).

I know this might sound odd, but, my toe nails "feel" a bit different already. They have a different sensation.

Any other recommendations Dr. K? An IR bulb at toe level perhaps? Quantlets on my ankles

### Curves — 2015-10-29T21:33:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/post-180741>

@[JMO](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/403/) is this the UFO light you are using and like so much ? [http://www.amazon.com/BloomBoss-UFO-LED-Grow-Light/dp/B0031QWXPU](http://www.amazon.com/BloomBoss-UFO-LED-Grow-Light/dp/B0031QWXPU) thanks!!

### Beepsilver — 2023-08-28T20:13:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/post-325647>

I've made some progress on my toenail fungus that I'd like to share--the remedy? Not sure what that is exactly because I've done several things and the fungus appears to be going away, so you decide.

I've had a toenail fungus on my big toe for several decades. I've taken lamosil pills two different times and the fungus goes away, but comes right back. I've tried soaking in vinegar, using Vicks vapor rub and dermatology-provided topicals. I replaced socks/shoes but none of it worked. Every week or so I'd try my best to trim the ugly distal end of the nail back which was an effort because the affected part of the nail was still attached to the nail bed. The fungus also liked to eat away at the skin around the nail--it was a poor sight.

I always wore boots/shoes because I was still in the military and always stationed in cold climates. I avoided the sun and wore sunscreen because several dermatologists I'd seen told me that's what I needed to do after having having no less than 10 skin cancers removed (basal and squamous cell) over the past 20 years.

Two years ago I moved from Nebraska to the Florida panhandle near the Gulf of Mexico. Toenail problem persisted. However about a year ago I discovered Dr Kruse and became hooked after watching the Nourish Vermont series on youtube (PS, Uncle Jack, it'd be nice if you'd make the 2018 Nourish Vermont video accessible again...at least to paying members, which I'm not, but would be if you unlocked the video ). Since then I've watched, listened and read as much as I possibly could to optimize my health. I wear the blue blocking glasses, turned off circuit breakers, use candles at night, etc. But what have I done to make toenail progress?

I can count on one hand how many times I've put shoes and socks on in the past 3-4 months. I either go barefoot, wear flip flops or Earth Runner sandals. I see the sunrise practically every morning in a speedo and over the past several months that includes feet in the surf at the beach 4-5 days a week. I usually spend two to three hours out there--75% of the time my feet are wet in one or two inches of water. Some days if I get up early or if there's a thunderstorm with lightning I use a Ruby 250W reptile lamp from Amazon on the front of my shins close to the ankle (be careful, you can and will burn yourself if you try to tolerate the heat...two feet away is good). Other days I'll use the EMR Tek light I recently got instead. I probably use the red light 3 or 4 days a week. Also I started doing CT in a cold outdoor pool last year, but the water temperature only dipped below 55 a few times, so even though I was technically becoming cold adapted, it wasn't the ideal exposure. However 3 months ago I was able to get into a water-filled freezer a handful of times, but the owner was hard to schedule with (and I don't think he appreciated that I wanted to use it every day). Not only that, I couldn't handle temps in the high 30s for anything more than 5 minutes. So I've recently started adding ice to the water in my bathtub and consistently stay 20 minutes at temps between 50-55 degrees. My tub is smallish, so after 20 minutes I roll over and do a couple of minutes of face dunking (shaking your head "no" under water speeds up the brain freeze ). (I pay for the ice until I get a freezer of my own)--I'm lean and weigh around 145ish so I'm a shivering mess afterward. Finally, the last couple of things I do is eat local seafood daily, gulf oysters/shrimp/scallps/fish which is loaded with iodine among other things. And every day over the past 6 months I've been having one serving of the sea vegetable: Maine Dulse Flakes (Amazon) which is also high in iodine. For what it's worth I've been eating mostly high fat carnivore since Dec 2020. I don't take supplements of any kind and am on no medications.

So why am I more excited about my toenail progress this time versus when I saw my toenail grow out normally after dosing with Lamosil? Because this time the fungus portion of the nail has completely pulled away from the nail bed and I was able to cut it off completely, exposing half the nail bed. Also the skin around the nail has completely healed and looks normal. So while I wait for my nail to grow out I'm keeping my fingers crossed that I staying my current course my nail will eventually grow out normally.

As an aside and maybe Dr Jack can chime in here with an explanation, but I had real difficulty tanning my face vs my body until recently. My body could take an hour or more of Florida July solar noon sun, but my face would burn after 5 minutes. I only recently discovered that if immediately after doing CT (I'd shiver-walk outside and lay face up on a cotton towel during midday sun for 40 minutes before I rolled over)--no facial burn! Also no sweating--granted it takes me 10 minutes just to stop shivering, so it's easy to understand the no sweating. I begin to sweat after I flip over, so often I will go get back in the tub (having left the cold water in it) to cool off again before going back outside to finish UVB exposure. Just FYI, I've tried face dunking only on a non-CT day and it didn't seem to have the same effect with regard to facial exposure to solar noon sun. BTW, I had my Vitamin D 25OH tested in June and it was at a whopping 116! I can only imagine what it is now.

### JanSz — 2023-08-28T20:46:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-leaky-gut-silent-reflux-and-toe-nail-fungus-related.634/post-325651>

Because this time the fungus portion of the nail has completely pulled away from the nail bed and I was able to cut it off completely, exposing half the nail bed. Also the skin around the nail has completely healed and looks normal. So while I wait for my nail to grow out I'm keeping my fingers crossed that I staying my current course my nail will eventually grow out normally.
>
> As an aside and maybe Dr Jack can chime in here with an explanation, but I had real difficulty tanning my face vs my body until recently. My body could take an hour or more of Florida July solar noon sun, but my face would burn after 5 minutes. I only recently discovered that if immediately after doing CT (I'd shiver-walk outside and lay face up on a cotton towel during midday sun for 40 minutes before I rolled over)--no facial burn! Also no sweating--granted it takes me 10 minutes just to stop shivering, so it's easy to understand the no sweating. I begin to sweat after I flip over, so often I will go get back in the tub (having left the cold water in it) to cool off again before going back outside to finish UVB exposure. Just FYI, I've tried face dunking only on a non-CT day and it didn't seem to have the same effect with regard to facial exposure to solar noon sun. BTW, I had my Vitamin D 25OH tested in June and it was at a whopping 116! I can only imagine what it is now.

Forgot to add, no basal or squamous cell carcinomas since seeing the "real" light

# Thread: Took the kids in for a celiac panel
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-04-04T00:43:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-11005>

The kids had their annual physicals today. And since my IgA is still off the charts we decided to run ceilac panels on both since the do get eczema when they eat wheat in large qtys.

Drawing blood on a 2yo and 4yo isn't that fun. Took two separate sticks to get the boy. The girl was very brave even though she cried when it was her turn (she went first). The interesting thing was she was more upset when it was her brothers turn (she's very sensitive - I never realized how much until today though)

But at least I'll know now if the have any issues and if they don't I still plan on keeping the crap away from them

Also we're taking ds for an evaluation on his speech and hearing b/c he's not talking much and is having problems pronouncing the words he does know. Based on Drs initial eval and discussion w/us about his speech patters she thinks it might be auditory. Guess well see when we get the appointment

I'm exhausted from this afternoon!!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-04T00:59:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-11010>

Did the doc say it was likely to get accurate results? My impression was that blood testing was primarily useful only if you've been eating at least 2 servings of wheat per day for the last two months. Of course, you can do the gene test, too, but that doesn't prove you've got it, just susceptibility.

I'm running through the questions b/c I'm considering this for my children, too, largely to know (and have backup for the grandparents) whether a 100% no wheat policy is needed. Currentyl they have it about once/month, but when they get a lot at the grands', they all have symptoms. Different ones for every child.

I'm looking at the fecal fat malabsorption and antigliadin sIgA for them if bloodwork isn't the way to go. How did you get your antigliadin results?

### colleencoble — 2012-04-04T01:00:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-11012>

You probably would have been better off to get stool panels, Gretchen. Those blood tests are notorious for false negatives.  Hope you get to the truth though!

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-04T01:05:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-11014>

Yeah but we'll start slow and work our way up

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-04T01:10:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-11017>

They eat wheat at DCP b/c its part of the state program. So they are getting more than I would prefer. She did say we should retest in a couple of yrs to reverify. But b/c the get eczema when they eat a lot it would be good to know. Also the boys speech/hearing may be impacted by food allergies as well. So well see if I need too well do stool samples w/both and go from there. Those will be ezpz especially w/the boy b/c he's still wearing diapers.

### MJ* — 2012-04-04T07:11:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-11165>

> **Shijin13;11074 wrote:** The kids had their annual physicals today. And since my IgA is still off the charts we decided to run ceilac panels on both since the do get eczema when they eat wheat in large qtys.
>
>
>
> Drawing blood on a 2yo and 4yo isn't that fun. Took two separate sticks to get the boy. The girl was very brave even though she cried when it was her turn (she went first). The interesting thing was she was more upset when it was her brothers turn (she's very sensitive - I never realized how much until today though)
>
>
>
> But at least I'll know now if the have any issues and if they don't I still plan on keeping the crap away from them
>
>
>
> Also we're taking ds for an evaluation on his speech and hearing b/c he's not talking much and is having problems pronouncing the words he does know. Based on Drs initial eval and discussion w/us about his speech patters she thinks it might be auditory. Guess well see when we get the appointment
>
>
>
> I'm exhausted from this afternoon!!

How old is your boy? Does he have a lot of allergies and does he get a lot of ear infections? Sometimes this can be the reason kids have speech difficulties and it should be ruled out before the speech issues get pinned on an auditory processing issue...it can be as simple as fluid filled tubes that can sometimes be so chronic that it's not picked up, especially if he's young...if you haven't looked into that, consider checking it out...

### MJ* — 2012-04-04T07:13:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-11168>

> **Shijin13;11086 wrote:** They eat wheat at DCP b/c its part of the state program. So they are getting more than I would prefer. She did say we should retest in a couple of yrs to reverify. But b/c the get eczema when they eat a lot it would be good to know. Also the boys speech/hearing may be impacted by food allergies as well. So well see if I need too well do stool samples w/both and go from there. Those will be ezpz especially w/the boy b/c he's still wearing diapers.

Ok I just saw this, so he has a lot of allergies, you may want to see an ENT doc to make sure it's not fluid...

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-04T15:35:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-11233>

> **MJ\*;11237 wrote:** Ok I just saw this, so he has a lot of allergies, you may want to see an ENT doc to make sure it's not fluid...

No allergies that we know of...just the ezcema when lots of wheat is consumed. doing the early intervention then we go from there based upon the recommendation; I anticipate we'll look at both allergies, check hearing, see an ENT, and maybe even start speech therapy...

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-04T19:10:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-11348>

My youngest was diagnosed non-coeliac gluten-intolerant when she was 4 - she had the blood test and the results were off the scale, so we took her off all foods containing wheat immediately (we had to re-introduce them for her biopsy, which was hell - she was really, really ill then). Within a week she started to sleep better, her eczema started to clear up, her speech improved (but not her hearing - she is partially deaf in one ear, so it is worth having a hearing test done) and her school commented on how much her behaviour improved. When she had the re-introduction to gluten for her biopsy, it was back to square one and then some - and disappointingly her biopsy was clear, hence the non-coeliac gluten intolerant label as she is so symptomatic when she eats anything containing gluten! She also has multiple allergies which clear up considerably, although not completely, when following a primal/paleo diet, and she is sadly hypermobile and dislocates her hips, shoulders and kneecaps with the greatest of ease ...

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-04T19:16:43+0300

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-17T11:03:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-15835>

Gretchen we really need to F2F! Probably should ask Stacy to join us!

As you know from my postings, I have walked just about every step you are talking about. I swear I should have a second degree in nutrition and child development by now, but of course as you know... the more you know, the more you know there is that you don't know.

First, I do NOT want to discount that there might be issues with a child, but I want to encourage everyone who reads this thread, that children do not develop in a linear fashion, all have issues of some kind or another, and we are actually blessed when they are obvious enough that we can treat them without someone hitting us on the head with a 2x4. After nearly a decade with a child with special needs, I can sit in a room of preschool children and pick out the ones that need evals. And while I will not get in a parent's face about it, I will talk to a teacher. Never to I ask them to divulge confidential information, I just flat out give them my Mom Who Knows assessment, and remind them of their obligation to bring these issues to a parent's attention.

Yes go for the celiac tests - IgA and DNA if available. That said, we all know there are ways to have gut issues without these tests coming up positive. In your situation though - with the DCP, I would hope that you get some tangible data that you can work with. Jack or someone with more allergy experience can correct me, but my pediatrician told me that a child's enzymatic function totally changes when they hit 2 years of age. So what is happening at 12 mos and 30 mos can be very, very different and you are chasing a moving train. That is why if nothing is seriously wrong, try not to tinker too much - the little guy is doing his own tinkering. And there are notes in the literature that withholding Ige mediated allergens - particularly dairy in children under 5 - increases the chances that the allergy will be "outgrown".

On the speech/language... The Late Talker by Marilyn Agen was the source recommended to me and how we started fish oil supplements. The book has some really good common sense guidelines on where a child's language development should be. I would take note of those guidelines and make some notes of what you remember before you start forgetting. it will help the speech eval. And PLEASE, try not to stress. The absolutely best thing you can do for speech is talk to your child with tons of eye contact - the latter being a more important indicator of behavioral issues, the former being more related to neuro/motor development. Most importantly, look for progress. Is his language improving? And I put lots of weight on the gold standard of 2 word noun-verb phrases at 2 years of age.

On fecal fat/malabsoprtion tests - we have passed those with flying colors too and still show signs of leaky gut (Jack's assessment). So what am I doing? The same thing I did years ago... modifying the diet as best I can by adding really good foods and watching the problem ones get nudged out. I know this may sound like the dreaded "moderation", but with kids with behavior issues - who wants them to cope with "die off". How do you explain that to a kid?

Collecting the ff test - they should give you a collection kit - ask for a "hat" - Like the one they use in the hospital post partum to check your urine output. You will need to get your son to stand and pee first and then sit to do the rest of the business. The bigger pain is you need to then take it to the lab pronto. Yes, I've rolled in hot on the ER at Ft B to get an after hours gift into the lab system. LOL - not.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-17T11:22:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-15839>

> **Sem;12655 wrote:** I have been lurking on this forum but this is my first post. You guys are so inspirational. I am trying really hard to switch my kids over to a paleo/primal lifestyle. They have lots of allergies, both food and environmental. They both have eczema. My oldest especially can be very difficult to handle. He's 8 tomorrow (Easter!) but still has frequent meltdowns, oppositional behavior and has been diagnosed with ADHD. He's on meds for that bit I really don't feel good about it. We tried to take him off but it seemed like his anxiety got worse. I don't know where to turn for help. Doctors just propose more drugs and poo poo any talk of dietary change. My family is downright hostile about it and school and activities are such minefields. I really feel at the end of my rope. Help!

Concur here that everyone is saying to start looking at diet, but I would also be looking at sleep and vitamin D as well - these were recommended to me for my son - which we now need to test.

With respect to the medicines, of course, not ideal. But they don't have to be permanent either. Most ADHD has two components - the working memory part - where the kid can't hang on to the thought they just had or heard from you. And the other is inhibition - the ability to hold a thought off to the side because you are working a different thought and then bring back that other thought at the right time. ADHD meds work on the inhibition part and can be a good temporary crutch to help a child work on wiring working memory, master material that must be learned by rote language (math facts are actually store in the language part of the brain), and other executive function skills. So no I'm not a fan of medication, but it may be worth a try to make some gains in the academic arena. Lots of ADHD kids slide under the radar until about 3rd grade when their executive function issues start to pop out trying to manage more detailed work.

Reasons NOT to do the meds or stop them - crashing emotional meltdowns when coming off them or failure to thrive/weight/growth issues sue to appetite suppression. The latter situation is what kept us from trying them for years. We haven't been successful with them because of these side effects although they were a wonderful help to his visual distraction.

And yes there are days when I wish I could just make him a BP coffee and call it good!!

FWIW - we have done two interventions to aid him - Interactive Metronome (intensively and not just as a component of OT) and CogMed Working Memory Training. Both were done without medicinal intervention in parallel, so we were able to collect data on how these therapies worked. The IM we did first and saw great improvement in prosody of speech. CogMed we were able to actually measure improvement in the working memory and processing speed components of the WISC IQ test.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-17T14:13:54+0300

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-17T15:18:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-15864>

How do we test for BPA? my chiro can get me the heavy metals tests... I think 1st we'll do the GIFX and go from there...

As for the DS' speech - we've talked w/the headstart program - have an eval to fill out - the counselor is of the opinion, that he's just more advanced on motorskills, vs talking (Based upon the phone interview she had w/me) but she still wants to go ahead for the full evaluation - to be on the safe side. they also want us to see an ENT to rule out any hearing issues... so that's next on the list of things to do in my busy life...

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-17T17:00:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-15883>

> **Shijin13;15934 wrote:**
>
>
>
> As for the DS' speech - we've talked w/the headstart program - have an eval to fill out - the counselor is of the opinion, that he's just more advanced on motorskills, vs talking (Based upon the phone interview she had w/me) but she still wants to go ahead for the full evaluation - to be on the safe side. they so want us to see an ENT to rule out any hearing issues... so that's next on the list of things to do in my busy life...

Yes, the hearing tests are very helpful. OEM and ABR were all fine, but where the break down happened for us was in the auditory processing - something that you can't test for until at least age 7 (there are no established norms below that age) GAP testing, Dichotic listening , Listening in Noise are all tests that can be pursued later if language or an executive function disorder seems lurking.

Childfind within FCPS has been FANTASTIC, but this is where I will share that Speech/Language issues have to be worked both from an educational standpoint and a medical standpoint. For example Tricare and some other insurances don't cover SLT after age 3 unless certain criteria are met, as they feel it can be addressed by school system based services. Accurate diagnosis of any comorbid deficiencies can make a major difference in what services can be obtained through the medical community.

I would also offer that if there are no major hearing/listening deficiencies, that emphasizing music will make a huge difference in speech. Lots of poetry reading, tons of melodic music, and since his motor skills are great, plenty of percussion toys.

There is a book called Disconnected Kids, which talks plenty about right/left brain dominance and how it plays out for kids not only with ASD's or ADHD, but any child. It gets pretty detailed on how to rewire the brain with simple exercises to restore balanced processing.

But I stop here, before I continue to unleash the decade of information that is packed in my head. I am convinced that your little guy is just all boy and it won't be long before you are asking him to stop interrupting you!

### Mud Flinger — 2012-04-21T01:52:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-17261>

> **cgk;15953 wrote:** But I stop here, before I continue to unleash the decade of information that is packed in my head. I am convinced that your little guy is just all boy and it won't be long before you are asking him to stop interrupting you!

This was so true with our son. He barely spoke before 2 and with all the autism talk... well just plain scary!

Hopefully your son is just a normal kid who is speaking later just like so many boys. They just are not as verbal as girls are!

### Kiera2 — 2012-04-21T06:10:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-17331>

It's facinating how different they can be, and yet still be fine. My firstborn only spoke two words until he was almost two: More! and No! His first words after these two were adressed to me, as he dragged his fathers sledge hammer out of the laundry. Slowly, deliberately, he asked "Can I use Daddy's big hammer....please." From that day on he kept bringing me book after book, pointing and watching me speak, then repeating everything I said.

My 2nd babbled as if he made complete sense, and just gradually started adding real words to the flow.

Then my 3rd started talking before her first birthday, but in the chaos of a busy household I didn't really notice the significance. My sister is a speech pathologist, and nearly fell off her chair when my 12mth old said 'more please mum'. This same child didn't walk until she was 16mths. Coming down the hallway she said proudly 'look mum, emmy's walking'.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-21T12:57:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-the-kids-in-for-a-celiac-panel.635/post-17350>

We used Johannsen Sound Therapy for youngest, as she has dyslexic-related auditory processing problems - she didn't really start speaking until she was two, and then only a few words which she couldn't say properly. She ended up have fairly extensive speech therapy from age 5 - 6, in combination with the Sound Therapy and now has much fewer problems - I doubt she'll ever be 100%, especially as she is partially deaf in one ear. I must say, we were particularly worried when she didn't talk, as eldest started at around 11 months, and middle one around 10 months - both were making good sense by a year - so it was a bit of a shock when youngest was so slow to talk (mind you, she makes up for it now - a real blether-box!).

### challdo — 2012-04-04T00:50:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spectracell-results.636/post-11007>

I have been on the hCG thread because of doing another round but had done labs prior to this round and waited quite some time for my results.

When they came in today I called Spectracell to get help in the interpretation as it came back with a graph of averages.

I am 52 yrs old, female, and have done multiple hCG rounds for weight loss.

After running into this website, I started Leptin Rx instead of phase 3 in mid-Jan. and had a rock solid weight maintanence, i.e. no regain. Started CT immediately with baths in early Feb when Doc posted the first blog.

Spectracell test was drawn 3/9/12.

My average was 31-32 years old (I am 52!) on the telemere length. There is a 5 year +/- on this. Worst case senario I am at 37 yrs old.

I'm thrilled. Just the opposite of Dr Nancy Snyderman on the Today show. Testing older is a bummer but this is the way to lengthen telemeres, lengthen life and embrace wellness.

Cindy

jesse

### MTVASKO — 2012-04-04T02:20:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-11040>

Love the article, I understand peoples response, mainly because in this day no one really wants to "work" at understanding, they want it spoon feed to them like baby food, easily digested. You writing is MEAT, meat that has not be braised, you have to work your brain, thts ok with me, I have read your blogs over and over many times, I get the ideas, the flow , the intent, all the science, no, do I keep wikepedia up when reading , yes. At 60 yrs old I like to see the boundaries explored, test , tried. For me it has been Paleo 1 1/2 years, lost total of 50 pounds, Crossfit 2 times per week, mainly for the heavy lifting. ### Rhoswen — 2012-04-04T07:50:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-11176>

> **hazyjane;11239 wrote:** Why are you here? I ask that genuinely. You know that those of us here are all fans of Dr. Kruse, since this is his forum, so what gives?

I don't think you'll get an answer. I asked him why he came here to defend Paleohacks in another thread, and I didn't get a reply. I'm genuinely curious because I can't wrap my head around why it's worth it for this person to come here and defend, whatever they're trying to defend. It seems like a waste of time.

Which is why I tend to avoid reading what critics have to say because... Well a lot of the critics seem to be very angry and bitter. It stresses me out to read that kind of stuff. So instead of seeing what the critics had to say, I tried Dr. Kruse's way for myself. And since February 22nd I've gone from 155 pounds to 138. I don't feel tired, I get to eat delicious things that I enjoy making, and I don't have to do chronic cardio or starve myself. I NEVER feel super hungry anymore, even on days where I look at my food log and go, "Oh wow, did I really only eat 1400 calories?!"

I used to be STARVING on the old 1400 calorie CW diet. I don't binge on sugar or anything like that anymore.

I'm not even mentioning how nice my skin looks now. I used to get pretty bad cystic acne. What little acne I have seems to go away quickly with the application of tea tree/coconut oil.

But I guess my point is I didn't buy into this all at first myself... But I didn't see how following Dr. Kruse's advice could harm me in anyway. And it's working out great. I'm sure I'd be labeled as a blind follower though and that's okay. If it works for me, why question it? Perhaps I'm just a lazy thinker.

Edit: Oh I should mention... When I first started Paleo, I had intense cravings for sugar for weeks. Everyone said, "low carb flu" and I was cool with that. The intense cravings passed for several weeks... And returned FULL FORCE way worse than before. Only doing the Leptin Reset has helped.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-04T07:58:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-11181>

> **APow;11244 wrote:** Hazy Jane, Kruse posted those insults tonight, in the midst of this whole circus. He has talked that way in the past and continues to do so. He seems to respond to criticism--genuine, intelligent criticism--like an adolescent. Yes, clearly you are all fans of Jack. I guess I was hoping these forums would offer a somewhat more nuanced view of him and his ideas, or at least provide some evidence that he deserved all the attention he's getting. So far I'm unconvinced.

I went back to look and that's a bit misleading if you read the whole back and forth on his facebook page. He seems to have been bating Kamal... however, he made no comments whatsoever on Richard's blog where the paleo cage fight is going on, LOL.

He does like controversy and doesn't deny it. He can be rude. I'm not a fan of rudeness, myself (I stopped reading Dr. Harris a long time ago because of his own MD god-complex rudeness) but Jack has a heart. I've seen him help too many people to discount his information.

I see people who haven't truly read his work (including the comment sections where we usually have to make him clarify his vague points) building a lot of arguments based on total misconceptions. It can be frustrating to understand him- communication in writing is not his forte by a long shot.

I understand why he's a controversial figure, but some of the controversy is made up and based on non-issues and straw-man arguments. I don't see the Paleosphere reacting to Dr. Ray Peat and his fervent fans the same way as they guzzle their coke, orange juice, ice cream and skim milk with sugar - likely because he's reclusive and doesn't blog, but many of his ideas are certainly worthy of critique.

The fact is that a lot of people do not seem interested in dissecting (or understanding) Dr. Kruse's actual theories, but rather acting like high school kids.

You'll see people here defending him because he's helped them in profound ways that none of the other writers or docs have. I hope that's worth something in your consideration

I have had swollen ankles for 20 + years and since taking CT showers and not eating any potatoes, (biggest offender for swollen ankles it seems) strictly keto-paleo, my ankles have no swelling what so ever. I woke up four days ago and amazlingly they are a bit wrinkly from the fact there is NO swelling whatsoever. Short of amazing for someone that felt I would not be able to walk one day because of the pain. ### MamaGrok — 2012-04-04T16:31:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-11259>

> **David;11321 wrote:** I have long been aware that anything or anyone with actual knowledge that challenges any concept and idea that is essentially based on faith and not on deep understanding of underlying principles must be attacked by any means possible. Think Galileo or Copernicus. Could it be that underlying many of the very hostile blog posts is a nagging suspicion that many things the authors have accepted as articles of faith have no real basis in science or, for that matter, reality?

True, but (somewhat) in their defense is the fact that most of science is faith-based. Faith doesn't imply a god, but is anything that you take without proving it yourself because you find the source reliable. We take the moon landing on faith. We (unless we are scientists in these specific disciplines who have specifically undertaken to prove these points) take the heliocentric solar system on faith, and Hilbert spaces, and negative numbers, and dark matter, and neutrinos, and the quadratic formula and distance of the sun and relativity and quantum mechanics and electrons and evolution and even dinosaurs on faith.

The question comes down to, do we take them based on what we \*know\* to be true, and common sense, and gut sense, and how do we respond when dogma is challenged by new claimants to the title "fact"?

### Valorie33 — 2012-04-04T17:59:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-11308>

This whole thing is really making people think, not only on the internet but in real life. It has started discussion and disagreements in my home and family. My son in law works in medical research and he is not too thrilled about what Dr Kruse did with the MRSA however he is now doing the cold bath, today he asked my daughter to make him some bulletproof coffee. WIN

My boyfriend thought things were pretty good he was eating clean, and doing a bit of cool showers, and then he watched the video at Ted and he was not so happy, however he is now icing his back everyday for an old injury from 30 years ago. WIN

So whether people are hating or loving it is making change and what more could we ask for. The controversy seems harsh at times, who cares, it is getting the word out and I think that may have been half the reason he did it........BIG WIN

### Valorie33 — 2012-04-04T18:42:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-11333>

I keep thinking of a part of an old movie called What the Bleep Do We Know.

In the movie there is a scene where a guy sees some ripples in the water but has no idea what is causing it, he cannot see a thing(he has never seen a boat before). The Shaman comes to the shore and looks out studies the water and finally sees boats out there causing the ripples. Once he discovers this, then he tells his people and describes what it out there. Because they respect him as their Shaman they believe what he is saying and then they can also see the boats. (might not be exactly like the movie, but that was what I got out of it)

So people who have taken the time to know Dr Kruse can look and listen and finally see, those that haven't may never see...their loss.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T00:17:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-11496>

> **caroline;11256 wrote:** I am continually amazed that Dr. K. has time for all of this. Over the last number of years I have been lucky enough to have some personal friends that are wonderful Docs. They struggle to balance their lives. They are totally committed to their families and their patients and their community. Never enough hours in the day and always putting themselves out there for whoever needs help. I bow to them

This. So here is this guy who has an actual practice where he helps people. Hey, in addition to that he offers his time to countless questions (no matter how remedial) that come up. He manages to find the time to try and teach anyone ready about all of this "stuff". . .oh wait, for nothing, not even a tax write off for charity! AND during all this he hastily types thing up to just get them out to try and spread the word to those who want it. His personality is such he kind of just spews it out and hopes the ready person picks up the info. . . SO WHAT. If you are not wanting to read through and come up with an actual argument on why something would not work, then why the vitriol. If you are capable of disputing and "disproving", go for it. I'd love to see it. Does some times less than accurate hyperbole come out of Jack, welllll yup. It's a baby/bathwater situation.

So far, I've seen a case of an actual former fat ass correct and maintain himself. Oh wait, he's used his method for family members, too. AND I hear in in practice - proof to come in book.

SO, when the qualified scientists show me how their methods and tweaks have helped former fat asses lose that kind of weight and keep it off, I'll listen to them. So far Mark Sisson seems to have people he's genuinely helped, so I listened to him. For me it didn't get me past my lifetime of derangement, so I kept looking.

The best thing I ever did was quit taking advice on health from those who've not been there.

If someone can make a cogent argument on why something Jack is suggesting won't work, then I don't think Jack would mind. I think he genuinely tried to disprove. Is he 100% correct on the science and why's? I have NFI, I forgot to finish the multiple-discipline PHD's required to understand it all. I'm ok with errors on the details, if the actions are a working for people.

For gods sake it's annoying enough waiting to find out about Factor X, if I also had to wait while he did peer reviewed studies and hired technical writers to proof read anything - It would be years from now before we were hearing about this at all. I'll take it NOW with flaws, Thank You.

I have a very hard time making mistakes, but recognize, if one isn't making a LOT of mistakes, they are not making much progress either. I am willing to give a little cold a try, and find out if it is a mistake for moi.

### AKMan — 2012-04-06T23:34:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-12277>

Back to the topic of the Free the Animal blog piece. There was an interesting exchange that probably got lost on everyone because of the strange threading on that blog, but Ray Cronise, you know the NASA guy, chimed in. I will cut and paste the entire conversation so you don't have to hunt. I think Ray seems like the real-deal kinda guy and I hope he does well. His version of CT is a lot easier to take than Jack's! Before you bash him for being vegan, I think his vegan-ism is like Dr. K's MRSA-injectionism, just done for N=1, not dogma. Here it is:

**John // Apr 4, 2012 at 15:24 wrote:

Awesome. I would often go back and forth on my opinion of Jack Kruse while reading some of his posts, from â€œThis guy is a genius!â€ to â€œWhat the fuck is he talking about? Is he just making shit up and throwing some fancy words around?â€ Sometimes, I would have those some reactions in the same paragraph. Hell, maybe the same sentence.

But I am certainly interested in the Cold Termogenesis stuff. I have Wim Hoffâ€™s â€œBecoming the Icemanâ€ and was really into Ray Croniseâ€™s adventures in using cold temperatures to help with fat loss. He had instant credibility to me as thermodynamic engineer at NASA. Unfortunately, he lost me when he started promoting a vegan diet on his blog.

But I think the point is still valid. Temperature could be as important to body composition (and maybe overall health) as diet and exercise. Itâ€™s amusing to think of all the nutrition experts that have quoted the first law of thermodynamics, and then completely overlook heat, which is what thermodynamics is all about.

Reply Ray Cronise // Apr 6, 2012 at 06:58** wrote:

Sorry to have sent you astray. Aristotle said, â€œIt is the mark of an educated mind to be able to entertain a thought without accepting it.â€

I think I have been quite clear to state that I am not a vegan, but have been eating a vegan diet. There actually is a pretty sound reason for the self-experiment I am doing. Iâ€™m being scientifically pragmatic, not ideological â€“ on either side. There are some very interesting things that happen biologically during caloric restriction/nutrient density.

What I am surprised about is that both sides â€“ meat eaters and plant munchers get wrapped around the axel on ideology. I donâ€™t really care one way or the other. Iâ€™ve eaten meat sparingly as part of a very well thought out experiment. Itâ€™s results have been extremely postive and consistent. Weâ€™re omnivores, but there are consequences of caloric density â€“ meat or no meat.

Perhaps the most important take home is to continue challenge status quo â€“ popular or not.

When we were trying commercialize private space flight in the 90s, one of the biggest obstacles was my employer, NASA. I left after 15 years. Iâ€™m glad we went against popular wisdom, because 20 years later companies I started have sent 7 to space station (one twice) and over 15,000 into weightlessness on our parabolic flights (gozerog.com). The most INCREDIBLE three passengers \[insert bias\]: â€“ my three kids at 8, 10, and 12 got to experience what I had worked years to achieve 20 years earlier â€œin the system.â€ Most importantly the X-Prize launched a huge race for space and now Elon Musk, the paypal guy, is the best hope for those same people still at NASA that havenâ€™t built a new launch vehicle in decadesâ€¦ Steve jobs talks about connecting the dots looking backwards, I think there is wisdom here.

Whatâ€™s do vegans and astronauts have to do with cold stress and diet? Itâ€™s a simple lesson. Itâ€™s about challenging our core beliefs and trying to ease into what is true. I know now for sure now that the â€œprotein, carbohydrate, and fatâ€ fuel myth is bunk. Iâ€™m certain that â€œfoodâ€ is more important. I believe that there is a downside to excessive consumed â€œfuelâ€ even when it does not contribute significantly to body weight.

As for mild cold stress, I continue to gather and analyze data. Wim Hof and I have made some great strides. I hope to bring it all forward in time. Like Richard (the little I have read) I would rather be right than popular, but Iâ€™m certain I only learn when wrong. In the mean time, there is just as much fluff on the paleo side as the vegan. Iâ€™m not a middle man kind of guy â€“ I want the truth. Sometimes we have to challenge the â€œbest thing goingâ€ and not â€œvote for the lessor of two evils.â€ That may require stretching the box, not getting out of it.

I now know a fundamental truth about food â€“ macronutrients and micronutrients and I am grateful to have had TIm Ferrissâ€™ help in popularizing some old concepts and rejuvenating the investigation. Jack and I talk too as he will tell you and there is plenty of work to do ahead. Whatâ€™s interesting is sometimes disagreements are more like rectangle/square debates: all squares are rectangles, but not all rectangles are squares. It often can lead us to conclusive affirmation, only to find we donâ€™t have it exactly correct.

I invite you to give it a little more time and rest assured I am not some sort of plant (pun intended) for the vegan ideology. I do have respect for many scientist on that side of the argument, but I am sometimes gored there, because I donâ€™t share the ideology. Weâ€™re learning at an exponential rate (google my Zero-G biz partner, peter diamandis and abundance and watch his TEDtalk).

I will quote your last two sentences, because in them is wisdom:

â€ Temperature could be as important to body composition (and maybe overall health) as diet and exercise. Itâ€™s amusing to think of all the nutrition experts that have quoted the first law of thermodynamics, and then completely overlook heat, which is what thermodynamics is all about.â€

we completely agree.

Ray

Reply Richard Nikoley // Apr 6, 2012 at 08:33

Ray:

Thanks for stopping by.

â€œIâ€™m certain that â€œfoodâ€ is more important.â€

and

â€œmacronutrients and micronutrientsâ€

These two pasts posts might interest you, the first compares the micronutrient density of liver to fruits and the second live and salmon to bread:

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-09T01:40:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-12822>

For those no longer following the debacle, here's a great post by Ray Cronise that all should read:

> Author: Ray Cronise
>
> Comment:
>
> I think that it is not something to be done "for the fun of it" and so I understand completely 1) the concern and 2) the seriousness. Unfortunately there are two kinds of people in the world that fuel her concern: adrenaline junkies and lawyers.
>
>
>
> One of the positive benefits of N=1 experiments is that we are able to dance around OVER-burdeneed procedure where "failure is not an option." If I may return to my worn out space exploration analogies - NASA is becoming irrelevant because they've stopped taking calculated risks. Statistics say how often, not when, so a risk is just that - a hedged unknown.
>
>
>
> Reckless is something completely different. There are many things I (and wim) know about mild cold stress that we haven't spoken of, because our goal is not to share with people everything we know, but to help guide them into the positive benefits. We don't want ANYONE hurt and one can get hurt, so you must think carefully about what you ask people to do.
>
>
>
> This is even MORE important when dealing with obesity and sickness, because these often fall on the ears of people desperate that might try anything.
>
>
>
> The criticism of N=1 is that it's "not a large enough group" and "not peer-reviewed." That is really a strawman argument. We are now doing some very sophisticated aggregation of N=1 experiments thanks to what is going on right here in this venue - internet dialog and exchange.
>
>
>
> What is exciting is that at least The 4 Hour Body and great news coverage has popularized the idea. This is not a time of "discovery" or invention, but a time of exploration and results. For well over 2 decades, Wim has been a lone ranger out there and I feel honored to take it up one more notch. As well, Tim has been working on the ideas for many years and only covered a subset in his book of his (or my) real trials - a good story is mostly about saying enough and not too much.
>
>
>
> I don't feel in a rush to get all of this out "before someone beats me to it," because the goal is transformation and health, not popularity and credit. History has a way of sorting the other stuff out.
>
>
>
> I appreciate the discussion and hope that my contributing here will diffuse the negative energy and get us on track to real change that can come from this.
>
>
>
> Ray

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-09T01:41:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-12824>

And:

> General Guidelines for exposure:
>
>
>
> Water temperature less than 60F/15.5C and air temperature less than 32F/0C are great lines of WARNING. in temperatures lower than this there is a chance of hypothermia. Walking hypothermia can be very serious (google it) and so it stands to reason when you go below these thresholds it’s 1) at your own risk and 2) should be done with caution.
>
>
>
> That being said, cardiac response is rare, but happens with sudden cold shock. The best source I have for those of you that want to evaluate the overall depth of physiological responses is:
>
>
>
> [http://bit.ly/HAS8c0](http://http://bit.ly/HAS8c0) (Journal of Applied Physiology).
>
>
>
> I will add that this article is not dealing necessarily with those trained, but everyone should recognize a lot of similar information surrounding the subject and see that it’s pretty well documented. There are literally 3 binders on my desk of papers (I haven’t learned to keep them on my iPad yet and have to print to really study).
>
>
>
> So, Adam is correct in that there are no bright lines, only “guidelines.” What Richard and others have described here is also true – it’s an endorphin rush and absolutely is oddly addictive (I hated to be cold when I started). For the record, Wim gets cold, wears coats, and feels the initial pain. He can push past it better than anyone I’ve seen, but he isn’t a genetic freak as everyone dismissed him as for years.
>
>
>
> I think the method is to ease into this and the others here that are pressing how serious this can be, please heed that warning. I think everyone can learn to do it and it will have a measurable, positive impact on metabolism and overall immunity, but I also think we need not be reckless.
>
>
>
> While it seems obvious to everyone that the wrong advice to someone wanting to run a marathon is: buy some shoes and get in the starting place – just do it. That would be a miserable and dangerous proposition (See: Cardiac Arrest during Long-Distance Running Races, Kim, teal., n engl j med 366;2 january 12, 2012) considering .5 in 100,000 have cardiac arrest even when trained. Fortunately it takes hours to hit the wall running and blisters/dehydration are a bit more obvious and less serious than hypothermia.
>
>
>
> I think in water/air above those thresholds you are free to play within reason and incidentally, we are finding all the repeatable, documented positive benefits seem to be equally obtained in mild vs extreme exposure. Below that, think about what you are doing.
>
>
>
> Ray

Richard is going to do another blog on his own experiences with CT, and is inviting Ray to give some initial input, and then to comment extensively, so keep watching FTA if you're interested.

### AKMan — 2012-04-09T07:41:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-any-of-you-like-to-chime-in-here.638/post-12921>

> **Lyndra;12979 wrote:** MamaGrok - thanks for the updates - the thread was getting too much for me to follow. I appreciate seeing what Ray has to say.

I second that, and when I went through that thread again, I found more Cronise nuggets. We should consolidate these to a new thread. From the last posting, it looks like they are starting a new blog on Free the Animal with some actual constructive talk and not just Jack-bashing.

"You are correct. The generic number I came up with about three years ago when working with Tim on The 4 Hour body is that the energy to melt, and raise to body temperature, 66 lbs of ice = 1 lb of fat (3500 Kcals).

later I did a one dimensional solution to Founier’s law and then a more sophisticated model using mathematic that took into account skin thickness, adipose tissue, and muscle.

It turns out that sitting in 27C water (80F), which is where mild cold stress begins, burns about 2.4x RMR or for the average 100 watt human, or about 240 watts. This was compared to actual measurements of metabolism that range about 230-270W, so I considered it solved. (btw, using watts here as that takes into account time so that 1 watt is .86 kcal/hour ).

Air gets MUCH more complex as does the overall metabolic response to extended time with mild cold stress.

Because of vasoconstriction and vasodilation (happens in SECONDS not minutes), the body is VERY good at conserving heat. This is why Tim and I took slightly different routes. I was using overall chronic mild cold stress and Tim was trying to engage and recruit BAT. I think that the bigger picture is that overall we see fundamental metabolic changes as a response to cold stress.

Total time in ice baths is not the key. Cold stress activates the basic genetic protective pathways, just like the biological stress of lifting weights causes muscle to grow in response.

Wim has seen and documented (in a hospital as part of a controlled study) muted response to direct endotoxin injection. It’s the ability to override the immune system and control cortisol that seems to be key. He also had a PET scan recently in Holland and we measured BAT and total heat output. He produces about 4.5 times the heat of the average 20 year old male in the study.

Hope that helps…

Ray

AND:

Endotoxin is simply a “fragment” of a virus that essentially mounts the same immune response, but does not cause disease. It is the part of the virus, the protein coat, that your immune system seeks and destroys. As such, it’s safe to do (no chance of getting the disease), but your body still mounts a defense.

Injections are like going from zero to two days sick in minutes. Normally one gets severe headache, fever, cramps, etc… Wim was able to mentally boost his cortisol level after the beginning of the headache and completely shunt the immune response.

For the record, this went through committee, was done in a control setting and there was plenty of data taken.

Some papers are in progress now. a case study can be found here:

[http://bit.ly/HmdWIN](http://http://bit.ly/HmdWIN)

hope that helps. There are many very solid projects out there on all of this. It didn’t start this year. When I began research in 2008, I found dozens of papers. A lot of the mild cold stress work was done in the 50s and 60s to support caloric needs for hostile military environments like remote outposts, high altitude aircraft, and water landings. >

The first patents on cold stress and weight loss are in the mid to late 80s. It’s not new, but it was new to me at the time I used it to lose weight. Once I started digging into the science I was pleased to find out that there was even more work being done on the genetic pathways of thermal regulation. As I discuss on my blog these are all related to the sirtuins and up regulation of PGC1-α.

We are way beyond this being new. What hasn’t been done widespread is simply deployment. I’ve been doing various things over the last 2 years to make it much more “enjoyable” (LOL) for the masses. It turns out you don’t have to do extreme to get the benefits and that is what wim and I are teaching.

Ray

AND:

I don’t want to spoil the effort of some very hard-working researchers in The Netherlands, so suffice it to say, they measured BAT and most importantly total heat output. I separate the two, because as I have covered in early posts on my site, BAT is only a high-density of mitochondria; there are mitochondrion in every living cell – it’s the power plant for ATP as you probably know.

What happens there in terms of substrate utilization and production of ATP is a symphony of reactions. There are over 1000 identified genes encoding mitochondrial proteins – recently 240 listed that have association with disease in Humans when mutated.

This is complex, despite what anyone might try to say. I don’t think anyone “understands” exactly what is going on – I am certain wim and I don’t.

With that said, you don’t have to know how the watch works to tell time and Wim has definitely cracked through some key elements that have repeatable, demonstrated, and documented performance. I have done similar things in my own work.

At this time I have been working with some of the nations best physicians, anthropologists, diet “gurus,” biochemists and biologists to attempt to get us all paddling in a similar direction.

For example, many critique my TEDMED talk at being vague, and lacking detail. The reason it is structured the way it is had to do more with what they wanted – a simple story that launched interest in the subject – not a PhD dissertation defense. TEDMED and TED are the only two true TED events. TEDx events can be put on by anyone and anyone can attend – just pay the fee to the organization. Great talks still come out of them, so I am not casting dispersion, but people are not quite as limited in the talk.

My goal was to get national attention on the balance between diet/exercise/thermal load – to quote another comment here who hit the nail squarely on the head:

” Temperature could be as important to body composition (and maybe overall health) as diet and exercise. It’s amusing to think of all the nutrition experts that have quoted the first law of thermodynamics, and then completely overlook heat, which is what thermodynamics is all about.” (john)

What I wanted as a goal is to attract media and other scientist’s attention to this obvious connection. It’s been phenomenal and there is a LOT of work happening worldwide on the subject. I realize that this Paleo-conteversary seems like it is exploding, but truth be told, there is a much quieter revolution happening all over. I get media requests and emails/calls now far more frequently and it has even helped some of the earlier researchers in Europe get much-diserved attention.

so, I can say that even BREATHING correctly has impact on HDL/LDL, there are diet impacts and mild cold stress effects. It’s not a simple BAT/Mitochondrion issue. It’s not a simple hormone issue. I am NOT saying that many people have not “learned to tell time,” but rather than the fundamental problem is deeper.

I am grateful to Richard for allowing me to comment extensively here (I asked/introduced first) but I want to stick with the constructive challenge that moves science and people forward. There is so much we have left to learn – so much we don’t know. I think it’s a sign of strength to say I don’t know when you don’t.

I thought I would take the time to stomp a few misconceptions here, so that we don’t throw the baby out with the (ice cold) bathwater

As time permits, I’ll comment more on 1) length of exposure and adaptation. It turns out that fat in humans is not there as an insulator and some very sound science has been done on this. It is an energy store and has little effect on survival (it’s not seal blubber).

If you take a look at “The New Eye On Bat” I cover some of the exercise-induced work that was done very recently. Also look at Ch-CH-Changes and The Heat is On. There are a few more there that cover adaptation and insulation…

hope that helps and thanks to Richard for allowing me to post here. I hope everyone will redirect some energy into how to make this a positive addition to what you’re already doing.

Ray

Reply Ray Cronise // Apr 8, 2012 at 10:56

ps…the 2) should be adaptation.

Reply Richard Nikoley // Apr 8, 2012 at 11:05

Ray:

Thanks a lot. Great stuff and I think a lot of us are interested in knowing more.

I plan to put up a new post on Monday (tomorrow) with a few of my own questions and observations, primarily revolving around CT. It might be best to withold additional input until then, if only because at 850 or so comments, it’s getting more than a bit cumbersome at this point.

If you’d like to email me with some suggestions as to what questions to ask and issues to raise for that new post I will be delighted to include it so that as you say, we can move this forward as constructively as possible.

Thanks again.

### Croak — 2012-04-09T07:43:01+0300

> **Lyndra;12979 wrote:** MamaGrok - thanks for the updates - the thread was getting too much for me to follow. I appreciate seeing what Ray has to say.

Just keep in mind what Cronise is trying to do is markedly different than what Kruse is. Totally different rabbit holes. Seems to me that Cronise is all about losing weight (and getting a cut from 4-Hour Body sales?), Kruse is all about longevity, with weight loss just being a side-effect of optimal.

So don't take Cronise's tips on how to CT as gospel, keep in mind that he's coming at it from a different angle than Kruse...and frankly, the man takes lukewarm baths compared to what some of us are doing.
