**

Thank you all for your support, **I'm gonna go for it. My quality of life is so low now and I'm in so much pain, it can't get much worse and there is truly little at risk.

I have a couple of things to bring up.....

First, I take low dose aspirin, fish oil and flax supplement. The supplements are for O6 to O3 ratio fats, which I've researched pretty thoroughly already. I noticed Dr. J wasn't thrilled with flax supplements, but the ratio of 06 to 03 is far better in flax than in any other type of oil by far-so I'm slightly at a loss. I know flax tastes bad, but for those who have to force oil by the spoonful to do the BAB, I would think a brief bad taste would be a minor issue since flax has so much more O3.

The info about ox bile is good info for me. If the gallbladder is the mechanism to break down fat, then a missing gallbladder seems pretty significant (medically speaking). Glad to know about the alternative and that the missing gallbladder isn't a deal breaker.

With regard to the gallbladder failure mode.....is it possible that the gallbladder originally became dysfunctional due to the interference from blood glucose? I wonder, if I had been on a Paleo type diet years ago, is it more likely that my gallbladder might have not failed at all?**

Should I begin using GB Digestion from Pure Encapsulations now rather than beginning to use it later??? Or, should I wait until the LR is completed?

**

Despite my really marginal BAB this am, I did notice I wasn't impossibly hungry by lunchtime-which is a vast improvement. I should also note that I've already been fructose free gluten free for the past week-4 days before I found LR and Paleo (I was able to figure out these were no good and just stopped them even though I didn't have diet plan/strategy yet).

I am taking insulin now (started March 15), and I'm in the process of slowly ramping up the dose. I intend to test BG often since the LR process might radically and abruptly make my BG lower.

Again, ty to you all. I'll keep you posted. I can't wait to convert from a sugar burner to a fat burner!!!

Regards,

Art

### Jim — 2012-04-05T05:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11622>

Art,

Good on ya for cutting out the gluten and fructose. Assuming your pancreas is still working correctly then you should be able to digest fat. The problem is that it only produces digestive enzymes at a certain somewhat continuous rate. Your gallbladders function was to store these enzymes for when you eat a large meal. Think your big breakfast. Consuming all that fat at one meal could quickly overwhelm your pancreas. You therefore might have a problem digesting the fat in a large meal. The digestive supplement should help prevent any issues.

I would dump the flax and go with coconut oil. It's pretty much a wonder fat. I eat it right out of the jar. You can use olive oil to make salad dressings etc.

Ideally you will not snack at all. However while you are transitioning, if you get an uncontrollable urge either eat some protein or a spoonful of coconut oil. This will keep your carbs low and tamp down the hunger until the next meal.

Hang in there.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-05T06:03:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11626>

Welcome, Art. It can be really overwhelming starting all this - all the info, the science, the worries. But bottom line: eat your BAB (and make sure it has at least 50g of protein (ideally, meat and eggs, not chicken) and lots of fat. Coconut oil will serve you really well. It burns fat, it satiates hunger and it's great for you. Do NOT worry about eating too much. When you're trying to lose weight and cravings we are often so used to trying to stop ourselves from eating and holding back that it's engrained (at least it was for me). The freedom in this is that you get to health (and weight loss) by not worrying. Just eat the good foods - clean diet as has been suggested - and don't snack if you can help it.

Hang in there.

### gemstar — 2012-04-05T07:19:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11635>

Hey Art,

Welcome to the first days of the rest of your healthier life. I've been playing with low carb for many many years. First experience was our old buddie Dr. Atkins. I even did the CALP (carb addicts life plan) for a while a number of years ago. They all worked wonderfully, but what I didn't know then was how important it is to eat enough FATS in order to not get hungry. So at one point my yearning for carbs became larger than my willpower (duh!).

I live in a country where grass fed animal products are not available. Our green pastures are only green for a few months a year. Agribusiness has taken over here in Israel on many levels. Fortunately our produce mentality is based in locally and seasonally grown items. But I still eat low carb high fat and can only brag about our own home grown olive oil and the wonderful date syrup we can sub for your maple syrup.

Eating low carb high fat is better than a diet based in grains and legumes, even if it's not organic and grass-fed. I also have lost quite a bit weight without all the exercising so many people seem to do. So you even don't need to do anything like that to start losing the weight.

BTW, do make sure that the coconut oil you buy is cold pressed only and not refined in any way. It seems that in some countries that produce and export CO, there is a demand for them to refine the CO so that it stays hard even in high temps. I found ORGANIC CO in our HFS and it stayed hard during our very hot summers, this led me to learn about the oils. Cold pressed CO will have the distinct aroma of coconut. Any other kind will not!! Same goes for palm oil. I have yet to find cold pressed non damaged palm oil here in Israel, and I have a feeling it's not imported.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-05T20:23:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11812>

TY all.

I have a brief update, with some news that is not great.

First, the update. This is day 2 of the LR.

I had a sub level protein BAB yesterday (Day 1 of the LR) and a modest plate of raw green veggies for dinner. I did have some nuts, brazil and cashews. During the day yesterday, I didn't get horribly hungry until well past noon. I did not sleep well the night before due to stress, so I went to bed earlier than I should have and I simply had to take a 1 hour nap in the afternoon-I set my alarm so that I wouldn't sleep longer than 1 hour.

Today, I woke up at 5, but didn't get out of bed until 8:30-I just felt horrible in general and couldn't get myself to move.

I made the BAB, this time with 2 large slices of ham. Here's where the bad news comes...... After I made and ate the BAB, I looked at the package the ham came in. One of the ingredients was 'dextrose', which I know is a sugar! I was so bummed and I thought I had been sugar free these last few days!!! Clearly it's not the end of the world, but it is definitely a setback and a newbie mistake. SO, I learned something else....ALWAYS LOOK AT THE LABEL, without exception.

I pulled a frozen turkey out and began thawing it in the fridge shortly after breakfast. I looked at the label this time. It said there was 'sugar' in it!!! I was so damned disgusted. There's no need to add sugar to a whole turkey!!!!

I did read the leaky gut and the gaps diet FAQ-wow!!! It sure seems to me that all this crap the food vendors add to the food has created many of the medical issues we have today!!! Maybe all/most of the ailments and medical issues are man-made by the food packaging industry.

Saturday, my wife and I plan to drive 50 miles to a food coop known for organic and homegrown foods. We have a local Amish clan nearby that supplies some of the food there, and they are known to be wholesome. Hopefully I can buy meat there that isn't raised on grain.

I have a question about butter. I keep seeing 'pastured' butter mentioned in the forum. Here in Maine, we have many dairy producers. We can buy pure butter in the supermarket and it is all local, and it's all raised with hay for feed (no grains). It's my understanding that I need to buy only pastured and clarified butter. Is the locally produced butter the type I need, or do I need to seek out other sources of 'specialty' butter?

Again, TY to u all.

Art

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-05T20:54:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11827>

Hi Art! The butter many of us on the forum use is Kerrygold brand butter. It's carried in Whole Foods or Trader Joes if you have either one. If you know of a small local dairy that pastures the cows during the summer months and feeds them hay during winter it's probably OK. You really don't want the grain or anything from cows that have been fed hormones (to keep the milk coming) or antibiotics.

Also I don't think anyone mentioned that you can, if you need to, eat up to 75 gm of protein in the morning for your BAB.

I buy most of my meat online. Check eatwild.com for sources/ranches near you. I buy from a producer in the northern part of the state and bits and pieces from uswellness meats like liverwurst...YUM! I have a slice every morning with b/fast. Also be sure to make sure that the beef is 100% grass fed and not grass fed and gran finished.

We're glad to see you here and welcome! My best advice is to enjoy this and have fun! It's such an easy way to eat and live.

### ealachan — 2012-04-05T20:59:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11832>

Art, if you're in Maine you might have Hannaford grocery stores near you? We have those down here in Vermont, and they carry the Kerrygold butter that everyone here is so fond of. I was quite happy to find that out.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-05T21:14:35+0300

### nonchalant — 2012-04-05T21:52:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11848>

Welcome Art!

Locally produced butter from cows fed hay is good. When the cows start eating Spring grass, that is the best butter.

Around here in Texas, my local dairy feeds them grass year around, but gives them grains while they're being milked. So just a small amount of grains.

I had to work myself up to the BAB. Took me a while to be able to digest all the fat and protein.

So glad you and your family are working towards optimal!

\-Sally

# Thread: Heat Intolerance
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-04T23:03:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11455>

I know it has been said several times that once we become cold adapted our bodies can still adapt to heat as well and maintain a normal temp. However, I'm finding the opposite to be happening and I can't even say that I'm fully cold adapted yet either.

This doesn't surprise me at all given that my body tends to always respond to things differently . . . apparently I'm a bit of a complex case!

I'm really struggling when I'm in warm environments. My office is very warm and stuffy and I assumed it was more the stuffy air that was getting to me. When I'm in there my cheeks get flushed and super hot. I start to feel extremely weak all over and sometimes even get nauseous. This week I'm staying with a friend in South Carolina and we're having beautiful weather. I'm from New Jersey, so this 80 degree weather is new for me right now! I've been trying to get outside and soak up some vitamin D, but the same thing is happening to me that happens in my office . . . nausea, weakness, fatigue.

Any ideas why my body is reacting this way?

### nonchalant — 2012-04-04T23:16:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11463>

Hi BK, how long have you been CTing? I wonder if it might be toxins. I'm finding the opposite--others complain about the 80F+ heat before I notice it.

### Guest — 2012-04-04T23:20:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11464>

There is a guy in my building that is cold adapted and he said heat does not bother him. He wore nothing but shorts and a t shirt outside all winter long. I am thinking that the healtheir we get the better we are able to tolerate all temps.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-04T23:30:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11471>

> **Nonchalant;11532 wrote:** Hi BK, how long have you been CTing? I wonder if it might be toxins. I'm finding the opposite--others complain about the 80F+ heat before I notice it.

I've been CTing for 6 weeks total - 1 week of face dunks, 2 weeks of ice pack spotting and 3 weeks of baths.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-04T23:32:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11472>

Kevin Cottrell posted some remarkable things on FB that I would tell you to ask him about......he is the leader of Paleo fx. Friend him.

> **Jack Kruse;11541 wrote:** Kevin Cottrell posted some remarkable things on FB that I would tell you to ask him about......he is the leader of Paleo fx. Friend him.

Just sent him a friend request. Thanks Dr. K!

### Adriana — 2012-04-05T15:23:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11691>

The health of your liver also influences your ability to tolerate heat, as I found out after I got Hepatitis A. You might consider taking milk thistle supplements.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-05T15:51:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11704>

Hormonal "hot flashes" from detox or hormone re-arrangement?

I'm loving starting the day on my deck while it's still sub-60 and spending all day out there homeschooling and eating lunch till it's 80+, then leaving the door open and returning to the deck for dinner as the sun sets and we return to the 60s. All in tank top & shorts.

### Sherry — 2012-04-05T16:33:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11716>

> **BJK77;11524 wrote:** I know it has been said several times that once we become cold adapted our bodies can still adapt to heat as well and maintain a normal temp. However, I'm finding the opposite to be happening and I can't even say that I'm fully cold adapted yet either.

When I'm in there my cheeks get flushed and super hot. I start to feel extremely weak all over and sometimes even get nauseous. This week I'm staying with a friend in South Carolina and we're having beautiful weather. I'm from New Jersey, so this 80 degree weather is new for me right now! I've been trying to get outside and soak up some vitamin D, but the same thing is happening to me that happens in my office . . . nausea, weakness, fatigue.
>
>

> Any ideas why my body is reacting this way?

This is exactly what has happened to me over the last few years. And I live in the Florida Keys so summers were most of the year with the high humidity. I started escaping to the high elevations of Colorado for the worst of the hurricane season. What I found there is that I could better tolerate the heat because the humidity level is so low. However, I've been doing the leptin Rx for over 3 months and CT for two months and I'm finding it so much easier to tolerate the heat. It's not perfect yet. I still have bouts of intense uncomfortableness with the heat but they are diminishing in intensity, length of time and frequency. I haven't changed anything else so it is definitely the Kruse protocol. I'm sure you'll start noticing an improvement.

### Birdy — 2012-04-07T05:13:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-12368>

I had been sleeping and waiting in the morning for things to warm up before going outside. Now I do the opposite. I do outdoor things early in the day to get the cold. I have found that after a few weeks, I am not too hot when it is hot. I think it also might be that I don't wear as many clothes, and am usually in a constant state of just a little bit of cold, so I have some upwards flexibility if I need it.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-05-04T16:35:05+0300

# Thread: Ice/Cold post exercise - Blunts Adaptations
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 49
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Barry — 2012-04-04T23:43:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11483>

I've been looking at cold water/contrast baths/cryotherapy for recovery post exercise for a while now.

If you look at all the studies, and there are plenty of meta-analysis to go by, the conclusions are as follows:

\- Ice baths do numb the pain

\- But they blunt adaptations

Basically, ice/cold therapy inhibits the acute inflammation. This prevents the body from adapting to the stress. This is not what you want to happen. You want to adapt so that after subsequent training sessions you don't cause as much damage and can thus train harder.

So the take home message for me has been:

\- only use ice baths during competition or after extreme exercise

\- but the rest of time, let the body adapt while you train to improve adaptations

So I'm not sure why Dr K is recommending "icing" after all exercise ?

... question everything ;-)

### Glamorama — 2012-04-05T05:04:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11599>

I exercise and do my LHT hours prior to CT. So gym at 10 am, CT at 5 pm, the walk around 6 pm. So far I've had nothing but gains in the gym.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T15:42:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11700>

I train 3 x week from 4-5:30, dinner then CT after, for an hour. CT on days off from training as well. My technique coach finally picked up a calculating error I put into my program. For six weeks I have been doing a MENS program, forgot to adjust for women, I have been doing 5# jumps on worksets of sq and dl, 2.5 on bench, should have been 2.5/1.25 lbs. ha ha, oops. Also I am "masters" so I should have factored in a lighter recovery day, and I didn't. I still feel like I could keep making these jumps too. My powerlifting meet is in less than two weeks, I have put on 12.5# on my single squat which is significant gain in 6 weeks, from 220 to 232.5

I don't feel my adaptation is "blunted". I would have stalled in the 3rd week. AFter the meet, I have a la few months, won't be interrupted with tapering for/recovery from a meet. I should be able to see much longer linear progression. Yay

### hellojtm — 2012-04-05T21:28:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11842>

> **Barry;11552 wrote:** So the take home message for me has been:
>
>
>
> \- only use ice baths during competition or after extreme exercise
>
> \- but the rest of time, let the body adapt while you train to improve adaptations
>
>
>
> So I'm not sure why Dr K is recommending "icing" after all exercise ?
>
>
>
> ... question everything ;-)

Good post Barry. I would like to hear Dr. Kruse's thoughts on it as well. The acute inflammation from exercise is valuable as long as your chronic inflammation is low/non-existent. So I always wonder about the context of the advice given. Is icing after all exercise optimal for someone with low inflammation and a good acute inflammation response? Seems like it is not. But, then again, I don't think we can control for the diet and lifestyle of all the people in your meta-analysys, and most likely they had some level of chronic inflammation as the norm (it's normal), so perhaps it isn't even of benefit then!

jesse

### Barry — 2012-04-06T01:32:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11980>

> **hellojtm;11911 wrote:** Good post Barry. I would like to hear Dr. Kruse's thoughts on it as well. The acute inflammation from exercise is valuable as long as your chronic inflammation is low/non-existent. So I always wonder about the context of the advice given. Is icing after all exercise optimal for someone with low inflammation and a good acute inflammation response? Seems like it is not. But, then again, I don't think we can control for the diet and lifestyle of all the people in your meta-analysys, and most likely they had some level of chronic inflammation as the norm (it's normal), so perhaps it isn't even of benefit then!
>
>
>
> jesse

Jesse

most studies never control "everything". However, the general consensus is that constant ice treatment post exercise inhibits the acute inflammation signalling that is beneficial to adaptations. Thats what the science says and its pretty well documented at this stage.

There is obviously some timing considerations to take into account i.e. immediate icing is counter productive, but perhaps a time delay between cessation of training and icing is beneficial, like what Glamaroma and Teenie Leek do above.

Don't get me wrong, I'm not saying CT doesn't work in the Leptin Signalling context etc. I'm purely talking about the athlete, who trains regularly and uses ice baths after every session for recovery. The evidence shows that this is wrong and its pretty conclusive and makes sense.

Performance Enhancement = exercise + acute stress + acute inflammation + adaptation x repeat.

NOT

exercise + acute stress + anti/inhibitory inflamation = poor adaptation

### SimonM — 2012-04-06T10:10:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12071>

I'd like to see the references you are looking at, as I find it very hard to believe that ice baths a) prevent all adaptation and that b) anyone can possibly prove that long term.

By adaptation you are talking about everything from improvements in V02 max, lactate tolerance, muscle enzymes, quantity and quality of mitochondria, new capillarisation, cardiac muscle changes leading to improved stroke volume and on and on and on... so, really? Ice baths stop all that? You're laying a lot of adaptation at the feet of acute inflammation.

Then I want to see a study or two that quantifies and balances the loss of "adaptation" when set against the benefits of faster recovery and the ability to train more and/or harder than would be the case without ice baths. Many of the elites I know use ice baths to the legs to enable them to train twice a day.

Also bear in mind that, for athletes, adaptation sometimes takes a back seat to recovery: think Tour de France cyclists with another stage in the morning', or a track and field athlete (especially a masters' athlete) with championship heats and finals giving him or her five races in seven days to get through. In those situations ice baths are performance enhancing.

### eva — 2012-04-06T11:52:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12077>

plus.. is it the case that these studies are done on warmadapted people. so maybe its not the case for coldadapted. plus its a choice - what is more important - sacrificing a bit of proteinsynthesis - gain a lot of HGH and testo. i know my choice. and once you are oldadapted - maybe the effect will be different alltogether. i understand that inflammation is concidered necessary for muscle growth - but is it? im not so certain anymore...

### eva — 2012-04-06T11:54:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12078>

the C-s on my keypad are stuck  sorry for typos

>
>
> I think that's because the science is nowhere near as conclusive as Barry is suggesting and that the "science" (such as it is) directly conflicts with real-life experience. I know it is a pain, but you really do have to experiment with your boys and see what works for them long-term. I say long-term because the overwhelmingly majority of sports "science" experiments are short-term. There is also currently no placebo control for an ice bath known to man
>
>
>
> That said, a cold shower after training is NOT going to interfere with inflammation and/or adaptation, so you are perfectly safe recommending that! And you can also get them to follow the protocol Glamorama and Teenie Leek are using - they are both seeing improved performance with cold treatments - timed away from training sessions.
>
>
>
> Dr Alex Hutchinson, who writes the Sweat Science blog now a permanent feature on runnersworld.com, [reviewed the "ice bath" research at the end of 2011](http://http://sweatscience.com/ice-baths-lab-fatigue-vs-real-fatigue/) in the wake of what he described as a "a major meta-analysis in the British Journal of Sports Medicine that adds a couple of interesting insights. The analysis covers 14 different studies with a total of 239 athletes." (Not much of a sample and I'd love to know how many of the subjects were highly trained elite athletes compared to the usual untrained volunteers - makes a huge difference to the response.)
>
>
>
> He quotes those researchers as concluding (my emphasis added):
>
>
>
>
> > "It has, however, been suggested that the inflammatory response is critical for optimal repair of damaged tissue. Although the mechanisms of training adaptation are not fully understood, it may be detrimental to reduce the commonly accepted damage-repair-adaptation model by diminishing the inflammatory response; however, there is a lack of evidence to support this.
>
> **
>
> > This raises the question of whether frequent or habitual use of strategies designed to reduce inflammatory responses can be detrimental for elite athlete adaptation to training."
>
>
>
> Hutchinson himself says:
>
>
>
> "...no one really knows whether repeated ice baths have a practically significant effect on reducing adaption to training. From what I understand, the English Institute of Sport has been advising its athletes to avoid ice baths after routine sessions during heavy training phases, but to incorporate them during tapering and competition. In other words, periodize your recovery protocols so that you maximize adaption during training periods and maximize recovery during competition periods. Does this work? Maybe we’ll find out at next year’s Olympics!"

Ice baths are like taking antioxidant supplements - beneficial but should be timed right so as not to interfere with inflammation which, never mind adaptation, is crucial for healing to occur. However use of ice baths after/during training - a standard set by France's national institute of sport being 15 minutes in 50 degree cold water - is a far cry from CT...I think.

### Barry — 2012-04-07T02:15:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12327>

Simon

First up, do us a favour and lose the sarcastic tone , that's not what this forum is about.

Secondly , I'm here to merely postulate and hypothesis and to try at look at the big picture. I also happen to work as a sports scientist and have actually spent a number of years working with a colleague who actually did his phd in ice baths/recovery. So I know a few things and I know the research.

So my purpose here is to share and learn, that's all. I'm not trying to mislead anyone or even claim to be correct.

I also work with elite world class athletes and I know exactly what they do. And I can tell you, they don't ice bath post training. They are only advised to do so during competition, which if you read my very first post it's exactly what I pointed out.

Now if you want to look at the research , I have heaps of it . Why don't you read this in its entirety first and then we'll go from there [http://www.sonafe.org.br/images\_up/immersion\_cryotherapy.pdf](http://http://www.sonafe.org.br/images_up/immersion_cryotherapy.pdf)

>

>
>
> > I also happen to work as a sports scientist and have actually spent a number of years working with a colleague who actually did his phd in ice baths/recovery. So I know a few things and I know the research.
>
>
>
> What would save some time is if you give us a reference or two when you are launching what you call a hypothesis on us. But really it didn't read like a hypothesis, it was more like a done-and-dusted, case proven statement:
>
>
>
> If you look at all the studies, and there are plenty of meta-analysis to go by, the conclusions are as follows:
>
>
>
> \- Ice baths do numb the pain
>
> \- But they blunt adaptations
>
>
>
> Basically, ice/cold therapy inhibits the acute inflammation. This prevents the body from adapting to the stress. This is not what you want to happen. You want to adapt so that after subsequent training sessions you don't cause as much damage and can thus train harder.

With one Google search I found a meta analysis from 2011 in which the researchers said the effects on adaptation are unproven. If you are hear to share and learn it would be helpful if you would address that, rather than refer me to a paper from 2007 which was a one-off treatment measuring inflammation markers and not addressing anything to do with adaptation. I don't know what the second link refers to as it doesn't work.

So you work with elites who don't use ice after training; that is not my experience of what the elite runners and triathletes here in Boulder do, not to mention the pro US football teams who are currently seem to exploiting cryotherapy chambers en masse if the media are to be believed.

We know that ice baths reduce inflammation; they MAY blunt adaptation, but unless there's been a better or more up to date meta-analysis than the one I referred to, published September 2011 in the British Journal of Sports Medicine, that hasn't been proven. So I'd still like to know how you are so sure. And I need to know this not just on an intellectual level but for highly practical reasons. You may work with elite world-class athletes; I AM one, albeit at the masters level, and getting this wrong has major implications for me.

As far as I'm concerned the spirit and purpose of these forums is about questioning and exploration. We are all trying to sort out how best to use Jack's discoveries for our own best purposes. I don't want to get into a useless argument with you, so I'm sorry if my tone has offended you... but we are, as you say, here to question everything.

### Barry — 2012-04-08T23:25:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12789>

Simon

right, things get lost in translation, so screw my comments... lets get straight to the point and we can help each other, power in numbers and all that :

Regarding the first study: the reason I posted this is because I know the researcher. What happened after this study is as follows :

Several weeks later, the same group then conducted a follow-up study to look at the effects on subsequent training bouts. By repeating the model of the initial study following a bout of exercise promoting exercise induced muscle soreness, they achieved similar results. However, they then waited a week & repeated the exercise bout but this time allowed neither group to recover using cold water immersion & what they found has significant implications for the use of ice baths in the training phase. The results showed that, after the second bout of exercise, the group that had performed cold water immersion after the initial exercise test demonstrated ratings of perceived muscle soreness, decrements in maximum voluntary contraction & levels of serum myoglobin post-exercise consistent to the those recorded on the control group of subjects following the initial bout. In contrast, the group that had initially acted as the control group, showed significantly lower ratings of perceived muscle soreness, decrements in maximum voluntary contraction & levels of serum myoglobin when the results were compared - ie, the group that hadn't had an ice bath had managed to adapt & gain some form of protection from the damaging effects of the exercise, whilst the adaptation had been blunted in the group that initially had had an ice bath.

This isn't published yet so you'll just have to take my word for it for now. But what happened as a result of these findings is this - The English Institute of Sport now advise all their athletes NOT to ice bath post standard training sessions, only competition. In terms of the mechanisms involved, well we are not entirely sure. Possibly something related to mTOR, Sacromere's and Interluekins. Not my specialist field (I'm more into the Nutrition), so this is a guess for now on my part.

However, if you look at all the meta-analysis, all conclude the same thing - more research is needed, its not clear whether ice baths improve recovery. Also, there are very few long term studies, apart from the unpublished one I refered to above. Yet, when you think of it, acute inflammation is what drives the hormetic affect and thus physiological adaptations. So, I believe that it doesn't make sense to constantly (and immediately) ice the muscles post training. Let the body adapt.. that's how it works best

Your take ?

### SimonM — 2012-04-12T01:26:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-13976>

> **Barry;12858 wrote:** Simon
>
>
>
> right, things get lost in translation, so screw my comments... lets get straight to the point and we can help each other, power in numbers and all that :
>
>
>
> Regarding the first study: the reason I posted this is because I know the researcher. What happened after this study is as follows :
>
>
>
> Several weeks later, the same group then conducted a follow-up study to look at the effects on subsequent training bouts. By repeating the model of the initial study following a bout of exercise promoting exercise induced muscle soreness, they achieved similar results. However, they then waited a week & repeated the exercise bout but this time allowed neither group to recover using cold water immersion & what they found has significant implications for the use of ice baths in the training phase. The results showed that, after the second bout of exercise, the group that had performed cold water immersion after the initial exercise test demonstrated ratings of perceived muscle soreness, decrements in maximum voluntary contraction & levels of serum myoglobin post-exercise consistent to the those recorded on the control group of subjects following the initial bout. In contrast, the group that had initially acted as the control group, showed significantly lower ratings of perceived muscle soreness, decrements in maximum voluntary contraction & levels of serum myoglobin when the results were compared - ie, the group that hadn't had an ice bath had managed to adapt & gain some form of protection from the damaging effects of the exercise, whilst the adaptation had been blunted in the group that initially had had an ice bath.
>
>
>
> This isn't published yet so you'll just have to take my word for it for now. But what happened as a result of these findings is this - The English Institute of Sport now advise all their athletes NOT to ice bath post standard training sessions, only competition. In terms of the mechanisms involved, well we are not entirely sure. Possibly something related to mTOR, Sacromere's and Interluekins. Not my specialist field (I'm more into the Nutrition), so this is a guess for now on my part.
>
>
>
> However, if you look at all the meta-analysis, all conclude the same thing - more research is needed, its not clear whether ice baths improve recovery. Also, there are very few long term studies, apart from the unpublished one I refered to above. Yet, when you think of it, acute inflammation is what drives the hormetic affect and thus physiological adaptations. So, I believe that it doesn't make sense to constantly (and immediately) ice the muscles post training. Let the body adapt.. that's how it works best
>
>
>
> Your take ?

Thanks Barry,

I appreciate you opening up this line of inquiry. The thing that most worries me about all this research is that, with a few exceptions, the scientists are carrying out research on different things, but don't seem to be bothered by that fact. Some are studying the effects of cold water, some ice water. They are studying different time periods of application. They are studying the effects in everyone from untrained to highly-trained elites. They study the effects after widely different forms of physical activity, some of which bear no relation to what athletes do in training. And then along come the meta-analysers claiming to have definitive conclusions after selecting studies that fit their own personal criteria.

One thing we CAN say for sure is that no one is studying the application of ice or CT as we are experimenting with thanks to Jack.

For example, I was stunned to realise that in the latest meta-analysis by Leeder et al, nowhere in the paper do they define what they are reviewing - "Cold Water Immersion". The Leeder review focused on 14 studies, but the criteria for inclusion didn’t include water temperature nor time under cold; and nowhere in the full-text article do they define what "Cold Water Immersion" actually is - neither in terms of a temperature range or duration of exposure.

In that meta-analysis they also don't specifically identify the 14 studies (and, indeed in some of their tables, have included many more than 14 studies), but looking at a couple of the article's references at random, you'll find Cold water immersion defined as

"seated immersion in an ice bath (plunge pool) (9.2Â°C Â± 0.2Â°C) to a level of the iliac crest for 9 min followed by 1 min seated at room air temperature" and in another study as "15min in a dedicated bath...continuously immersed in water temperatures of 10 C" (50 degrees F).

The previous most up to date systematic review (2009) from the University of Ulster included 16 studies, but only those using cold water (wards, compared to the control group. So in the second go-round, they would maybe show some biochemical markers of that. I don't agree that finding lower markers in the control group means they had in some way adapted...the only way you can see who's adapted or not is by the scoreboard. Ie, I'\[d want to see a real-life performance measure before jumping to any conclusions.

Finally, (phew! I know, sorry about the length, but I did say this was important to me  ), in terms of adaptation and timing and what-not...Ned Kock writes:

> Ned Kock // Apr 6, 2012 at 15:59
>
> I wrote part of a post on cold-induced thermogenesis, of the mild (cold) sort, and basically forgot about it. Now that post is going to be resurrected!
>
> It should be no surprise that one would feel pretty good after a bath in ice-cold water. At least in theory, that type of cold exposure should induce muscle glycogen depletion, just like intense exercise, with the resulting hormonal changes:
>
> [http://bit.ly/hkvlfv](http://http://bit.ly/hkvlfv)
>
> Growth hormone should be up after that, for hours. Done right after weight training, or intense exercise, it may have a boosting effect on the hormonal response.
>
> But if you do that in the recovery phase (eg several hours after the weight training session), it should impair muscle recovery. It would be like doing another strength training session, when the body is trying to recover.

I've often wondered about the strength of this "timing" thing with regard to the so-called nutrition "window". We are told that to optimise recovery we should eat protein and carbs ASAP after finishing training - I've heard it put down to 20 minutes after. But when do you start the clock? After squeezing out the last rep on the weight stack or on the track? Or after you've finished your cool-down? Or after you've finished walking home form the gym or the track? So.. similar question now crops up: if you do a proper ice bath immediately after your training session, the body "probably" perceives it as just another part of the training session and proceeds to adapt to it. I often have a 15-minute swim after a treadmill run... not quite the same stress...but you see the point.

Done.

Thanks Barry for bearing with the endless questioning of everything. And apologies to any other Krusers who are not quite as nerdy as us!!!!

### Barry — 2012-04-16T00:33:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-15399>

Simon

Thanks for the reply, you've been doing your homework I see ;-)

1\. What constitutes "ice baths "

I agree, the studies vary quite a bit. I do think the standard is between 10-15min and 10C. Even Dr K's outdoor CT protocol is 10-20mins. Not much more we can say here other than yes I agree, the meta-analysis does not review consistent "ice bath" treatments.

2\. Subjects, Training Type etc

Again, I agree, it's all about context. So not much to argue here either

3\. Inflammation and Adaptation

This is what I am questioning !

Your synopsis doesn't tie in with what happened. Both groups ran the same distance/speed/gradient. The "ice bath" group had more DOMS and myoglobin than the non ice bath group. This was the observation, wish I had the paper to share.

I agree, the Kruse CT protocol does differ from the standard 10min @ 10C. Maybe 60min in ice would be different. We just don't know. The timing issue you mentioned I'm not too sure about. I don't think there is a "body thinking it is another part of the training" affect... it has to be more physiologically defined than that I think. What I mean, to go back to my whole initial argument, is whether "icing" reduces the inflammatory response which then causes a blunting of the adaptation response.

Here is a study done on the Italian rugby team, not sure of the time/temp of cryotherapy though

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S030645650800106X](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S030645650800106X)

So they showed a decrease in pro-inflammatory cytokines. The question is: is this a bad thing ? In terms of training adaptation, the general consensus would be that yes it is ?

I know there are lots of variables and you did a good job at pointing them all out. My focus though is purely on the effect CT has on training adaptation though. There is now plenty of evidence to show that training adaptations are induced by acute inflammation. Therefore, "if" CT reduces inflammation and reduces the pro-inflammatory markers, particularly if it is done on a repeated basis (i.e. after every training session), is it blocking adaptation ????

question everything ;-)

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-17T06:14:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-15821>

> **SimonM;14045 wrote:
>
> In the inflammation study you had me look at, they refer to Cold Water Immersion in the title, but call it "cryotherapy" in the paper - and define it as "10 min cold-water immersion (mean 10 degrees C)" (50 degrees F).
>
>
>
> So again, I'm not sure I would call this an ice bath as we know it  And it is five minutes short of an "ice bath" as per the standard proposed by the French national institute of sport (mentioned in previous post). **Jacks "prescription" for a CT ice bath is ice water at approximately 40 degrees F** ([The Cold Thermogenesis Protocol.](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/?utm_source=rss&utm_medium=rss&utm_campaign=the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx))
>
>
>
>
>
>
> following this thread with interest but just a note to the bolded part, the 40 degree F is in reference to the ice water ingested prior to the CT immersion
>
> _Also drink 16-32 oz. of ice cold water immediately prior_ to the test no matter what stage you are at. Why you ask? Your body temperature is incredibly hot at approximately 98.6 degrees Fahrenheit, and ice water is approximately 40 degrees Fahrenheit**. In order to maintain this homeostasis, your body has to bring that ice water up by about 60 degrees, and, by definition, it takes 1 calorie to raise the temperature of 1 liter of water by approximately 2 degrees Fahrenheit.

elsewhere on the blog and in the forum when asked he has indicated that 50-55F is the ideal temp for full immersion for 45 minutes

### hellojtm — 2012-04-17T06:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-15822>

Here is the thing I don't get from a systems perspective. Supposedly the micro-trauma is what drives the need to adapt to exercise. So we lift some weights and we make little damages on our muscles and the body comes in, gets inflamed, autophages the torn up tissue, replaces it with more new tissue (more , meaning in excess of before). So if we are going to say that some form of CT (as Simon and Barry agree it's not well defined) blunts adaptation, what happens to that process? Where does it stop, would you just adapt more slowly or would you end up with scar tissue instead of new muscle...?

### SimonM — 2012-04-17T23:52:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-15994>

> 3\. Inflammation and Adaptation
>
>
>
> This is what I am questioning !
>
>
>
> Your synopsis doesn't tie in with what happened. Both groups ran the same distance/speed/gradient. The "ice bath" group had more DOMS and myoglobin than the non ice bath group. This was the observation, wish I had the paper to share.
>
>
>
> I agree, the Kruse CT protocol does differ from the standard 10min @ 10C. Maybe 60min in ice would be different. We just don't know. The timing issue you mentioned I'm not too sure about. I don't think there is a "body thinking it is another part of the training" affect... it has to be more physiologically defined than that I think. What I mean, to go back to my whole initial argument, is whether "icing" reduces the inflammatory response which then causes a blunting of the adaptation response.
>
>
>
> Here is a study done on the Italian rugby team, not sure of the time/temp of cryotherapy though
>
>
>
> [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S030645650800106X](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S030645650800106X)
>
>
>
> So they showed a decrease in pro-inflammatory cytokines. The question is: is this a bad thing ? In terms of training adaptation, the general consensus would be that yes it is ?
>
>
>
> I know there are lots of variables and you did a good job at pointing them all out. My focus though is purely on the effect CT has on training adaptation though. There is now plenty of evidence to show that training adaptations are induced by acute inflammation. Therefore, "if" CT reduces inflammation and reduces the pro-inflammatory markers, particularly if it is done on a repeated basis (i.e. after every training session), is it blocking adaptation ????
>
>
>
> question everything ;-)

Hey Barry,

Thanks for the response... I'll cut to the chase. I'll ignore (for now) the study you know about but isn't published yet, as it's becoming obvious I can't make any intelligent comments about it. So the question remains: does CT and/or ice bathing (in any of its many variations) blunt adaptation? I still think this is unproven. Here's my reasoning so far:

1\. Recovery and adaptation are linked - and I \*think\* we agree that CT/ice does help recovery. (You're saying - at the expense of adaptation.)

2\. Adaptation and recovery occur as a result as a massive “switching on” of specific genes. I don't think exposure to cold is going to stop this. I may be wrong.

3\. Part of the immediate response to intense exercise is switching on of mechanisms designed to quench free radicals and inflammation. There is acute inflammation going on DURING exercise. Once exercise stops, biochemically it is all hands to the pumps to STOP inflammation - or at least to tone it down to reasonable levels so that it does not interfere with healing. CT/ice helps that process.

4\. Part of the training/adaptation response is an upregulation of defences against inflammation - specifically against free radicals and other pro-inflammatory signals. CT/ice exposure helps that. In fact exercise has been proposed as a treatment for chronic inflammation.

5\. As far as I can see from what's published, this immediate post-exercise boost of natural anti-inflammatory mechanisms does not affect the adaptation to training; in fact it seems to be an adaptation itself. Adaptations to training are complex and involve a lot of different areas of the body, so right now I'm not convinced that inflammation is even necessary for adaptations in skeletal muscles.

So, is a decrease in pro-inflammatory cytokines - shown in the rugby study - a bad thing? No, it isn't. And having done a bit more "homework", it is clear that there is another consensus that this is just what we want to happen... the overall effect of the acute inflammatory response generated by exercise is a net anti-inflammatory response.

Here are a couple of relevant references showing that first, one of the key adaptations to exercise is an increased anti-inflammatory response (therefore, as above, damping inflammation with ice baths or whatever is going to help, not hinder). And, secondly, research showing that people exercising the most, and the most intensely, have LOWER markers of inflammation. Again - an enhanced anti-inflammatory response (aided by ice/cold, to go on and on about it) - is itself an adaptation to training.

1: “Regular exercise causes adaptation of the antioxidant and repair systems, which could result in a decreased base level of oxidative damage and increased resistance to oxidative stress.”

Radak Z et al, Adaptation to exercise-induced oxidative stress: from muscle to brain. Exerc Immunol Rev. 2001;7:90-107.

2\. “Overall, data from observational studies show that the greater the volume of physical activity, the lower the risk of elevated levels of inflammatory biomarkers.” and

"The well-known activation of inflammatory pathways invoked by a single bout of exercise makes it almost counterintuitive that regular physical activity would serve to reduce chronic inflammation. However, it is now evident that an acute inflammatory response plays a major role in the training adaptations observed in exercised muscles. Contracting skeletal muscle produces and secretes several cytokines (myokines), most notably IL-6, which mediate metabolic changes during exercise (8). IL-6 release from muscle increases up to 100-fold during contractile exercise and its production results in increased anti-inflammatory cytokines...(SNIP)....Thus, acute exercise activates an immune response, but the effects are primarily anti-inflammatory and serve to enhance lipid and glucose metabolism. In turn, regular/chronic exercise not only leads to lower levels of circulating inflammatory markers, but also reduces the inflammatory response to acute exercise."

Barbara J. Nicklas, Ph.D. and Tina E. Brinkley, PhD. Exercise Training as a Treatment for Chronic Inflammation in the Elderly. Exerc Sport Sci Rev. 2009 October; 37(4): 165–170.

So, I'm still with Plan A. And, still "question everything" - agreed

### SimonM — 2012-04-18T00:57:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-16023>

Oh and just to complicate matters further  I was just reading a blog by Steven Magness (runner and ex.phys) where he says that in a lab he knows they have just shown that antioxidants (taken at the wrong time) suppress PGC-1a, which is a pathway that leads to increases in miotchondria - one of the adaptations we train for.

Steve repeats the idea that free radicals and oxidation occurring during endurance training are signals for adaptation - and act on that PGC-1a pathway (but note, he says "during", not "after"). So I was "homeworking" on that and just found that PGC-1a **"is strongly induced by cold exposure".**

That statement is in this paper: [Liang and Ward, PGC-1α: a key regulator of energy metabolism,

Adv Physiol Educ December 2006 vol. 30 no. 4 145-151](http://http://advan.physiology.org/content/30/4/145.full)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-18T01:37:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-16037>

Barry I am well aware of what you are talking about but I wonder if you realize why they are positioning themselves this way? I do . Only a few elite athletes are marrying the cell membrane needs to the training for signaling. So, what I think youre asking is the their a problem in a mismatch of cT and post training yes......not because CT, per say, but because they are not eating to support their optimal EFA ratio's How do we know this? The Russians have done this work on their Olympic athletes and it has leaked out to some of the super elite world athletes. Those athletes are using much different sessions and ideas that you bring up here. I think your point is very valid but the real issue for the super elite for performance is all about marrying CM biochemistry with the possible performance. I have personal knowledge of only six elite athletes that are marrying these two issues together. One in particular has seen massive gains and performance changes that has translated to their bottom line. The other five are trying to regain an edge after an injury or increase a falling performance due to age. What they are doing is quiet but the fact that they are marrying CT with their diet congruently is not. I think the training in high performance athletes is evolving tremendously these days. Old Russian coaches are being paid 7 figures by some top level athletes for data they have.

Simon,

great info. thanks.

I'm going to stick to my whole adaptation argument. Yes, I agree, the adaptation from exercise results in lower oxidative damage, more anti-inflammation and upregulation of endogenous antioxidants. I'm not debating that at all. My debate, and I know I am repeating myself here, is whether suppressing the inflammation (and the magnitude by which it is supressed) can inhibit the adaptation process.

You started it now, so I'm going to get a bit Geeky too, so for the Geeks:

one of the main pathways needed for skeletal muscle adaptation is the NF-kB pathway,. This comprises a family of several transcription factors (p65, p52. p50, c-Rel). ROS promotes the NF-kB pathway and this triggers the gene expression. Pro-inflammatory cytokines cause ROS production. Therefore, if we inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokines, is it possible that we are not activating the NF-kB pathway and thus gene expression ? Of even if CT only partially reduces it, isn't it still "not optimal" in the bigger picture ??

And since you mentioned the PGC-1 (another can of worms!)... this can be blunted by quenching ROS through antioxidant supplementation. Interesting the potential switching on of PGC-1 through CT, need to look at that paper again. But it still raises the question as to whether we are actually blunting adaptation in the bigger scheme of things.

By that I mean, serious athletes. Your references are 1. for average exercisers and 2. the elderly. But really what I am referring to here is full time/elite athletes or even those amatuers that train like the pro's. So am and pm sessions, endurance/intervals/S&C etc, 6 days a week, all year round. You know the type I mean. Does constant CT/ice therapy cause inhibition of the training adaptations on a long term basis ????

A great read for you here in relation to the geeky stuff, bit of a head melter but good stuff

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/expphysiol.2009.050526/full](http://http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/expphysiol.2009.050526/full)

### Barry — 2012-05-07T12:36:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-21900>

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-07T16:07:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-21920>

I do not believe we lose any adaptation with cold exposure after training. Sustained chronic cold increase depletion of glycogen at muscles while we lose WAT and gain BAT. This combo of events depletes intramuscular glycogen which also increases testosterone and growth hormone secretion in a pulsatile fashion to a great degree. This increases adaptation and does not decrease it. This is not well studied in humans but I know people are doing these studies now because there are many reports in the professional sports leagues where performance increases after cold. In fact one of my blogs will talk about this very issue.

### Barry — 2012-05-07T19:10:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-21957>

Jack,

I know of other ways to deplete glycogen and promote BAT etc .. But If cold suppresses HPA-axis , how can this be good for adaptation ??

As you said , there are very few human studies on this . It's not new though . Cold baths and cryotherapy have been used by teams and athletes for years. Yes, acute use is good i.e once a week after a game or during competition - I'm fine with that. It's chronic everyday use which I question . What we know now about the acute inflammatory response in terms of training adaptations doesn't correlate with chronic ice therapy. And it's all about context of course - I'm talking about athletes, those which already have many of the attributes you referred to like BAT, up regulated GH, UCP-3 etc . Lots of athletes already have this. So why ice after every workout if it can inhibit the acute inflammatory response which they need ???

### shah78 — 2012-05-07T22:27:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-22023>

Hi barry, why do you keep insisting you need the "acute inflammatory response". As a recovering adrenal deranged ironman triathlete, I can surely state: "F ck the acute inflammatory response". I have eliminated almost all DOMS with this CT. WOW! Performance maybe? But you (me) have to be able to function to complete the next workout. Perhaps you've lived a charmed athletic life and don't need to recover, but I sure as hell do! Adaption at age 56 is not as important to me as just being able to do the workout.All the ability to" adapt" is not worth a thing if I'm lying on the couch in Pain dreaming of my "next" workout. All the best. Keep on moving/thinking/writing.

### shah78 — 2012-05-07T22:35:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-22027>

Old football players (32 years old!) state similar things. They use the cold tubs way more than the younger guys. Like 10 x more! It gets them on the field.They don't need to get any "better". They need to stay on the field....... Why do you feel you need to get "better" at your "advanced" age. You're in the top 1%(for your fitness level) of all human beings whatever age. How much more adaption do you need?You my friend ,just need to stay on the field for the next 40 years. What am I missing?

### Barry — 2012-05-08T18:23:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-22249>

> **shah78;22104 wrote:** Old football players (32 years old!) state similar things. They use the cold tubs way more than the younger guys. Like 10 x more! It gets them on the field.They don't need to get any "better". They need to stay on the field....... Why do you feel you need to get "better" at your "advanced" age. You're in the top 1%(for your fitness level) of all human beings whatever age. How much more adaption do you need?You my friend ,just need to stay on the field for the next 40 years. What am I missing?

Shah78,

you just highlighted two things for me

1\. Context - yes, I agree, if you simply want to "get through" your training, then constant ice baths work. They help DOMS and also psychologically numb the pain. But that's not what I or many other athletes just want from our training. We want to get better and not just "get through" the session. We want to adapt !

2\. Individual v Team Sport - so in team sports its about getting ready for the next game, you are already talented, so adaptation per se is not top of the agenda. However, as you know, with individual sports its different. Every second counts. And in order to improve you need to adapt and constantly hit the switches, upregulate x, y and z, express this and express that. Excuse my words, I'm just making simple without going into the deep biochemistry ! Anyways, this is why acute phase inflammation is key and inhibiting it is counter productive.

Inflammation is your friend, if its managed correctly ;-)

### SimonM — 2012-05-08T20:51:15+0300

But running all the miles and doing all the weights, **and icing,** and eating right and sleeping right, it's just gathering dry material and at the end you have a pile of sticks. And just remembering what it's like to catch fire; I caught fire again."

(emphasis added)

\- American leader in the 5,000 (15:08), Julia Lucas, talking about how she went back to her roots at North Carolina State after a downturn in her running career. In a year she went from "running slower than I did as 19-year-old" to being a possible contender for the US Olympic team. [http://news.runnersworld.com](http://http://news.runnersworld.com/2012/05/06/brief-chat-julia-lucas-in-the-5000/)

### Croak — 2012-04-05T02:48:47+0300

> **HPC
> : Do you have a certain way you approach food?**LA: I didnâ€™t for a long time until about a month ago until I started messing around with this new diet.HPC**: What changed?
> LA**
> : I started swimming again, and I swim with a guy \[ed's note: former triathlete Rip Esselstyn\] who started basically a
> [food program called the Engine 2 Diet](http://http://engine2diet.com/)
> , which is a plant-based, 100% natural, organic diet. His dad was a famous cardiologist who did Forks Over Knives, and
> [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aPpcBMwLg2Q\[/video\]\]was President Clintonâ€™s doctor](http://[video=youtube_share;aPpcBMwLg2Q)
> . Clinton has gone to a completely vegan diet and heâ€™s essentially erased his heart disease.

It's an agenda, a marketing cash in, and just plain misguided touchy-feely thinking. And too much time in bed with Sheryl Crow.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-05T02:52:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-lance-armstrongs-engine-2-diet.664/post-11553>

Here is what the known world forgets.......Lance and Phelps are still competitive.......they wont tell the world what they are really doing......but we know it involves cold.......that we know.

### jumcc — 2012-04-05T03:06:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-lance-armstrongs-engine-2-diet.664/post-11560>

> **Croak;11621 wrote:** It's an agenda, a marketing cash in, and just plain misguided touchy-feely thinking. And too much time in bed with Sheryl Crow.

haha that's awesome. i find it very hard to believe he would expose how he eats and trains, it's just interesting the type of information these athletes put out there in interviews. the sad thing is there are a lot of people looking up to him who will jump on this way of training and eating.

### PaleoSue — 2012-04-05T05:24:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-lance-armstrongs-engine-2-diet.664/post-11614>

Where can we find more about how Lance utilizes cold? I haven't been able to find out anything about it.

"Born to Run" talks about a much more plant-based diet, too. Was only part of the book I didn't particularly care for.

### MonteD — 2012-04-05T06:07:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-lance-armstrongs-engine-2-diet.664/post-11629>

> **Croak;11621 wrote:** It's an agenda, a marketing cash in, and just plain misguided touchy-feely thinking. And too much time in bed with Sheryl Crow.

That is a logical fallacy. There can never be too much time in bed with Sheryl Crow.

### MonteD — 2012-04-05T06:16:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-lance-armstrongs-engine-2-diet.664/post-11630>

I've talked to Rip Esselstyn about his Engine 2 Diet (vegan) a few times. He claimed that none other than MARK SISSON was one of his former trainers. When I asked about the primal diet his response was basically; "I love Mark, he's a great guy, but I think we've evolved past the need for that kind of diet. It comes down to protein and everything has protein in it."

He then went on to explain how his gluten laden breakfast had almost 80 grams of protein in it. lol.

### SimonM — 2012-04-05T07:29:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-lance-armstrongs-engine-2-diet.664/post-11637>

> HPC : Do you have a certain way you approach food?
>
> LA: I didnâ€™t for a long time until about a month ago until I started messing around with this new diet.
>
>
> Two things to "get" here: 1) As he's just started "messing around" (ie researching) this diet, it is NOT the diet that enabled LA to dominate 7 Tours de France. So you can ignore it in terms of performance.
>
>
>
> 2) The idea that he has never had a "certain way" to approach food is, as Jack suggests, PURE misdirection. The guy is a detail fanatic who leaves NOTHING to chance. Elsewhere he has talked about very strict dietary control.
>
>
>
> PaleoSue
>
> Where can we find more about how Lance utilizes cold? I haven't been able to find out anything about it.
>
> "Born to Run" talks about a much more plant-based diet, too. Was only part of the book I didn't particularly care for.

1\. There is nothing, anywhere, documenting how Lance uses cold, apart from his experiments with raising his body temperature so that Dr Allen Lim and his other advisors could work out the most effective way of keeping his core temp down while he is warming up and competing. (You will see him warming up on rollers wearing a cooling vest, for instance).

2\. "Born to Run" is a fantastic, inspiring book. However it is ironic that the only two take-home messages people get from reading it (or reading about it) are a) run barefoot and b) eat chia seeds

Ironic because the Raramuri (Tarahumara), the heroes of the book, do not run barefoot and have a much more varied diet that chia and corn. In fact if you read almost anything about the Raramuri other than Born to Run, it is clear that just like almost every other traditional culture, they eat meat whenever they can get it. In the past this was rabbits, goats and snails. Now they also have a liking for beef.

And I agree with Monte.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-05T08:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-lance-armstrongs-engine-2-diet.664/post-11649>

good post Simon. Hoping to see more critical thinking like this on the forum!

# Thread: Awesome HDL with Paleo/CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Phoenix — 2012-04-05T03:44:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-11573>

My company offers free cholesterol tests once a year. Here are my results:

Cholesterol 283 (100-199)

HDL 89 (>50)

Ratio 3.2

LDL Calc 183 (0-99)

I think the HDL is amazing. Last year it was 68. So post paleo/CT it increased by 21 points. Prior to that it only budged a little when I was exercising like crazy. Currently I walk every day, but only a few miles. Been doing Paleo for about 4 months, CT for one month.

I eat no dairy, no nightshades, no gluten, no starchs, lots of low carb veggies (I eat more than my vegetarian husband), very little chicken, lots of grassfed beef/salmon/sardines, cook in CO.

I'm feeling stronger too. Stairs have become a bit easier for me. I also stopped my thyroid meds when I started Paleo. They didn't seem to do anything for me anyway. I plan on having a thyroid panel done soon to see how it's behaving. Feels pretty good though. No brain fog, and more energetic.

What do you all think of the high total cholesterol of 283? I've heard of the "fluffy" cholesterol explanation, so am hoping that's what this is. This lab report had all kinds of big red exclamation points all over it telling me I had a serious cardiovascular risk because of that number. It was forwarded to my doctor, so I'm expecting a frantic call from his nurse, telling me that they want to give me some poison (statins) to bring it down to "normal".

Thoughts?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-05T04:26:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-11582>

Too bad you did no thave your HS CRP done at same time.....but I love those numbers. But numbers have to be put in context.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-05T04:47:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-11592>

Phoenix, I had similar cholesterol numbers very recently. My brand new doctor, recommended on Jimmy Moore's low carb doctor list, just praised my high HDL and my low Trig. He said the LDL was probably mostly large and fluffy. He said he could see if my insurance would pay for the more expensive test to analyze LDL, but it was just an afterthought.

Wow it is so AWESOME to have a doctor who understands!!!

He also took an HS CRP, but it was high. However, it was lower than it was just before I started CT.

### Phoenix — 2012-04-05T05:15:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-11608>

> **Jack Kruse;11651 wrote:** Too bad you did no thave your HS CRP done at same time.....but I love those numbers. But numbers have to be put in context.

I'll see if I can have that done when I have my thyroid tested. I'm guessing it's going to be low. Inflammation in my body is way down. I can tell.

### Phoenix — 2012-04-05T05:19:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-11611>

> **Nonchalant;11661 wrote:** Phoenix, I had similar cholesterol numbers very recently. My brand new doctor, recommended on Jimmy Moore's low carb doctor list, just praised my high HDL and my low Trig. He said the LDL was probably mostly large and fluffy. He said he could see if my insurance would pay for the more expensive test to analyze LDL, but it was just an afterthought.
>
>
>
> Wow it is so AWESOME to have a doctor who understands!!!
>
> He also took an HS CRP, but it was high. However, it was lower than it was just before I started CT.

At least your HS CRP is moving in the right direction. Nice to have an intelligent doctor too!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-05T06:05:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-11627>

Your numbers are similar to my BFs. He is really proud of his HDL @ 90 and trigs @ 41. When he was bragging about them someone told him those aren't that great since you want your trigs 75-100. WTH?!?!?!? I don't think so! Way to go for having such great numbers. I hope your thyroid numbers come back good too!

### sisterclare — 2012-04-06T05:05:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-12050>

I am so happy that my doctor is ordering most of the lab tests to be done next week!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-11T17:02:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-13732>

Good job everyone on the good numbers! Keep up the good work---not that it's some slave work. :-D Don't you love that feeling of knowing and seeing what you're doing is actually WORKING?

### MartiD — 2012-04-11T17:25:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-13745>

I am also looking forward to having my numbers redone. I had them done in October after two rounds of HCG and losing 50 lbs and starting the Leptin RX and actually things were worse (cholest was up 241 from 227, HDL droped 5 pts to 67, LDL raised from 133 to 155 and ratio went from 1.82 to 2.31) from year before but I am hoping that after 6 months of LRX and Paleo and CT the numbers will be better!

### ruby — 2012-04-15T10:11:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-hdl-with-paleo-ct.666/post-15263>

Since going paleo and then transitioning to "Krusing" my results, in the space of a few months, went from:

Total cholesterol - before - 166; now - 160

HDL - before - 69; now - 84

LDL - before - 88; now - 49

triglycerides - before - 44; now - 55

VLDL - now - 27

HDL ratio - 1.9

weight: before - 138; now -128

current BMI: 20.5

Just discovered that I have borderline (hereditary?) hypertension. Any idea, in the above context, what I can do to bring my BP down?

Dr. Jack, please give it a shot.

# Thread: Bet my cholesterol is higher than yours! :)  Hack request...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bet-my-cholesterol-is-higher-than-yours-hack-request.672/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### texasmom4 — 2012-04-05T18:27:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bet-my-cholesterol-is-higher-than-yours-hack-request.672/post-11771>

I've spent the past two days researching in order to understand my lab results. Could I get some input, please?

VAP (my numbers, then CW guidelines in parentheses)

Cholesterol, Total - 400 (

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-05T18:43:18+0300

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-05T21:55:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bet-my-cholesterol-is-higher-than-yours-hack-request.672/post-11849>

If you're not seeing any signs of hypothyroidism, I wouldn't bother supplementing (if you get swings from hyper- to hypo-, signs of Hashi's, I think different advice would apply). If they're mild, I'd wait it out with the LR & CT. If they're interfering with daily life, I'd consider supplementing, but track changes carefully (there are some free chapters from a book I found linked from Dr. John Lowe's website that explain what and how to track) & read up at STTM for other things to consider (like iron, adrenals, aldosterone, dosage, ramping up, etc.).

Colleen and some others are better thyroid experts than I. I'm just a mediocre hack.
