### Guest — 2012-04-07T18:31:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-cold-adaptation-story-in-the-media.675/post-12477>

Before people start breaking open the celebratory kombucha, I would argue that none of these articles really tell us all that much about CT. Essentially, they show that it is possible for these _individuals_ to adapt to cold temperatures. Of note, the guy who swam in the Antarctic sea maintained a reasonably high core temperature through "frantic swimming." This tells us nothing about the putative health benefits of CT. Are this guy's telomeres longer? Does he have a more robust immune system? We don't know.

I don't think anecdotal evidence is enough to support a theory.

### AKMan — 2012-04-07T18:36:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-cold-adaptation-story-in-the-media.675/post-12478>

> **APow;12546 wrote:** Before people start breaking open the celebratory kombucha, I would argue that this article doesn't really tell us all that much. Essentially, it shows that it was possible for this _individual_ to adapt to cold temperatures. He maintained a reasonably high core temperature through "frantic swimming." This article tells us nothing about the putative health benefits of CT. Are this guys telomeres longer? Does he have a more robust immune system? We don't know.

This article:

Man breaks world records with Antarctic swim

How can a person wearing only Speedo trunks survive the icy sea?

You have to be fit or fat - or both - to survive a dip in the Antarctic.Â© GettyBritish swimmer Lewis Pugh this week broke two world records, for the most southerly swim ever undertaken in the ocean, and the longest-duration polar swim ever completed. In completing the two feats, he battled temperatures that would kill you or me in minutes. So what does he have that we mortals don't?

On 14 December, Pugh swam a kilometre in the seas off the Antarctic Peninsula at a latitude of 65Âº South, some of the world's coldest waters, where the sea's saltiness allows temperatures to dip to just below 0 ÂºC without freezing. The feat, which took 18 minutes and 10 seconds, required him not only to maintain a safe body temperature throughout the ordeal, but also to stave off the crippling effects of the body's natural reaction to icy water.

Two days later, he swam a mile off the nearby Deception Island, spending 30 minutes and 30 seconds in the water - longer than any other polar swimmer. Physiologist Tim Noakes of the University of Cape Town, South Africa, who accompanied Pugh on the trip, said: "I did not believe it possible to swim for 30 and a half minutes in 2-3 ÂºC water in just Speedo trunks."

"Normal people would probably be dead within a minute because of the cold-shock reflex," says James Butcher of British medical journal The Lancet1, which profiled Pugh ahead of his record attempt. Less than a second after hitting the water, the lungs constrict, causing an untrained person to hyperventilate, taking up to 60 gasping breaths per minute. Heart rate and blood pressure also skyrocket as the heart panics in response to the shock. Pugh says he felt "screaming pain" all over his body as soon as he dived in.

"It's an exaggerated fight-or-flight response," explains exercise physiologist Michael Tipton of the University of Portsmouth, UK. The paralysing effect of this response means that, in icy water, the body becomes its own worst enemy; not that this is typically a problem. "In our normal day-to-day experience, we don't tend to fall into cold water," Tipton points out.

Cold shower

Fortunately for Pugh, overcoming the response is fairly easy. "Anybody can develop a habituation to the cold," says Tipton. "You can do it with as little as five or six 5-minute immersions."

Tipton and his colleagues have found that, with enough exposure to cold water, volunteers can even be trained not to shiver, allowing muscles to work more effectively. In the weeks leading up to his swim, Pugh took frequent cold showers as well as training in icy water.

“The thing that keeps him going is the ability to produce large amounts of heat.”

Michael Tipton

University of Portsmouth

Of course, preventing the body from shutting down is just half the battle - Pugh and his support team also had to ensure that his body temperature remained above 35 ÂºC, which is generally regarded as the cut-off point for hypothermia. "The thing that keeps him going is the ability to produce large amounts of heat," Tipton says.

Pugh is aided in this by what Noakes calls 'anticipatory thermogenesis'. Just the sight of icy water is enough to send his body temperature ratcheting up to 38 ÂºC, giving him a head-start against the ravages to come.

"Mental imagery can have a profound effect on physical responses," says Tipton. The same thing happens on a smaller scale when the average person anticipates giving a speech, for example, as their body releases stress hormones and ups its metabolic rate.

Fit or fat

Once in the water, the best way to maintain body heat is to produce a lot of it through frantic swimming, and to stay well insulated. The best long-distance swimmers tend to have a combination of these "fitness and fatness" factors, Tipton says.

Women tend to be relatively better than men at outdoor swimming, because their tendency to have more fat over their leg and arm muscles keeps them better insulated. This is important during active swimming, when blood is pumped into the arms and legs and so can accelerate heat loss. The previous record-holder for polar swimming is US female Lynne Cox, who has attributed her success in part to her 36% body fat (most women have a percentage in the 20s).

But thinner men and women can gain back an advantage by being fit. "If you see a thin outdoor swimmer, the chances are they're really fast," says Tipton. Pugh falls into this category. Besides his record as the only person to have swum at both ends of the Earth, he is also the fastest man to swim around South Africa's Robben Island, completing the circuit in just three hours and 42 minutes in 2004.

# Thread: I think I hacked some unpleasantness of the cold tub!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-think-i-hacked-some-unpleasantness-of-the-cold-tub.676/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T22:10:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-think-i-hacked-some-unpleasantness-of-the-cold-tub.676/post-11859>

I remembered Jack saying he would sometimes sit in the hottub with warmer water on his bottom half, while icing the upper.

This inspired me. I've been having a real hard time getting into the tub, because of the shivering. So last night I put a big ol space heater up by the tub (caution, don't do where it could fall IN the tub). So it was roasting my head and feet as I put each of them near the hot areas. Meanwhile, my lower half was in the 50 degree water and getting chilled properly.

I don't know if this hurts the CT process, hopefully someone that knows will say.

The outcome was A W E S O M E! No shivering, and I made it all the way to 55 mins (and could have stayed longer cept it was time to put the kiddos to bed). Previously 25 mins was my max, and I was miserable for a bit after.

My skin temp got down to the 55ish zone. I was certainly cold on the cold parts for hours after, but I think I was firmly in the non-shivering-thermogen stage vs the internal full body shivvvver.

If this temp variance is allowed, and the CT is still all good with the contrast in temp, it sure did make it easier!

### AKMan — 2012-04-05T22:58:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-think-i-hacked-some-unpleasantness-of-the-cold-tub.676/post-11886>

I don't think this will hurt your efforts, anything to make it more bearable has to be good.

I am amazed at you folks doing 50 degree baths. I lowered my hot tub down to 75 degrees and I think that feels damn cold-- especially when the pump kicks on. I get these kind of mini-shivers at 75 degrees. I will try 65 degrees next week.

I have also been walking around in the snow&ice barefoot in 20 degree air this week just prior to getting in the 75 degree water. I am amazed that anyone (Wim Hof) could run a marathon in the snow while barefoot. The sharp ice stabs at you, but the cold makes your feet go numb in just a few minutes.

I'm so sad our Alaska Winter is coming to an end, supposed to be 50 today. Snow melting fast.

### Croak — 2012-04-05T23:02:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-think-i-hacked-some-unpleasantness-of-the-cold-tub.676/post-11891>

What's this 50 stuff you talkin' 'bout Willis? Mine is 40f when I get in, 45ish when I get out an hour later.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-05T23:15:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-think-i-hacked-some-unpleasantness-of-the-cold-tub.676/post-11901>

I have a feeling that once you get below 75 it feels cold... and somewhere around 40-45 you get so numb that it's actually easier than 55.

I'm just making this up...

I have no idea if the hot air is going to set back your CT. What do we really know about CT? Dr. Kruse said something about getting the skin to 50-55degrees and that this lets your body know a big change is coming. But he has alluded to steepening the gradient, and also that getting in the warm sauna after a CT session will undo the effects. But then he sits in his warm tub while wearing ice.

A lot of variables... I don't know that anyone is in a position to actually answer these questions at this time. It's not like Dr. Kruse has a bunch of control groups trying every approach. I take what he says as more of a canned answer that is guaranteed to work, not a minimalist approach at all. My 2c.

jesse

### TheKid — 2012-04-05T23:23:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-think-i-hacked-some-unpleasantness-of-the-cold-tub.676/post-11908>

Well if you want unpleasantness, try doing only showers. I don't have an alternative soon have to use the cooled showers at my gym. Usually constant at 50-55. But every so often it dips into the high 40s. Yesterday was one of those days, and I can tell you that it, how shall we say, challenging.

I am really thinking of buying a huge. Garbage can instead. Guess I need to buy an ice maker as well.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T23:34:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-think-i-hacked-some-unpleasantness-of-the-cold-tub.676/post-11917>

> **hellojtm;11970 wrote:** I have a feeling that once you get below 75 it feels cold... and somewhere around 40-45 you get so numb that it's actually easier than 55.
>
>
>
> I'm just making this up...

I actually think you are right with this. I live in the same city as Croak and find the colder water is easier to take than the 55F

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T23:44:18+0300

I still got lots of results (including detox results!) Now that it's warmer out and I'm more cold adapted, the water doesn't feel so bad. I'm also experimenting with warmer water (65Âº) with more immersion, since BAT is still activated at this temp. It feels pretty good!

Now that my water temps are higher, I can even go straight into a slightly cool shower and then almost immediately go full cold and actually enjoy it

I should clarify: I'm also doing ice wraps all the way around my torso on my skin, so I'm def. still getting some major cold. In fact, the coldest I feel is after taking off the ice!

I turned the house air way up when I was taking baths in the winter (60 degrees or less). Now the tap water is closer to 75 and the outdoor air is 80 and i relish the cold baths!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-06T18:57:17+0300

Careful staying frosty! (LOVED my cold bath yesterday!)

# Thread: Awesome Wim Hof Video
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-wim-hof-video.680/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-04-06T01:16:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-wim-hof-video.680/post-11975>

You guys gotta watch this. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nRsNh0eB-Io\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;nRsNh0eB-Io\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nRsNh0eB-Io\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;nRsNh0eB-Io)

Scientists injected him with a toxin and he was able to control his immune system to where he had almost no effects. They are going to let Wim teach 10 people his techniques and do the toxin test on those 10 and 10 others with no training...I can't wait to see the results!

I'd bet this research facility would be interested in what Dr. Kruse has been doing.

If you want to know Wim Hof's breathing technique, it's simple:

1\. Take in 30 deep breaths and blow completely out, like blowing up a baloon.

2\. On the last breath, breathe it all out and hold as long as you can without taking in more air.

3\. When you can't hold it, take a deep breath and hold for 10 seconds.

4\. Do this often (several times a day), recording your times as you go.

5\. There also seems to be a variation, where on the 30th breath, you fill your lungs and hold it in as long as you can.

I have been working on this technique for a few weeks, when I started I could hold with no air in my lungs for about 30 seconds and with air about 1 minute. Today I am up to 1 min 20 sec w/o air and 2 min 45 sec with air.

Hope you can watch the video, it's in Dutch w/english subtitles.

### chocolate — 2012-04-06T01:27:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-wim-hof-video.680/post-11977>

Neat, I can't stay submerged with air in my lungs....If I'm not moving, I float....I can't wait to see the brilliant/correct way. (No, it's not alternate air.)

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-06T02:17:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-wim-hof-video.680/post-11997>

> **AKMan;12044 wrote:** You guys gotta watch this. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nRsNh0eB-Io\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;nRsNh0eB-Io\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nRsNh0eB-Io\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;nRsNh0eB-Io)

> Scientists injected him with a toxin and he was able to control his immune system to where he had almost no effects. They are going to let Wim teach 10 people his techniques and do the toxin test on those 10 and 10 others with no training...I can't wait to see the results!
>

>
> I'd bet this research facility would be interested in what Dr. Kruse has been doing.
>
>
>
> If you want to know Wim Hof's breathing technique, it's simple:
>
> 1\. Take in 30 deep breaths and blow completely out, like blowing up a baloon.
>
> 2\. On the last breath, breathe it all out and hold as long as you can without taking in more air.
>
> 3\. When you can't hold it, take a deep breath and hold for 10 seconds.
>
> 4\. Do this often (several times a day), recording your times as you go.
>
> 5\. There also seems to be a variation, where on the 30th breath, you fill your lungs and hold it in as long as you can.
>
>
>
> I have been working on this technique for a few weeks, when I started I could hold with no air in my lungs for about 30 seconds and with air about 1 minute. Today I am up to 1 min 20 sec w/o air and 2 min 45 sec with air.
>

>
> Hope you can watch the video, it's in Dutch w/english subtitles.

Hi AKMan,

That is a great video. Thank you for sharing your research. Do you have a source where someone could get more information about his techniques? I just beat pneumonia in February without antibiotics. I would have liked to know a technique to increase my immune reaction at first and then reduce my cytokine storm afterwards.

One more question about your breathing experiment. Do you have any insight as to the significance of increased time for empty lung and full lung? Are you doing anything else?

### chocolate — 2012-04-06T02:28:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-wim-hof-video.680/post-12005>

You know, there is an sheeps adrenal extract I'm thinking about keeping in my fridge. I was looking at an article I posted on the gut brain axis and its seems to be a circadian disconnect for the cytokine storms in the brain. I would think It would be the same for anything, for some reason we aren't kicking it loose, exhaustion or timing. But I'm with you on the breathing exercises.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-06T02:39:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-wim-hof-video.680/post-12007>

I sent all my photos today to CBS. just sayin......the haters will hate.

### AKMan — 2012-04-06T03:03:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-wim-hof-video.680/post-12017>

> **Caroline Cooper;12066 wrote:** Hi AKMan,
>
>
>
> That is a great video. Thank you for sharing your research. Do you have a source where someone could get more information about his techniques? I just beat pneumonia in February without antibiotics. I would have liked to know a technique to increase my immune reaction at first and then reduce my cytokine storm afterwards.
>
>
>
> One more question about your breathing experiment. Do you have any insight as to the significance of increased time for empty lung and full lung? Are you doing anything else?

Wim has a website; [http://www.innerfire.nl/en-home](http://http://www.innerfire.nl/en-home) He has a lot of info there, but I got hold of his breathing technique from the book "Becoming the Iceman" by Wim Hof and Justin Rosales.

I'm not sure what increasing your time for breath holding actually does for you. From [http://gettingstronger.org/tag/justin-rosales/](http://http://gettingstronger.org/tag/justin-rosales/) I got this:

...the key adaptation occurs within the brain during meditation–specifically the yoga and controlled breathing exercises that Wim and the tumo practitioners follow. Of these exercises, breath retention exercises are key. As a result, there is a significant activation of blood flow and electrical activity in his frontal cortex and hypothalamus — areas that regulate peripheral nerves and veins involved in the regulation of body temperature. Normally, the circuit between the hypothalamus and these temperature control circuits is involuntary, governed by the autonomic nervous system. Kamler reasonably speculates that, through meditation, Wim is able to override the normal function of the hypothalamus, allowing the peripheral veins to remain open and heat the extremities, preventing injury. He points out that Wim must be generating heat and distributing it more efficiently, but he admits having no idea mechanistically how Wim’s meditative techniques accomplish this.

If you read the article and comments you will see some familiar names!

# Thread: Chocolate !
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 77
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-06T08:36:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12069>

Help from all you nutrition gurus....

I have been eating lots of 100% chocolate (can't get 99% here - I've been eating fermented, raw powder, as well as teh roasted) for the resveratrol and magnesium (and upon Dr K's recommendation). I have just, however, read a post by Chris Kressler regarding phytic acid and how chocolate (in any form) should NOT be a regular part of our diet.

So... for all your chocolate eaters out there... what do you think?

Is chocolate on your go-to list? Or your no-no list? In what form? Quantities? If you eat it, do you supplement with zinc to deal with the potential mineral depletion?

If anyone knows if there is a specific form of chocolate that reduces phytic acid and is best for health, please chime in. Or with any opinions about chocolate

Thanks!!

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-06T17:23:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12144>

Dr K has a chocolate bark recipe in his book made from 100% ... So must not be keeping him from optimal?

Others have posted they're using the 85 percent from Green and Blacks organic during the reset (as have I). I've noticed meant brands include soy lecithin... Bad? I go for soy and rice free chocolate. I'm still losing 2-4 lbs a week so I'm happy....

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-06T18:13:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12169>

I eat about 1 oz the 99% chocolate about 3-4 times a week. It doesn't seem like much. I stay away from anything with soy in it in any form. I also make the doc's bark and eat that instead of the chocolate. Either way I only eat a bit for taste and then I am done. I don't crave it or need to have more after I eat it.

### wyldeman — 2012-04-06T18:22:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12172>

Currently I am eating 1/2 oz of the 100% cacao Baker's chocolate with a brazil nut twice a day.

It's 100% cacao, so it will most likely not trigger binges ( I consider myself blessed to not have really any struggle with those)

### ealachan — 2012-04-06T20:47:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12219>

I mix a TBSP of CO, a TBSP of unsweetened cocoa, a tsp of good cinnamon, and a tiny pinch of sea salt into a paste and eat it as-is. Sometimes I add a tablespoon or two of unsweetened flaked coconut too, for funsies. It's very tasty.

### AKMan — 2012-04-06T21:31:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12230>

I gnaw on a square of unsweetened baker's chocolate every night after dinner. I used to think there was no way in the world anyone could eat that stuff straight, but now I love it!

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-06T21:36:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12232>

I gave up chocolate for lent to support DH who I challenged to give up wheat/gluten - I get it back on SUNDAY!!! WHOOT...

### nonchalant — 2012-04-06T21:37:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12233>

I eat 90% Lindt. No soy. Hard to believe a couple years ago I thought any non-milk chocolate was horridly bitter.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-06T22:05:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12246>

So it sounds like no one, including Dr K, is worried about the _phytic acid content of chocolate. It's not in the additives in chocolate, but in the cocoa itself according to Chris Kresser, it's as bad as nuts and grains. I am not eating anything but 100%. I have a choice between roasted and unroasted (both fermented).

from the article:

_One of the main principles of the Paleo diet is to avoid eating grains and legumes because of the food toxins they contain. One of those toxins, phytic acid (a.k.a. phytate), is emphasized as one of the greatest offenders.

But what is often not mentioned in books or websites about the Paleo diet is that nuts are often as high or even higher in phytic acid than grains.

Disappointing side note for chocolate lovers: Raw unfermented cocoa beans and normal cocoa powder are extremely high in phytic acid. Processed chocolate may also contain significant levels.\]_

He then lists a table to show it has more phytic acid per 100g than things like almonds, brown rice, peanuts, and lentils.

In the comments/Q&A people repeatedly asked him about chocolate and he kept saying to never have it as a daily part of your diet.

**Do you think it's just plain wrong?**_ I'm hoping so, but am not convinced.

(By the way, I'm also amazed with my changing tastebuds. I love the 100%.)

### ginastarke — 2012-04-07T18:13:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-12470>

I eat either cocoa nibs (The ones from Navitas are available online, and have a very nice crunch) or unsweetened baking chocolate. Ghirridelli is good ,and a lot more affordable, but Schaffen Berger is the best. I also melt down some coconut oil and manna with cinnamon and anise seeds and stir in 3-4 tablespoons of cocoa.

With me it depends on what part of the month it is, around "that time" I gotta have it, the rest of the time, I don't care that much.

### Glamorama — 2012-04-11T04:57:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-13610>

I developed extreme pains in my gut thanks to phytic acid (leaky gut) and dark chocolate never caused me any issues, however, last month I noticed that 85% gave me a weird feeling in the gut... like the leaky gut pain I used to have, but less. I couldn't firgure out why, but this makes sense. I take zinc picciolate every 2nd night... The gut discomfort is gone again now. Personally I love my dark chocolate/barks etc!

### healthnut — 2012-04-11T05:09:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-13618>

Glam~ Coffee and cocoa are very acid. Both also contain a lot of copper, maybe something to do with that too?

I purchase raw cocao powder from Wilderness Family Naturals: [http://www.wildernessfamilynaturals.com/category/chocolate-products.php](http://http://www.wildernessfamilynaturals.com/category/chocolate-products.php)

I noticed it a lot less mild than natural cocoa powder. It is more fruity and a lot stronger.

I make 'homemade' chocolate with organic cocoa butter and the raw cocao powder. That way I can make it even stronger than store bought. I melt 1/2 cup of cocao butter and let it cool a bit. Then whisk in about 1 1/4 cup of the raw cocao powder and a little vanilla. Pour it on some wax paper on a baking sheet and freeze it to harden. You could add other things like crushed green tea leafes, nibs, sea salt....

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-18525>

I just made some chocolate "candy" with a 4 oz. 100% Ghiradellis chocolate bar, 3 oz. of Nutiva coconut manna, about 4 tbls. of finely shredded unsweetened coconut, some crushed macadamia nuts, and about 8 drops of English Toffee flavored stevia. I poured it in a silicone muffin pan and put in the freezer for about 10 minutes.

Yummy...not really sweet even with the stevia. The finely shredded unsweetened coconut makes it chewy.

Nutiva coconut manna is like crack!!!

So, here' my chocolate story. Since I've been on a low carb paleo diet for as long as I can remember (15 years or so) I've written off chocolate. Anything candida needs I try to avoid or have it in the am, even with the biotin. The candida makes ethanol at night when we sleep. I'm going to get some chocolate nibs and try the water soak and make a tea or extract. The epicatechin is the stuff that heals mitochondria. ### [email redacted] — 2012-06-05T15:48:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-29529>

Souldanzer the reason you might be sick with Lindt is that it is not gluten free. Lindt admits they use barley malt to sweeten all chocolate under 70% and they do not change or even clean machines for the 70%+ brands.

fwiw, be careful with Green & Black as well, if you are sensitive you may react to G&B

> Although some of our products do not use cereal ingredients, they are produced in a factory that does handle them and therefore there are risks of cross contact.

you have to check G&B country sites for your country to see if there is cross contamination

[http://www.greenandblacks.com/us/our-chocolates/bars.html](http://http://www.greenandblacks.com/us/our-chocolates/bars.html) (the US site)

if you click on the 85% bar it does have the cross contamination warning. not important if you are not gluten intolerant but helpful if you are.

I\`m one of the super sensitives and the only two chocolates I can eat at this point are Endangered Species 88% and CÃ´te D\`Or 86% as both are certified gluten free. (in case you are wondering Jack\`s 99% bars have the cross contamination warning  )

### kathylu — 2012-12-06T22:52:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-66633>

Coconut Chocolate "Crack"

Â 

1 jar of Nutiva coconut manna

2 squares (1 oz) of baking chocolate (unsweetened, I use Callebaut)

1/4 cup coconut oil

sweetener to taste (I use a mix of stevia and z-sweet)

Â 

Melt the chocolate in the coconut oil (1 minute in the microwave), stir thoroughly. Soften jar of coconut manna by setting in a pan of hot water for 10 minutes or microwave the open jar for 1 minute, stir until mixed. Add the chocolate-oil mix to the jar of manna, sweeten to taste, mix well. Eat anytime the urge for chocolate strikes!

Â 

Remixes:

Add cinnamon and nutmeg, maybe ginger too

Add a bit of sea salt (for you salted chocolate fans)

Mix 50/50 with butter, use as a "nutella" type spread on coconut flour scones

Try adding flavoring extracts (banana, cherry, vanilla, maybe peppermint?)

Have fun...this is delicious!

### Charlotte — 2012-12-07T16:09:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-66720>

I just gave the receipe in another post but here itis again:

Melt your favorite dark chocolate in a bowl over a pan of boiling water. Add some almond milk or other milk of your choice. When warm, add Mascarpone. It is an Italian cream which is almost pure fat. Mix until creamy and add any spice (like vanilla) you like. I add Stevia as well. Pore in little dessert cups and put in the fridge for one hr. Then you have a delicious mousse au chocolat for dessert or when the chocolate craving comes. I also crush raspberries in it occasionally. Real good and a great dessert.

### Charlotte — 2012-12-07T16:21:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate.689/post-66723>

I also put macadamia nuts and chocolate pieces in my Champion juicer or in blender and grind or blend until it is a chocolate-nutbutter. It is great on coconut or almond cookies. These I make with coconut flour, coconutÂ  coconut oil, Ghee. With eggs they stick better together but I cannot eat eggs so I make them without and keep them in the fridge.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-06T15:56:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-12098>

Hi all. Time to get this adrenal and leaky gut thread going. I have soooo many questions since I have stage 7 adrenal fatigue and leaky gut, both confirmed, along with candida and hormone issues but the latter 2 are for another thread.

For starters, my first question to getting to optimal is when is the appropriate time to take HC, DHEA & pregnenolone?

I have read to take the DHEA and preg. first thing with fat and protein. But I have also read to take DHEA and preg. on any empty stomach first thing. Which is correct?

I was also doing some reading on HC and it said it needs to be taken with food or it might cause stomach issues. I have enough stomach issues going on so I really need some insight into this. If HC is to be taken with food, how should I take it at my 3 pm time when lunch was 3 hours ago and supper isn't for 3 more hour since no snacks allowed? I cannot take it too late in the day since it is supposed to be mimicking my natural cortisol rise and fall.

Thanks for any input!

### hazyjane — 2012-04-08T02:44:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-12558>

> **vkiernan;12412 wrote:** Thanks Hazyjane. Can I ask how long have you taken the preg? How much? What have you noticed from it? So you are taking it without food? I take 20 mgs. 2 x/day. I started out with Apex Energetics brand, which is a liquid form and I seemed to need a lower dose of that (according to muscle testing by my doc ) than what I'm taking now (Source Naturals brand). I think I may go back to Apex.

I had low DHEA and progesterone, so my holistic doc wanted to try pregenolone. I've been on it for a few months- maybe 3-4 (I can't remember!) It's hard to say what I've noticed- I do think it's helped my adrenals and hormone balance, though right now I can tell I still have low morning cortisol going on. My head feels heavy in the a.m., but at least I can think clearly! My cognition has been pretty good, so it may be helping with that.

I need to do another ASI to see how my adrenal hormones are faring.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T04:14:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-12876>

I was on HC for two years. Luckily was able to get off of it recently. I took it 4 x a day and did not take it with food. I had no issues. If you do have issues then take it with a little tea and cream or coffee and cream. Unless of course you are sensitive to dairy. Anyway you shouldn't need much cream to tolerate the HC.

I've taken DHEA first thing in the morning on an empty stomach and have never taken preg. I'm happy to say that I am completely off of all adrenal support after having severe adrenal fatigue!!! Even though I was off of the adrenal meds a few months prior to starting CT I do feel like doing the Leptin Rx and CT have helped immensely.

\--Steph

\--Steph

Hi Steph. Thanks for the input. Yep, been on DIM since last fall along with silymarin. Estrogen was being reduced according to labs but pms and sore breasts tell another story. Sounds like you are dealing with everything I am attempting to deal with. The CT has really messed me up hormonal too but I am not quitting. I am going to keep letting it do it's thing and hope for the best. I cannot believe what cold water truly does to your body. Seems like most of us females and a few males are really getting the detox. It is crazy and I am floored when I read all the stuff that is happening to all of us like hormones, old injuries flaring up, nausea, headaches, etc. I have experienced all those I mentioned.

How much HC and what time of day did you take it? From some stuff I've read, I am taking too much at once. I don't know anyone that takes it to even begin to know good from bad etc.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-12T23:48:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-14416>

> **vkiernan;14155 wrote:** Hi Steph. Thanks for the input. Yep, been on DIM since last fall along with silymarin. It is crazy and I am floored when I read all the stuff that is happening to all of us like hormones, old injuries flaring up, nausea, headaches, etc. I have experienced all those I mentioned.

> How much HC and what time of day did you take it? From some stuff I've read, I am taking too much at once. One book talks about taking no more than 10 mg per dose to not shut the adrenals down and I am on 12.5 morning and noon then 7.5 at 3pm. Do you think this is an issue? I don't know anyone that takes it to even begin to know good from bad etc.

Check out the Yahoo NaturalThyroidHormonesADRENALS forum ([http://health.groups.yahoo.com/group/NaturalThyroidHormonesADRENALS/)...it](http://http://health.groups.yahoo.com/group/NaturalThyroidHormonesADRENALS/\)...it) is a great resource and they helped me heal my adrenals. They do suggest you stay under 10 mg for the reason you mentioned. I was at one point taking 10 mg of HC and 4 Isocort first thing in the AM and I had no issues. I think I also was on 12.5 mg on HC at one point, but can't remember. Do what works for you. I was on 25 mg of HC for about 2 years. There were times I had to go up and I found out I had low aldosterone so was also on Florinef for a while...that stuff is no fun and causes major issues for many including me. Anyway, if I remember my schedule was 7:00 am 10 mg, 11:00 am 7.5 mg, 4:00 pm 5 mg, right before bed 2.5 mg. The one at bed has to be taken right when your head hits the pillow so that it doesn't cause any sleep issues. I can tell you that I did a lot of experimenting with my doses. I journaled everything I was taking and how I was feeling, so it was easy to see if somethign wasn't working. You do need to stick with a certain protocol though long enough to know if it is working or not, but don't be afraid to experiment a bit. I think the hardest thing was knowing when it was time to wean off of the HC. I felt like after two years I should go ahead and try and thank goodness I was able to do it without any issues. I did it very slowly...going down 2.5 mg a week. On a week when I wasn't feeling great I wouldn't go down at all. It took some time, but I finally got off of it. I just got the saliva test kit from my doc, so can't wait to see what's going on now!

The moderators on the adrenal forum can help a lot. Unfortunately that forum gets very busy, so a lot of times I found it easier to read through the old posts for information versus trying to ask my own questions. Tons of great info and they can help you interprut your labs. Hope that helps.

\--Steph

### vkiernan — 2012-04-13T02:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-14491>

> **hazyjane;14492 wrote:** DIM actually GIVES me breast pain for some reason!! It's been a consistent side effect every single time I've ever used it. I ended up having to go off of it and use borage oil to reduce the breast pain, which was lasting for 2 weeks out of the month (I normally have zero to little breast pain).

Strange, but there is always one in the bunch that doesn't follow the rules! I wonder if it gets the estrogen moving from your cells and then your body has to deal with it and also if you weren't removing it from your system it just recirulates. Do you know if you were E dominant at the time?

### hazyjane — 2012-04-14T02:58:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-14959>

> **vkiernan;14560 wrote:** Strange, but there is always one in the bunch that doesn't follow the rules! I wonder if it gets the estrogen moving from your cells and then your body has to deal with it and also if you weren't removing it from your system it just recirulates. Do you know if you were E dominant at the time?

I was estrogen dominant (at least relative dominance). I believe that DIM is supposed to actually block estradiol from the receptors, so it would seem like that would keep it from affecting breast cells.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-15T02:16:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-15168>

> **hazyjane;15028 wrote:** I was estrogen dominant (at least relative dominance). I believe that DIM is supposed to actually block estradiol from the receptors, so it would seem like that would keep it from affecting breast cells.

You lost me on that one. I thought DIM helped remove it from the system; but, if it is only blocking it then what good is it if I am not actually removing it? I need help on this one.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-15T02:25:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-15171>

Steph, Thanks for the info and link. Good info out there.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-15T07:34:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-15250>

> **vkiernan;15237 wrote:** You lost me on that one. I thought DIM helped remove it from the system; but, if it is only blocking it then what good is it if I am not actually removing it? I need help on this one.

Ultimately, your liver is what removes the estrogen, but it gets metabolized along 2 pathways- one makes harmful metabolites and the other makes beneficial estrogen metabolites. From what I've read, DIM helps it to be metabolized along the beneficial pathway, to balance the ratio of the estrogen metabolites. It also reduces estrogen receptor activity, which is why I don't understand the breast pain thing...

### Clabbergirl — 2012-04-18T19:32:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-16248>

I've been trying to understand the benefits of DIM also. This is something I've found [here](http://http://www.naturodoc.com/library/hormones/diindolymethane.htm) that might shed some light, and backs up what hazyjane spoke of above:

**Q. What benefits can DIM offer?

A. Supplementing our diets with DIM can shift the production of estrogen metabolites away from dangerous 16-hydroxy in favor of beneficial 2-hydroxy metabolites. _Taking DIM in an absorbable formulation encourages active and healthy estrogen metabolism. DIM supports estrogen balance by increasing beneficial 2-hydroxy estrogens and reducing the unwanted 16-hydroxy variety. This improves

estrogen metabolism and helps resolve all three forms of estrogen dominance.

NaturoDoc Note: Remember, 2-hydroxy is good; 16-hydroxy is bad._**

### hazyjane — 2012-04-19T05:09:12+0300

### hazyjane — 2012-04-20T22:06:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-17180>

> **Destiny;16899 wrote:** Anybody is taking Maca? What brand do you recommend?

I'd recommend Whole World Botanicals or Maca Magic brand. You want "gelatinized" maca, which means that it's been cooked. Maca is never consumed raw in traditional cultures (it's in the turnip family).

### hazyjane — 2012-04-23T03:22:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-17944>

> **tweety;17279 wrote:** hazyjane- why not raw maca? I take that in the powder form. I thought that was a good product.

Maca is never used raw in traditional Incan culture and there's probably a good reason for that. It's in the turnip family and I believe it might be goitrogenic when eaten raw. When you think about it, tubers in general are not consumed raw, except for carrots.

The gelatanizing process removes more of the starch and makes it more digestible, so that would be a good thing.

### tweety — 2012-04-23T20:54:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-18132>

> **hazyjane;18017 wrote:** Maca is never used raw in traditional Incan culture and there's probably a good reason for that. It's in the turnip family and I believe it might be goitrogenic when eaten raw. When you think about it, tubers in general are not consumed raw, except for carrots.
>
> The gelatanizing process removes more of the starch and makes it more digestible, so that would be a good thing.

Thanks for the response. I looked into it and saw the cons to eating raw, though some advocate that as the way to go. I looked and sure enough mine is raw, so I roasted it at a low temp in the oven. I hope that helps, it sure brought out the flavor.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-30T21:41:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-20163>

> **BJK77;20181 wrote:** Would maca be beneficial for someone who is low on all sex hormones? Most of what I've been reading indicates it helps deal with ED, but I don't think that's an issue for me. I just pretty much have nothing going on in the hormone department!

I think it would. It can raise testosterone. I believe it works on the level of hormone synthesis and adrenal health, hence its usefulness for general low hormones or specific low hormones.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-30T21:42:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-20164>

> **janagram;20200 wrote:** I'm 64, should I still try maca or progesterone?

I would try maca first and then add progesterone later if you're still low. Give it 3-4 months. This is generally how practitioners use it

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-01T04:23:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-20276>

Hi everyone. Hope you are all feeling well. I have to say I feel like I've turned a corner. Just when I thought my poor adrenals were beyond help, I've gotten some relief. Not sure what to point to. I have been taking Pregnenolone 50 mg along with my other vitamins and the estradiol. I just added progesterone. I have not been able to take Maca or Astragalus because I'd get a low blood sugar response. I have had an occassional coffee but always regret it afterward. Today I had a cup and felt very low blood sugar like after. But 99% chocolate seems fine. Of course I eat too much of it! Dr K has posted that he eats the 99% choc when he wants to lose weight! Not sure what he meant by that but Here's hoping!

But any way. I feel a lot better lately even though I have been eating more chocolate and coconut manna. Ive kind of cut back on my veggies because im trying to get back to ketosis but i keep overdoing the manna. Not in control yet. And I've gained at least 7 pounds Since Easter, which on the one hand, I am not hapPy about but on the other hand, I am happy to feel better. Go figure!

But now I have some heart palpitations but only in the evening. I take all my supplements (except magnesium and progesterone) in the morning, so I have no idea about the Palpitations. Maybe I should take the Pregnenolone every other day?? Any suggestions?

I, too, have just started having the heart palps again but mine are w/BAB. I just upped my preg. in the am to 100 from 40 w/BAB and 40/lunch because I ran out of the lower dose. I was thinking it was my thyroid med. I switched taking it at 9:30 am to right away on an empty stomach in the am with my dhea and preg. So, I made 2 changes without thinking and now I don't know the cause. But it is interesting that you are having the same thing. I have to mention to that I am also feeling like I've turned a corner for the better. Are you on thyroid meds too? Perhaps our thyroids are turning on and we don't need as much? So, we both feel better and we both are taking more preg. Hmmm. We will figure this out.

I am surprised you are not taking some of progesterone in the evening at bed time to help you sleep.

Did you back off the preg today? If so, let us know how you fare this evening. I would consider getting a smaller dose of the preg and taking it daily instead of every other day. This keeps your brain going daily. I am not sure but I don't think it is one of those things that stays with you per se. From what I've read, it helps you focus and stimulates your creative thinking. As Uzzi Reiss says, helps you swim in the crowd with people. Makes you more social. He says he has never seen it build and turn into other hormones like they say it does.

I have NO TROUBLE sleeping. Which is a blessing. I had that whole thing where you lie in bed and are SO TIRED but your body feels ramped up, like it is about to ran a marathan. I would lie there and say, "Body, go to sleep!" LOL But IDK how that resolved. After I bought my house in Dec and moved in and got settled, I started sleeping well. When I first started Leptin Rest I also started sleeping like a champ...until daylight savings hit. Then it was bad, then good again. Now, I've been traveling and living life out there in the world (as in spending time with friends, going OUT, theatre, etc) and I'm not going to bed as early and getting up is harder. I want to find a balance. I have also now gained 10 pounds since Easter. I could shoot myself. It's all me, eating too much

But, back to the subject at hand, yes, I take synthroid for Hashimoto's. Not the best, I know. I hope to switch to Armour or T3 soon. I did skip the pregnenolone today. So, we'll see. I feel very edgy and cranky, but we are being audited at work and I've been out of the office and have WAY too much to do, so I am stressed. My temp is still ridiculously low, so I don't think it's thyroid kicking in.

That is interesting what you say about Dr Reiss. I wonder do I need the 7 Keto DHEA. I am going to have my estrogen drawn now. I have a feeling its still low. I can't stand the dry eyes. I didn't realize that was an estrogen thing. I really should not make any more changes until I see the endo and functional med doctor. I"m gonna make myself nuts! LOL

That's funny you are being audited at work, we are too. Plus I had a girl leave and we don't have our replacement yet. It has been about 2 months. Stressful for sure!

Dry eyes are caused by a lot of things and does really suck. I go to the eye doc tomorrow and hope I can remove the plugs for my dry eyes for good. He probably won't let me but I will ask.

When I first started Leptin Rest I also started sleeping like a champ...until daylight savings hit. Then it was bad, then good again. Now, I've been traveling and living life out there in the world (as in spending time with friends, going OUT, theatre, etc) and I'm not going to bed as early and getting up is harder. >
> But, back to the subject at hand, yes, I take synthroid for Hashimoto's. Not the best, I know. I hope to switch to Armour or T3 soon. I did skip the pregnenolone today. So, we'll see. Hello!

Since doing so, I experienced extreme fatigue so i'm still getting the dose right...recently they made the change to half the dose and take some in the morning and some in the evening so we'll see if that helps! i have suffered from extreme dry eyes lately which has never been a prob in the past. they are thinking they may add a bit of estrogen (i am estrogen dominant only by relation - my levels are actually normal to low) but want to wait a bit to see.

colleen said her daughter benefitted from testosterone for her dry eye so i'm anxious to see if that will help. i do know my thyroid levels have been dropping so that could be it too (have hashis). i looked like both my T4 levels and T3 levels could use a boost so i was expecting to have my dr recommend an armour increase, but instead he just bumped up the T3. i did ok yesterday morning with the new dose but i pretty much crashed last night. ...**is that a sign of adrenal fatigue?** ...would i be better off upping the armour instead or is it ok to ignore the T4 (assuming it stresses the adrenals more to conver T4 to T3) i'm tempted to ask my doc for a low dose of HC bc my cortisol levels have been depressed for a while. i did a saliva test and they looked a bit better but i was on a bunch of adapatogens at the time so i'm wondering if they are truly "better". did anyone suffer bad side affects form hc?

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-05-15T15:32:45+0300

> **tweety;24241 wrote: That is similar to my day, but I haven't done anything about thyroid. I am trying to treat the adrenals and hormones in an attempt to straighten it all out. I was surprised Dzugan said no salivary too but she said it was bcs they feel it is unreliable.

I am trying to compile info for my own doc bcs I have read so much about getting a strong enough dose. I know Colleen advocates stronger doses too from her experience. I suppose its all anecdotal, but I still like to see other's experiences.**

**

i take the gel, 5% 50mg/ml....They had me on a higher dose but I did not tolerate it well - it made me VERY sleepy - not so good when you are trying to work. i can see how for some woman who are anxious that might be a welcome gift, but for a person still struggling with the thyroid, i was almost catatonic! haha. luckily, the weeks right after my period are always my best so being on a low dose right now is fine. i still have some tinkering to do as my period approaches.

i had very little pms symptons this month while on progesterone which was promising - bloat was almost nonexistent and my boobs did not get sore...also, for the first time in a year, my period started exactly on day 28 (its been on Day 31-34 for while). i think i can do more progesterone towards the end of the month but we are going to experiment with splitting the dose up (am and then taking the other half before bed to minimize the fatigue during the day). the only downside is that i was a bit depressed/anxious right around my period and am hoping some hormone tweaking will fix that and for some reason I always have a day or 2 where it feels like i'm coming down with the flu - cannot get out of bed, muscles weak, light headed....this always happens around my period so trying to figure that out as well**.....wondering if i might need a little estrogen as well. i guess my body is out of everything!

he also gave me testosterone (.1) but i am waiting until the progesterone is straightened out before trying that.

i know my cortisol is low and while i get being so conservative about HC, i think it might be time to try. for whatever reason, i have a really hard time tolerating thyroid dose increases and adrenals is about the only thing left we havnet addressed. despite my clean primal diet, leptin reset etc...i unfortunately need more thyroid hormone  yet my body fights it. not good. i desperately need it.

If it is tanked and you have low cortisol all day, then I would say "think" about the use of HC but not necessarily. If you just have wonky cortisol, then I would use CT and the morning sun and no light at night to help straighten your circadian rhythm out.

I did the salvia tests and i came out low on adrenals...we tried adaptogens (through chris kressor and my reg thyroid doc) but if anything my adrenals aren't really responding. i follow the leptin reset, go to bed appropriatly, get a ton of sun...nothing. I know my DHEA is fine - not tanked - not super high, but i'm ready to try soemthing else to give my adrenals a rest and time to heal I realize i gotta stay on top of fixing them, but they keep preventing my thyroid meds to work properly. I'm going to go slow. Ifi have an adverse reaction I'll stop.

my next step is to get in with this lyme disease doc over the summer and be checked out of lyme - i'd love tohave it ruled out bc its always in the back of my mind - perhaps that is what is stressing my adrenals out

My doc just perscribed 15 mg of which I'll work up to...they want me to start like this

1st week: 2.5 mg in the morning

2nd week, 2.5 in the morning, 2.5 at noon

3rd week: 5 mg in the morning, 2.5 at noon

4th week: 5 mg inthe morning, 5 at noon

5th week: 5 mg in the morning, 5 at noon, 2.5 at 3 or so.

Does this sound about right? I read at stop the thyroid madness that I should probably start with the 5th week recommendations instead of slowly ramping up -how did you do it? what was your response? just curious.

Nutmeggs,

I take 12.5 at 7, 12.5 at 11 and 7.5 at 3. She had me drop the 3rd dose as I wasn't getting tired by 10. I dropped from 10 to 7.5. On good days, it is enough. On stressful days, I can tell it isn't. Yes, I've had adrenal fatigue on and off for years.

If you have stage 7 adrenal fatigue, anything you do is going to take awhile to work- that's just the nature of the beast, so to speak. In fact, at the height of my adrenal fatigue, my acupuncturist told me it could take up to 2 years to fully recover.

I'm on a classic formula called Gui Pi Tang. It has some ginseng but it's not a stimulant. It's actually a sleep/anemia formula. One of my issues (which makes it super hard to totally recover) is _excessive_ dreaming. When you're stuck in excessive REM cycling, you don't get as much deep, slow-wave sleep, which is when the body/brain repairs itself, so you wake up very physically tired even after 9-10 hours of sleep. If the dreams are intense enough, you wake up exhausted. The Gui Pi Tang does seem to be helping, although it takes awhile to have the full effect.

### hazyjane — 2012-05-30T00:39:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-28249>

> **vkiernan;28258 wrote:** HazyJane, I need your help again. What do the initials ND stand for? Can they prescribe? Still on the hunt for a doc. Thanks so much for your help.

ND is a naturopathic doctor and they can't prescribe (unless they're also an MD or NP).

I would check out [http://www.functionalmedicine.org/](http://http://www.functionalmedicine.org/) and [http://thyroidbook.com/practitioner-locator.html](http://http://thyroidbook.com/practitioner-locator.html) for practitioners who can treat the endocrine system.

Not HazyJane, but I have similar challenges (Hashimoto's hypoT, adrenal fatigue, low DHEA, low ACTH, lots and lots of food sensitivities, probably candida, not doubt leaky gut). I have been all over to various places getting different pieces of the puzzle, for several years. I have been seeing an MD who specializies in the environmental medicine, and although not perfect, he's really been looking for the underlying cause of all this body malfunction (I plan to buy him Dr K's book once it comes out!). My experience with him for testing/labs/etc has been excellent, and defnitely not the regular mainstream answers (often prescribes vitamins, etc). I recently did a mold test and it came back positive (urine test done through realtimelab), so I am being treated with Cholestyramine to hopefully clear out the toxins from my body. Meanwhile, I'm testing for the source of the mold exposure...

I have yet to find that ideal doctor, but I do keep finding pieces along the way. ### hazyjane — 2012-06-02T23:15:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-29109>

I totally know how you feel- it's hard to find one doc to do it all. It's like you have to have a cooperative/open minded MD or NP to prescribe and a good functional medicine practitioner to know which labs to do and how to properly read them!

I say go to the glorified chiro for your endocrine stuff. Most of the people who are doing the best endocrinology work right now are glorified chiros. Then, go to whatever MD/NP is most cooperative with prescribing Armour and HC (though you may eventually be able to get off of the Armour and you def. should be able to get of HC).

One thing that might be helpful would be to find someone who does advanced electrodermal screening to check you for pathogens. I have a guy here in Nashville who has helped me countless times on that end when I knew something was wrong in the virus/bacteria dept. He was also able to give me the right herbs/homeopathics to clear up the little buggers. Electrodermal screening also checks for food/chemical sensitivities. Where are you? I'll see if I can find someone in your area.

Here are some things you can do right now w/o a doc:

\-Re: leaky gut- you can just go ahead and start healing your gut. I'd recommend doing that no matter what! I think Sea Cure whitefish peptides and L-Reuteri probiotics and lots of bone broth is a solid regimen. That way you've got the materials to rebuild the gut lining and the bacteria to repopulate it and keep the villi healthy (Reuteri is specific to this).

\-For the candida, just do a month of Candex along with some cat's claw (either Saventaro or Nutribiotic brand). Along with the leaky gut stuff, this should be enough.

Adrenal fatigue-wise (I'm the queen of that, LOL!) the things you can do on your own are read (or re-read!!) Adrenal Fatigue by James Wilson and adapt as many of the lifestyle things as you can.

Consider doing qigong as a way to build your energy back. It really has helped me tremendously! It builds energy in the adrenals, builds the immune system and helps with stress.

Spring Forest Qigong is a great healing system that I've heard amazing things about (it's popular with people with chronic illness). I'll probably learn this style to expand my own personal qigong practice. I have a lot of respect for Chun Yi Lin. He was able to help my qigong teacher heal his vision after a series of mini strokes damaged his ocular blood vessels (as well as stop the mini strokes from happening!) He's the reason my teacher started studying!

[http://www.springforestqigong.com/](http://http://www.springforestqigong.com/)

A good DVD that has a short, easy to learn routine is Qigong for Cleansing by Daisy Lee and Francesco Garripoli. It only takes about 9 min. once you learn it. I noticed a difference very quickly when I started using it. This was my introduction to qigong.

[http://www.amazon.com/Gong-Cleansing-Daisy-Lee-Garripoli/dp/B000AYELB0/ref=cm\_cr\_pr\_product\_top](http://http://www.amazon.com/Gong-Cleansing-Daisy-Lee-Garripoli/dp/B000AYELB0/ref=cm_cr_pr_product_top)

The key is to do it every day, even for only a few minutes. That's my main challenge- I know that if I could be totally consistent with it, I'm positive I wouldn't slip back into adrenal fatigue as easily  At least when I do, it's _nowhere_ near as severe as it was a few years ago! I've come a long way!!

### hazyjane — 2012-06-04T03:33:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-29300>

I don't really buy that glutamine feeds candida because I've successfully used it during candida protocols...

The reason I like Candex is because it's a systemic enzyme that implodes the cell wall of the yeast rather than slowly poisoning it (and it doesn't have a chance to release acetyldehyde as it's dying, which is what causes "die-off" symptoms).

Some strains of candida are more tenacious than others (not everyone has the albicans strain- there are quite a few strains). None of them become resistant to enzymes.

Re: working out, I'd stick to very gentle stuff right now, but you could do some isometric stuff which still works the muscles but might be gentler than squats and pushups. You can get Bar Method dvds from Netflix to try (and modify the workout a bit to make it less strenuous).

Aim to do some qigong every day! Once you learn the routine, you can skip ahead on the dvd to where they do it straight through.

Here are some little tip that increases the effectiveness of qigong: breath into your belly rather than your chest and (this will sound weird!) try to keep your sphincter closed. This keeps more of the energy in. In qigong, we store our energy in the lower abdomen (the lower "dantian") so you can imagine energy as golden light or liquid accumulating in a bowl in your belly Adding visuals like that really helps me.

Walks and gentle yoga are totally appropriate. I find when I walk that if I really "stroll" and enjoy my surroundings, it feels wonderful and healing (rather than walking because I "should"). It feeds my soul. Get lots of barefoot time in the grass to electrically ground yourself to the earth (I usually do my qigong in the grass).

### vkiernan — 2012-08-29T14:33:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-48050>

Danielle, the following is from LEF.ORG

Among DHEA's many metabolites, one has attracted significant attention for its unique ability to lower cholesterol, burn fat, and improve the immune system. This metabolite, known at 7-Keto DHEA, is not converted into estrogen or testosterone, so it may be safely used among people with hormone-dependent diseases, including cancer.

Scientific studies have shown that 7-Keto can help people burn fat through a process known as â€œthermogenesis.â€￾ This means the body's metabolic rate is accelerated, generating heat and energy that consumes calories and burns fat. 7-Keto accomplishes this by boosting the levels of liver enzymes that stimulate fatty acid oxidation.

In one study of 30 overweight adults, study subjects received 100mg of 7-Keto twice daily or placebo. They also participated in a supervised exercise and diet program. At the end of the study, those taking 7-Keto lost 6.3 pounds on average, versus 2.1 pounds for the control group (Kalman 2000).

7-Keto has also been studied for its immune-boosting and cholesterol-lowering properties. In a study on cholesterol levels, human volunteers applied a gel containing 25mg of 7-Keto for five consecutive days. At the end of the study, the subjects taking 7-Keto experienced a rise in good HDL cholesterol and a slight reduction in harmful LDL cholesterol (Sulcova 2001).

Another study looking at immune function found that four weeks of 7-Keto supplementation improved immune function in elderly men and women. In this study, subjects over age 65 took 100mg of 7-Keto twice daily or placebo. The subjects on 7-Keto experienced a significant decrease in immune suppressor cells and an increase in immune helper cells (Zenk 2004).

**Because 7-Keto is not converted into estrogen or testosterone**, it may be the perfect complement to DHEA therapy, as well as providing an option for people who have hormone dependent cancers. In some women, high doses of DHEA may cause the growth of unwanted hair or acne. By adding 7-Keto to a daily program, it may be possible to lower the dosage of DHEA.

Because of the overwhelming evidence connecting low levels of DHEA to the degenerative diseases of aging, Life Extension suggests that all people over age 40 begin DHEA therapy. For most people, the starting dose of DHEA is between 15-75 mg, taken in one daily dose. Many studies have used a daily dose of 50 mg. One recent study showed that doses under 30 mg were not enough to significantly support blood levels of DHEA in young adults (Cameron 2005).

Ideally, DHEA replacement therapy should begin with blood testing to establish a base range. Since almost everyone over age 35-40 has lower than optimal levels of DHEA, most people begin supplementation and test their blood DHEA levels later to make sure they are taking the proper dose. Normal serum reference ranges and ideal ranges of DHEA-S are:

Normal

(depending on age)

Ideal

Men

16.2-492 μg/dL

350-490 μg/dL

Women

12-407 μg/dL

275-400 μg/dL

After 3 to 6 weeks, another test is recommended to measure serum DHEA. All individuals react differently to DHEA replacement therapy, so it's a good idea to closely monitor your blood levels and side effects. If side effects appear, it may be possible to add 7-Keto DHEA and reduce the dose of DHEA.

Those with compromised liver health should use DHEA sublingual tablets, which bypass liver metabolism. Otherwise, capsules containing the more common micronized DHEA are quite effective in restoring DHEA to youthful ranges.

D - I tried to post this prior to you asking but didn't make it. This is from Jack's dhea blog.

Because of the overwhelming evidence connecting low levels of DHEA to problems associated with aging, I suggest that all people over age 40 begin DHEA therapy. For most people, the starting dose of DHEA is between 15â€“200 mgs, taken in one daily dose. Many studies have used a daily dose of 50 mg. Women tend to use the 7-keto DHEA form and men can use the regular DHEA form. WHY? 7-Keto DHEA is not converted into estrogen or testosterone, so it may be safely taken by women with hormone-dependent diseases like breast cancer.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-30T03:34:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hc-dhea-pregnenolone.691/post-48265>

thank you VKiernan.. I was just reading this blog.. so obviously my doctor was mistaken. I am going to print this post and show it to her and see if she can explain why my testosterone has risen significantly. thanks for pulling this up

> **vkiernan;48718 wrote:** D - I tried to post this prior to you asking but didn't make it. This is from Jack's dhea blog.
>
> Because of the overwhelming evidence connecting low levels of DHEA to problems associated with aging, I suggest that all people over age 40 begin DHEA therapy. For most people, the starting dose of DHEA is between 15–200 mgs, taken in one daily dose. Many studies have used a daily dose of 50 mg. Women tend to use the 7-keto DHEA form and men can use the regular DHEA form. WHY? 7-Keto DHEA is not converted into estrogen or testosterone, so it may be safely taken by women with hormone-dependent diseases like breast cancer.

# Thread: Frequency of Cold Thermogenesis

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### laertes — 2012-04-06T17:38:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-12151>

Hi, y'all.

I wanted to ask, how often do you do CT?

Anyone knows what the optimal frequency for CT is? (I am aware that the answer is most likely

"Just stay in the cold water the hole time")

Sorry if I asked something that could have been easily searched.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-06T17:41:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-12152>

It depends upon your goals and your level of disease your currently have.

### laertes — 2012-04-06T17:50:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-12157>

Let's assume I'm an average diseased guy. Or better. What are the parameters? The sicker I am, the more frequent and longer?

### MonteD — 2012-04-06T18:35:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-12173>

Hour in the morning. Hour in the evening.

### Pamela — 2012-04-07T07:28:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-12399>

I don't have any major illnesses but I am really wanting to maintain my weight loss from HCG as well as just an overall feeling of well being. I also would like to improve sleep quality. I've been CTing 3 times a week and got it up to 45 minutes after a good high fat breakfast. I'll continue with this and maybe add one additional day.

### agatha — 2012-04-07T15:45:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-12433>

I would like to kinow whether it's better to do 10 minutes every day or 20 minutes every two days. If I do the longer time it takes me two days to feel recovered from the detox effects.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-07T15:54:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-12435>

Right now I'm doing 10-15 minutes cold shower in the morning. 60-90 minutes of ice packs in the evening. and every few days 40 minutes immersion bath in stead of the evening ice packs. Seems to be working for me so far.

But..... I would like to know - is it more effective to become cold adapted using frequency or length of time? For instance - would it be better to do one big 120 minutes therapy, or do the exact same therapy 12 times throughout the day for 10 minutes each?

I'm thinking the answer is - we just don't know?

### MonteD — 2012-04-08T07:17:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-12601>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;12504 wrote:** Right now I'm doing 10-15 minutes cold shower in the morning. 60-90 minutes of ice packs in the evening. and every few days 40 minutes immersion bath in stead of the evening ice packs. Seems to be working for me so far.
>
>
>
> But..... I would like to know - is it more effective to become cold adapted using frequency or length of time? For instance - would it be better to do one big 120 minutes therapy, or do the exact same therapy 12 times throughout the day for 10 minutes each?
>
>
>
> I'm thinking the answer is - we just don't know?
>
>
> [http://freetheanimal.com/2012/04/jack-kruse-neurosurgeon-leptin-reset-and-cold-thermogenesis-controvery.html#comment-124377](http://http://freetheanimal.com/2012/04/jack-kruse-neurosurgeon-leptin-reset-and-cold-thermogenesis-controvery.html#comment-124377)
>
>
>
> From the above linked comment:
>
>
>
> "Total time in ice baths is not the key. Cold stress activates the basic genetic protective pathways, just like the biological stress of lifting weights causes muscle to grow in response."

### Dali Dula — 2012-04-08T15:11:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-12632>

I have been doing ten + min full immersion every morning for the past two weeks in a pond that is currently at 45 deg. I also do cold shower 52 deg for about ten min in evening. Definitely seeing results from this. The most ripped in my 51 yr life.The need for food and exercise have decreased. Energy level way up. Feel great. Got labs back recently and except for some elevated liver enzymes they are great. Trigs @ 30!

### Arthur Morris — 2012-06-16T21:43:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-31593>

Newbie question: what is HCG?

thanks and later and love, artie

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-16T21:47:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frequency-of-cold-thermogenesis.697/post-31595>

HCG is a hormone people use to lose weight. In humans it is the hormone the placenta uses to fat a baby in the last trimester to get it ready for infant brain growth

# Thread: Hey Y'all from Southern Appalachia / Western North Carolina !
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-southern-appalachia-western-north-carolina.711/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### wyldeman — 2012-04-07T05:20:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-southern-appalachia-western-north-carolina.711/post-12370>

I have been "mostly" paleo for 7+ years.

Prior to that I had eaten meat again for 7 years after a 10 year period of being a vegetarian (with 2 of those being vegan).

So, when I went paleo it was the first time in my life that I didn't have bread at nearly every meal (other than when I was a baby, of course). Instantly, I felt a difference. Between going grain free and dairy free I noticed the film in my mouth disappear...and I had thought that was just normal for being human, I had no idea it was food related. Digestion improved dramatically. My mind became clearer and my skin too.

When I stay paleo, and am left to my own devices, I go keto and feel great. The stress that eating paleo causes in relationships often led me to 'stray'. People seem to love the idea but are very addicted to foods in the SAD.

I have decided that I can not let that stress effect my life and my health.

Since 1/1/12, after finding Dr. Kruse and the monster MDA thread on or about 12/14/11 and then finishing that thread soon after Christmas, I began the Leptin Reset. I had a significant amount of body fat in my face, back, arms, thighs, feet and waist.

I went to the post Leptin protocol around 3/15.

I am VERY interested in and dedicated to OPTIMAL !

I currently eat only wild caught fish (salmon, flounder, sardines and whiting) and local, grass finished beef and beef liver ( mostly ground, but the occasional roast and heart. The liver I have at least 2x/week). Occasional pastured pork (same farm) and I think I've had chicken only once since January. I eat pastured eggs, my homemade sauerkraut, kale, spring salad mix, broccoli, cauliflower and occasionally collards. Was doing bone broth regularly, but do not have access right now to a crock pot. I drink about a quart of green tea/white tea a day, otherwise I drink water.

I currently supplement with D3, fish oil, C, K2 (Carlson's MK4), a B complex, zinc, NAC, Milk Thistle and am going to try some Melatonin tonight for the first time. I eat 1/2 oz. of 100% cacao Bakers Chocolate with a brazil nut twice a day. Just upped my kombu intake from every other day to every day...a 3" x3" piece uncooked. I also do epsom salt baths every few days.

Up until around 2/15 I was doing a great job with meal timing, sun on my eyes at sunrise, dark evenings, and getting to sleep early. Tried CT for the first time around that date. Unfortunately that was the same day that my living situation changed suddenly. I have been doing my best and have been able to eat the same foods, but sleep has been impacted horribly (can not get anywhere near a full night's rest, dark is out of the question). Major stress.

Spent my first two weeks of life in an incubator as I was born jaundiced.

Was a Paramedic, then a Firefighter/Paramedic for about 15 years. I feel the stress of the job and the continual shift work and constantly interrupted sleep took a serious toll on my health that I am still recovering from. Couple that with the decade of vegetarian/vegan eating and I feel my adrenals and gut are probably pretty shot.

Presently I am a Personal Trainer who values joint mobility and longevity, I teach outdoor/primitive skills and I teach self defense ( a way that emphasizes breath, structure, movement and relaxation--controlled tension--). My passion is threefold: as a student, as an empowering educator and I LOVE using and caring for my body.

I am very grateful for the ideas of Dr. Kruse, the discussion it fosters and the support of others who strive for optimal. I have never felt better in my adult life. Period. This is freedom.
