### Glamorama — 2012-04-30T06:08:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glamoramas-food-in-photos.727/post-20007>

Recipe for meatza:

1/2 cup coconut flour or finely desiccated coconut

1-3 tsp salt (adjust for taste)

1 tsp oregano

1 tsp coarse ground pepper

2 lbs. ground beef

2 eggs

Pizza-sauce:

Tomato paste

Lots of EVOO

3 cloves of finely chopped garlic

1/2 tbs oregano

(mix together till the sauce is smooth and even)

Preheat your oven to 450 degrees F/225 degrees Celsius.

2\. Line your pan with the foil.

Use the original recipe from here: [http://www.antihousewife.com/2010/10/meatza/](http://http://www.antihousewife.com/2010/10/meatza/) to see directions for how to make it. I amended the meatza-base recipe quite a bit from hers though.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-30T19:12:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glamoramas-food-in-photos.727/post-20110>

So Glam you have no problem with the cheese, tomatoes or eggs with your leaky gut? I'm still trying to figure out what I can/ can't have! My diet is so very limited due to oxalate issues already, so I'd like to add in anything I can. My veggies right now are pretty much limited to kale and cabbage. The occasional tomato and adding in a little dairy would be nice!

### Glamorama — 2012-05-01T00:43:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glamoramas-food-in-photos.727/post-20218>

> **BJK77;20183 wrote:** So Glam you have no problem with the cheese, tomatoes or eggs with your leaky gut? I'm still trying to figure out what I can/ can't have! My diet is so very limited due to oxalate issues already, so I'd like to add in anything I can. My veggies right now are pretty much limited to kale and cabbage. The occasional tomato and adding in a little dairy would be nice!

No issues with anything. If I was to have dairy every day I would feel it, I'd bloat and feel a bit sick. A tiny bit once to twice a week seems to work. Tomatoes never caused n issue. Eggs, more than 2 in particular, used to cause gut ache, but not anymore. I've been working on healing my gut since July 2012. Took about 8 months!

### Glamorama — 2012-05-22T05:51:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glamoramas-food-in-photos.727/post-26238>

Hahahah you girls crack me up! Sorry, I guess I like to hear the begging from the masses. LOL

Here you go, primal choc fudge recipe:

‎1 bar of Lindt 85% (even better, 90% if you can find it), 200 grams raw cocoa butter, some ghee, 4 tbs raw cacao powder, 1/4 tsp stevia, sprinkle of sea salt, 1 tsp cinnamon. Let it all melt over how water bath in a bowl, stir, pour in ca 50 grams of coconut flour, then pour into greaseproof tray, let it set in fridge till firm. I gave it 2 hours, and that left it slightly gooey in the middle, which was good. You can also use almond or hazelnut-meal if you want a praline flavour, or add vanilla, or peppermint etc etc.

### Glamorama — 2012-05-22T05:52:26+0300

I replaced coconut nectar with stevia. Delish, zero sugar, zero gluten or dairy. You can cook them in butter if dairy isn't an issue.

# Thread: Vitamin c and ketogenic diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 18

### agatha — 2012-04-08T13:57:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12628>

Where do we get Vitamin C on the ketogenic diet?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-08T16:06:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12637>

from the seafood and from veggies.....that is why I eat skin of fish, some eyeballs, and heads etc. I eat the exoskeleton of shrimp too. I learned this all from reading about how the Inuit did it back in the day.

### primat — 2012-04-08T17:39:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12683>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12695>

> **primat;12752 wrote:** Would love some tips on how to eat shrimp shells

aren\`t they used to make broth?

### ginastarke — 2012-04-08T18:25:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12715>

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-08T18:50:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12722>

> **Jack Kruse;12706 wrote:** from the seafood and from veggies.....that is why I eat skin of fish, some eyeballs, and heads etc. I eat the exoskeleton of shrimp too. I learned this all from reading about how the Inuit did it back in the day.

any special prep to make le shells more palatable? Or just start and it's not so bad. Unless of course you buy into the 'unpalatability theory'

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-08T18:55:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12725>

> **primat;12752 wrote:** Would love some tips on how to eat shrimp shells

I make a special broth for AI patients.....using shrimp exoskeleton I put them in a broth and boil them down for ten to 20 hours in a crock pot......and then use an immersion blender to pulverize them and i use this to be the base of many of my sauces for fish.......it is awesome healthy. I make one batch and it lasts for weeks. I may put it in the summer edition of the cookbook. The bone broth is already in the winter edition

### agatha — 2012-04-08T20:27:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12748>

Thanks Jack. The shrimp broth is good - I already do this and use it as a tasty stock base for fish curries.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-09T00:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12808>

> **Cavemam;12791 wrote:** any special prep to make le shells more palatable? Or just start and it's not so bad. Unless of course you buy into the 'unpalatability theory'

When I was at university, I worked at an authentic Japanese restaurant. While we'd serve the shrimp 'bodies' to the customers, the chefs would take the heads and shells for us staff and fry them up in a wok with spices. They were crunchy and salty and delicious. I totally think you could do this with keto/paleo friendly oils and herbs.

### David — 2012-04-09T16:01:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-12954>

> **Jack Kruse;12794 wrote:** I make a special broth for AI patients.....using shrimp exoskeleton I put them in a broth and boil them down for ten to 20 hours in a crock pot......and then use an immersion blender to pulverize them and i use this to be the base of many of my sauces for fish.......it is awesome healthy. I make one batch and it lasts for weeks. I may put it in the summer edition of the cookbook. The bone broth is already in the winter edition

Can't wait for the next edition to the cookbook.  I always do this stock in the crock pot, if i can plan ahead. You can always save any seafood shells such as crab, lobster, shrimp, even fish bones and fish heads (without gills) in the freezer until you need them for a really hearty fish broth. But when you need a cup or so of fish stock for a quick recipe, you can simmer shrimp shells in a little water for 15 minutes or so and you get a lovely pink broth. Just barely cover the shells with water. I always include a bit of onion, celery and carrot, as well as some seasoning and herbs de Provence. It's a good quick soup, too.

### Glamazon — 2012-04-13T05:55:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-ketogenic-diet.728/post-14588>

There's a lot of vit c content in offal. [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vitamin\_C#Animal\_sources](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vitamin_C#Animal_sources)

The article also mentions in humans: Although the body's maximal store of vitamin C is largely determined by the renal threshold for blood, there are many tissues that maintain vitamin C concentrations far higher than in blood. Biological tissues that accumulate over 100 times the level in blood plasma of vitamin C are the adrenal glands, pituitary, thymus, corpus luteum, and retina.\[41\] Those with 10 to 50 times the concentration present in blood plasma include the brain, spleen, lung, testicle, lymph nodes, liver, thyroid, small intestinal mucosa, leukocytes, pancreas, kidney and salivary glands.

When we are under stress vit c stores become depleted.

You can dose yourself with vit c to bowel tolerance. When diarrhea kicks in, back off on the dosage.

[http://www.doctoryourself.com/titration.html](http://http://www.doctoryourself.com/titration.html)

# Thread: CT for hashimoto's? (Scared.)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/>

**Posts:** 42

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-08T16:44:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-12659>

I'm wondering about how CT will work without thyroid or - in my case - with a severely damaged one and high antibodies.

I'm quite wary on how my thyroid (or thyroid meds dosage) will keep up with the increased demand. I've done cold baths in my youth and then I'd afterwards get a heat rush, but now I stay cold for a long time, and sometimes my basal temperature falls as well, and I'm just plain scared that I'll bring on my hypo-stuff.

Any thoughts/experiences?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-08T17:02:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-12663>

I have Hashis and have been doing the CT since Feb 12. I only have TPO antibodies on the last lab a year ago.

I worked my way up to 45 min cold baths in 52-54 water, 5-6 days a week. I keep my feet and hands out because I have Reynauds. I have more energy and sleep wonderfully. I also do spot CT with ice packs if I am just sitting around.

I just switched my thyroid meds from NDT to Cytomel, T3 only due to bad ratio of rT3 and desire to finish healing the adrenals. I actually feel pretty good on the Cytomel although trying to keep myself slightly hypo while the remainder of the T4 from the NDT converts to T3. This will keep me from going hyper. That is just my n=1.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-08T17:04:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-12665>

There is simply no better treatment I have found honestly

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-08T17:15:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-12669>

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-08T18:21:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-12711>

I copied the paragraphs below this from the doc's CT6 blog. It briefly explains that the thyroid is not even needed if you are doing CT. The whole blog is a biggie!

THE COLD ADAPTED HUMAN THYROID FXN: does not bother with T3 at all. Why? When you are supremely LS by cold you go straight the the source, the hypothalamus and make TRH from the brain. The brain controls all thyroid function in cold…….forget the Moose thyroid.

SKEPTIC BOMB: You bypass all hormones and TSH too. TRH drives the whole show. The brain is completely in control and it up regualtes fat burning everywhere. This is how the Ancient Pathway lights your pilot light. The warm adapted human always complains about the cold and always feels cold……the cold adapted on is always pink to cherry in cold radiating heat like a furnace. You can thank TRH for this. This is does not even require a thyroid gland either. Is not life grand in the cold, folks?

### AKMan — 2012-04-08T18:22:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-12712>

I saw Dr. K give some advice on a forum comment concerning hypothyroid and CT. He said to continue with the meds, but test frequently if you are titrating your dose down.

I'm working on my own bio-hack in this area as well. I've been hypothyroid (non-hashi's) for nearly 10 years taking Synthroid. Last summer, after starting a strict paleo diet, I stopped my Synthroid. Several months later my TSH had climbed out of the reference range and T3/T4 had bottomed out. I started back the Synthroid for about 30 days, got my TSH/T3/T4 all well in the normal range and stopped again on March 12th, 2012. The only difference is I have been CT'ing this time. I will retest in 2 weeks (6 week period) to see where my thyroid labs are.

One thing I can report at the 4 week point, though. My feet had always been cold when not taking Synthroid--my first indicator I was hypothyroid 10 years ago, and last winter when my TSH was raising. Even with Synthroid, my feet have never felt warm, and I always wore heavy socks and boots when outside. This go-around, I have been walking barefoot in the snow every day. My toes are actually warm to the touch now. They used to be like blocks of ice. Yesterday was my longest snow-walk...I walked about 15 minutes in the snow, then put on a pair of crocs and spent another 30-45 minutes outside in the snow with minimal foot warmth. Last night before bed, my toes were actually radiating heat. Whether this indicates a re-awakening of thyroid function will be left to the labs in 2 weeks, but I never thought in my wildest dreams I would have warm toes again.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-08T19:23:55+0300

yes I have......and It occurs because many of them are eating a warm adapted diet at precisely the wrong time......Inuits ate carbs when they were available and so should we. We adapted to the warm. this is where a thyroid comes in......we need it for that. If you eat a AI paleo diet you should be covered in spring and summer. Hashi motos is still an Autoimmune disease caused by a zonulin issue......primates don't get this......wHY? they are not evolved to cold climates.....Humans are the only mammals with zonulin......this was an evolutionary trait that shortened our guts to eating protein and fat mostly.......to fuel our brain. As we did we migrated out of africa.......and back to cold. And then we re adapted. We can use both pathways if we eat based upon circadian rhythms ....one problem.......that big brain has allowed us to break the rules of Mother Nature.......AI is the result. Got it.

I've had Hashi's for 11 years. I've tried very low carb diets in the past, only to be derailed by feeling absolutely awful around a week into it. I'm very aware of what I feel like without meds (I take Synthroid and Cytomel) and its exactly how Ive felt without enough carbs.

I've doing a leptin reset for the past three weeks, and I use CT at least every other day. I can honestly say that I really feel fine, which amazes me. This is all completely anecdotal, of course, and I don't have labs to back this up. ### Kiera2 — 2012-04-09T04:07:03+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T04:29:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-12879>

So far I can tell that Dr. Kruse believes this but seems to be unsure of the reason or is saving the explanation - maybe it ties into his factor x. This is just based on lack of a biologic explanation.

I'm am into my 5th day of taking no Cytomel (t3) going in with 0 T4 in my system. I'm not one who know's "instantly" if I'm hypothyroid but can usually tell when headaches come on. My research indicates BAT uses T3 like crazy, so I should be (have been) depleted of it fairly quickly. I'm feeling good with the exception of a few unexplained dizzy spells when getting up from a sitting/laying position, which I'm not thinking is probably related. I will report back longer term - I won't have labs done for another month or so to know what those say for reference.

Before CT I tended to always have a high TSH even if my free T3 levels came in normal. When my doctor and I found/understood elevated rT3 and corrected with T3 only medication to clear it out (rT3 is made from T4 like T3 is), this seemed to resolve. I've wondered how long I've overmedicated feeding rT3, and what this was doing to my body. I'm still working to resolve a "dangerously" high hs-crp and have wondered if this isn't the source. However, the CRP did not go down with the lowered rT3 (although I subsequently found out that CT may have re-elevated my CRP...). So AKman, many thyroid meds are dirt cheap but I understand you desire to use only if required.

### agatha — 2012-04-09T14:10:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-12937>

There is an awesome article on CT posted by KiwiLauren on page 2 of the What body parts are you prioritising thread (sorry don't know how to paste it here). In the section entitled BAT as a Secretory Organ there is a section on T3. It says that BAT converts T4 into T3 during cold exposure. So theoretically, if we can increase our BAT stores and do cold exposure we can increase our T3 levels ( presumably assuming adequate levels of T4).

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T15:13:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-12945>

Yes, I know that one. But it still depends on adequate levels of thyroid hormone. Either t3 or t4 for conversion to t3. All theories I've been able to find just seem to rearrange the balance of these hormones and relies on having a working thyroid (or huge t4 depots in circulation).

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-04-12T17:33:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-14236>

I just happen to be interested on CT for Hashimoto's for a friend.

> **smgj;13014 wrote:** All theories I've been able to find just seem to rearrange the balance of these hormones and relies on having a working thyroid (or huge t4 depots in circulation).

I know the study has been done in rats, not in humans, but they are eutherian none the less. My apologies if it has been already mentioned elsewhere. [_Mitochondrial respiration in muscle and liver from cold-acclimated hypothyroid rats_](http://http://www.jappl.org/content/95/4/1584.full):

After thyroid ablation, NST \[nonshivering thermogenesis\] replaced thyroid-induced thermogenesis, and normal body temperature was maintained. These lifesaving effects of the sympathetic system, previously unknown in tissues of cold-acclimated hypothyroid rats except BAT, appear to result from an integrated operation from multiple organ systems aimed at preserving normal body temperature when the supply of thyroid hormones to tissues has ceased and body temperature falls.​

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T19:02:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-14277>

> [AndrÃ©s said:](/goto/post?id=0)
>
> I know the study has been done in rats, not in humans, but they are eutherian none the less. QUOTE\]
>
>
>
> Andres - Awesome contribution! I have never seen this before and seems to justify what Dr. K. has been saying. In the citations section of this study you posted, I found another study,[http://endo.endojournals.org/content/149/12/6262.full](http://http://endo.endojournals.org/content/149/12/6262.full) done on rabbits. Rabbits, unlike rats, have very little BAT, and they also were able to survive without thyroids. In the rat study, the only way hypothyroid rats could survive in the cold is if they were cold adapted _before_the hypothyroidism was induced. In the rabbit study, they could survive the cold even though not cold adapted.
>
>
>
> As you said, we are not rats (or rabbits) but studies like this make you think!
>
>
>
> In these studies, they used 40 degrees F as the cold environment. Since rats and rabbits are covered in fur, I wonder what that equates to in human terms? I would guess mid-50's.

### Pardus — 2012-04-12T21:29:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-14354>

Excellent finds, Andres and AKman.

It seems that mammals have various ways of improving cold tolerance, even with hypothyroidism. (BAT, conversion of T4 to T3, thermogenesis from muscle tissue)

I really wish there were some more long term studies done on this. Will prolonged cold exposure actually correct existing hypothyroidism and auto-immune thyroid issues, even once the animals return to a warm climate?

There is no doubt that these things show some improvement DURING cold exposure, but most humans will tend to spend most of their time in a warmer environment, only doing CT like 1 hr cold baths daily (maybe 2 hours eventually?)... And maybe add another 2 hours or so of air conditioning, cool packs, cold walks, etc... The rest of the 20-21 hours will be spent in a warm environment at room temperature... Will we have to live in refrigerated environments to 'maintain' our thyroids?

I'm optimistic about this, but unfortunately, although I have seen an increase in cold tolerance DURING CT, yet my hands and feet are still freezing cold 90% of the rest of the day, only warming up for an hour or so after a meal. To all the hypo/hashi/auto-immune crowd out there, keep us updated on your progress.

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T21:38:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-14358>

> **Pardus;14423 wrote:** Excellent finds, Andres and AKman.
>
>
>
> It seems that mammals have various ways of improving cold tolerance, even with hypothyroidism. (BAT, conversion of T4 to T3, thermogenesis from muscle tissue)
>
>
>
> I really wish there were some more long term studies done on this. Will prolonged cold exposure actually correct existing hypothyroidism and auto-immune thyroid issues, even once the animals return to a warm climate?
>
>
>
> There is no doubt that these things show some improvement DURING cold exposure, but most humans will tend to spend most of their time in a warmer environment, only doing CT like 1 hr cold baths daily (maybe 2 hours eventually?)... And maybe add another 2 hours or so of air conditioning, cool packs, cold walks, etc...
>
>
>
> I'm optimistic about this, but unfortunately, although I have seen an increase in cold tolerance DURING CT, yet my hands and feet are still freezing cold 90% of the rest of the day, only warming up for an hour or so after a meal. To all the hypo/hashi/auto-immune crowd out there, keep us updated on your progress.

I was very happy to see studies on hypothyroid animals in cold. I looked hard and couldn't find any. Thanks to Andres for finding this!

One thing that bothers me when applying this to humans. Look at a list of common hypothyroid symptoms:

Fatigue

Sluggishness

Increased sensitivity to cold

Constipation

Pale, dry skin

A puffy face

Hoarse voice

An elevated blood cholesterol level

Unexplained weight gain

Muscle aches, tenderness and stiffness

Pain, stiffness or swelling in your joints

Muscle weakness

Heavier than normal menstrual periods

Brittle fingernails and hair

Depression

None of the common symptoms are "death in less than 10 days at 39 degrees" or "Failure to maintain high enough core temp for life"

I don't think the rats and rabbits were tested for brittle nails or depression, so maybe there's more to the thyroid story than just being able to maintain a core body temp. A big problem some have with Synthroid is that it is just T4, and fake at that. Dessicated pig thyroid is favored as it contains T1, T2, T3, and T4. If our body doesn't produce T4, you can't have the other T's. Just because the body figured a way to maintain core body temp without a thyroid doesn't mean it's optimal to live without thyroid hormones even if cold adapted.

\*no excuse for skipping the cold baths... \*

[http://thyroidbook.com/blog/nitric-oxide-modulation-for-autoimmune-disease/](http://http://thyroidbook.com/blog/nitric-oxide-modulation-for-autoimmune-disease/)

_"This scientifically developed formula provides key nutrients that are used in the system for the expression of eNOS and nNOS, and dampening the expression of iNOS. It further supports healthy peripheral blood flow to the brain, hands, feet, and sexual organs."_

### hazyjane — 2012-04-15T07:15:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-hashimotos-scared.729/post-15245>

> **Jack Kruse;15294 wrote:** L-Arginine and ornathine will do this......so will resveratrol.

There's a reason it doesn't contain arginine:

_"Unfortunately, arginine may also increase a very pro-inflammatory and damaging form of nitric oxide called inducible nitric oxide. You want to boost the endothelial but not the inducible nitric oxide.

"Endothelial nitric oxide is found in the lining of blood vessels. It aids in tissue recovery and regeneration, enhances blood flow, dissolves plaques, and dilates blood vessels. One thing that dramatically activates endothelial nitric oxide is exercise. When you exercise, the increase in blood flow turns on the endothelial nitric oxide system, which helps dissolve plaque in the arteries.

Unfortunately, autoimmune disease often compromises this system, thus hindering the delivery of blood to body tissue.\[8\] This not only makes body tissue, such as the thyroid gland, more vulnerable to inflammation and destruction, but it also makes it more difficult for these tissues to recover and heal.

A weak endothelial nitric oxide system helps explain cold hands and feet, the loss of hair, weak nails prone to fungal infections, and other symptoms frequently found in conjunction with autoimmune diseases."_

# Thread: What is the hardest part for you?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 45
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-08T17:21:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12673>

It seems like the majority of posters that aren't yet fully adapted struggle the most with recovery after CT. I find myself recovering much more quickly than I had been, but the baths are still torturous for me! I'm having trouble staying in the tub at 53-55 degrees for more than 20-30 minutes. I've started shivering again and am still finding it to be super unpleasant . . . this is after 5+ weeks of CT.

Fortunately the recovery isn't too painful anymore and I'm usually fine within 30 minutes or so.

I still find myself with extremely cold feet throughout the day too, which makes it even harder to want to do CT, but I'm good at motivating myself to get in anyway.

Just curious if anyone else is struggling more with the actual CT itself than with the recovery. Wouldn't surprise me if I'm the only one . . . my doctor says I'm an "oddball" and "complex case!" LOL!! I seem to always have reactions opposite of those that are anticipated.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-08T17:40:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12686>

At the moment, the hardest part for me is actually being able to have a cold soak. Not only am I finding it difficult to face, but I am also finding it very difficult to fit into my life during the Easter break with visitors and family around! I've found that if I CT after about 3.00 p.m., my recovery time is very long, and I do not sleep. The last good soak I had, my recovery time was really long, and I think a lot of this came from not having been able to do a daily soak.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-08T17:45:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12690>

My wife thinks I'm a freak doing this. So trying to find the time for a cold shower, immersion bath, and walking around the with ice packs wrapped to a compression shirt - is the most difficult part. I was having issues with recovery after the immersion - but my body rapidly comes back up to temp now. So just finding the time is the biggest issue.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-08T17:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12702>

CT around TOM (time of month) is much more difficult to do, I still do it but cannot go as long or as cold

### AKMan — 2012-04-08T18:08:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12706>

> **BJK77;12742 wrote:** It seems like the majority of posters that aren't yet fully adapted struggle the most with recovery after CT. I find myself recovering much more quickly than I had been, but the baths are still torturous for me! I'm having trouble staying in the tub at 53-55 degrees for more than 20-30 minutes. I've started shivering again and am still finding it to be super unpleasant . . . this is after 5+ weeks of CT.
>
>
>
> Fortunately the recovery isn't too painful anymore and I'm usually fine within 30 minutes or so.
>
>
>
> I still find myself with extremely cold feet throughout the day too, which makes it even harder to want to do CT, but I'm good at motivating myself to get in anyway.
>
>
>
> Just curious if anyone else is struggling more with the actual CT itself than with the recovery. Wouldn't surprise me if I'm the only one . . . my doctor says I'm an "oddball" and "complex case!" LOL!! I seem to always have reactions opposite of those that are anticipated.

If you are miserable, you won't keep it up for long, just human nature. One person who is quite knowledgeable in this field, Ray Cronise ([www.hypothermics.com](http://http://www.hypothermics.com)) says you don't need to be cold and miserable for CT to work. He recommends 80+ degree baths/showers, turning the thermostat down a few degrees, not wearing a jacket on chilly mornings, and other simple steps you can take to be colder than normal without being miserable. Of course, as Dr. K. would say, maybe that's settling for a 'B' when an 'A' is available. But, we don't all get 'A's' our first time taking advanced quantum physics, so maybe shoot for a 'C' this semester and work your way up to that 'A'.

I really hate seeing people report bad experiences with CT because I feel so strongly about it's potential benefits. There are no other websites with normal people doing this and reporting results. I really, really hope this forum keeps growing and people keep trying different things and reporting their results.

Good luck!

### Birdy — 2012-04-08T18:19:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12709>

I am just finding cold tubs an insurmountable challenge. All I have done with them is a lukewarm soak, but with evaporative cooling, I soon get to shivering and it is very difficult to warm certain parts up. OTOH, air soaks are miserable for only 30 seconds, and then I get into fight mode, start warming up, and feeling fine. I think I am becoming acclimated to it because I am running around without lots of clothing, while other people are bundled up. And yea, not as much nipple message (and no, that was not an autocorrect...)

### vkiernan — 2012-04-09T01:16:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12814>

The hardest part of CT for me is the after event of shivering. Sitting in the cold water for 30-45 min. is a breeze. It is the uncontrollable shivering that I hate. I now clean the kitchen, vacuum, a few squats or anything to just move and get things going right or wrong. Within a good half hour I am fairly back to normal and can go about my day. The shivering really is miserable though.

### caroline — 2012-04-09T01:31:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12819>

I totally agree with moving after C/T. When I get out of the tub or shower I run in place for a few minutes and walk around naked for a bit. It makes all the difference when you get busy. I then put on a Terry robe for awhile

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T01:40:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12823>

> **BJK77;12742 wrote:** It seems like the majority of posters that aren't yet fully adapted struggle the most with recovery after CT. I find myself recovering much more quickly than I had been, but the baths are still torturous for me! I'm having trouble staying in the tub at 53-55 degrees for more than 20-30 minutes. I've started shivering again and am still finding it to be super unpleasant . . . this is after 5+ weeks of CT.
>
>
>
> Fortunately the recovery isn't too painful anymore and I'm usually fine within 30 minutes or so.
>
>
>
> I still find myself with extremely cold feet throughout the day too, which makes it even harder to want to do CT, but I'm good at motivating myself to get in anyway.
>
>
>
> Just curious if anyone else is struggling more with the actual CT itself than with the recovery. Wouldn't surprise me if I'm the only one . . . my doctor says I'm an "oddball" and "complex case!" LOL!! I seem to always have reactions opposite of those that are anticipated.

So this was my problem too, I would start shivering within 10 mins, and just couldn't go longer than 20 - and being cold and miserable after for a long time.

I started heating the bathroom, and taking measures to make sure I didn't get too "cold" I have two space heaters in the bath and it's HOT in there, wool socks on my feet and a thick hat on my head. That did the trick. First time I was able to go 25 mins without shivering, and now I'm up to 45 mins - all in a weeks timeframe. Also, I think I made some weight loss progress since starting to do that. I definitely am feeling peppy and more energetic. Did early eve CT last night, 55 mins, and was spontaneously jumping, doing pushups and running in place after to heat my body to see if it helped the warm up. The point was CT was energy-making instead of a drudgery as it had been.

I'm on about the 4th week., seeing detox symptoms, but weight wasn't moving much until the last week or so after starting the longer baths. . . so I think the heated room isn't hurting too much.

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-09T02:22:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12835>

I don't know how anyone can jump into 55F water right away so I'm taking it slow. I started with 70F shower/bath, they were not very challenging and actually quite stimulating. Now I have had 11 cold showers/baths. When I started I could only stay in for 20 minutes, now I can stay in for 40 minutes. The lowest temperature I got to was 61F, today it was at 66F. I plan to get to 62 - 64F next week. I don't really shiver much, and warm up quite easily afterward.

Now I start with bath, finish up with a cold shower. I don't see much of detox, maybe because I'm quite healthy and active to start with. Although I do notice that I get emotional more easily - I have cried at every movie I watched lately.

I do have quite a few positive effects too... sex drive, well I've got used to it , smooth hair, and elastic youthful looking skin, don't feel cold at all, except for when I was in the water and right after... so I believe in slow and steady progress. Remember when the winter comes in nature, it also arrives gradually.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-09T02:26:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12838>

I agree, don't let CT be a miserable experience. I look forward to it.

Don't tell anyone, but my mind wandered in church today and I got to thinking about CT... When my body started heating up, I had to start paying attention to keep from sweating!

### cantweight — 2012-04-09T03:04:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12849>

My trickiest part is my calf muscles about 20-30 after CT. They cramp up like mad! I am stopped dead when I try to walk because they are flexed rock hard.

I'm assuming I need to add some potassium since bananas and potatoes have been off the menu for weeks now.

### Destiny — 2012-04-09T03:05:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12850>

My CT was going well for a few weeks. Then, I got violently sick with vomiting and runs when I took a bath at 55 F. I wish I knew if that was related to CT or just a virus. Now, I am afraid to push it to 55F just in case I have a similar experience.

I'll have to check into Ray Cronise. I really haven't read much on his theories re: CT. You are absolutely right though that human nature will have us rejecting or abandoning things we don't enjoy. I'm afraid it's already happening and I'm trying to do whatever I can to stop it. I didn't enjoy CT from day 1, but I at least felt like it was a necessary evil. I believed in it and was so desperate for healing that I was always motivated to get in the tub, but I think that feeling is fading somewhat - probably a combination of not really seeing any results and feeling like the CT is actually getting harder for some reason. I had hoped to be more cold adapted by now.

Just got out of a 25 minute bath (52 deg) though, so I haven't given up! Just wishing I were further along . . . and hoping I can keep my eye on the prize

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-09T03:36:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12863>

any temp below body temp allows for CT.....but the optimal range is 50-55 degrees and the best diet for it is a high fat diet moderate protein diet. When it is warm you can seasonally adjust. If your very ill CT needs to be a constant event until you reverse all you need to. Its not that hard to CT with AC and cold showers and all the things we can do......If you make it hard by your thoughts you will never succeed with it. Change your thoughts and change your life.

### Pamela — 2012-04-09T03:56:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12870>

I think the high fat part is really important. Up until today I was having a high fat, meat, protein LeptinRX breakfast with Bulletproof coffee with oil and butter. Then I did the bath about an hour afterwards. Today I did the same thing but had cod for breakfast which is not a very fatty fish and I was way more uncomfortable and cold both in the bath and for a while afterwards. So my recommendation is not to skimp on the fat prior to the bath.

### Jim — 2012-04-09T03:57:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12871>

> **Destiny;12920 wrote:** I would also try magnesium malate.

I agree. I also put some Epsom salts in the water. I know absorption is not optimal given the vasoconstriction due to the cold however some is better than none.

### trish3 — 2012-04-09T04:21:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12877>

> **Pamela;12939 wrote:** I think the high fat part is really important. Up until today I was having a high fat, meat, protein LeptinRX breakfast with Bulletproof coffee with oil and butter. Then I did the bath about an hour afterwards. Today I did the same thing but had cod for breakfast which is not a very fatty fish and I was way more uncomfortable and cold both in the bath and for a while afterwards. So my recommendation is not to skimp on the fat prior to the bath.

I completely agree with this. I was very diligent about getting my fat in the first 2 weeks I did CT. I was eating bacon, sausage, and concentrating more on getting in my coconut oil. During that time I started at 71 F and managed to get to 50 F in seven days(after reading Dr K's admonition to not let my mind get in the way of what is right).

For the last two weeks I have become lazy: more leaner meat (pork loin, chicken, eggs), little to no CO. I have had an extremely difficult time getting comfortable after my 45 min CT @ 40F. The fat is the key. Also in the last two weeks my fatigue and freezing feet have returned, where in the initial 2 weeks I was feeling warmer during the day.

Dr K, if you have additional recommendations, I would love to hear them.

Keep it up everyone

### shilohman — 2012-04-09T04:46:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12884>

If CT is miserable for you,then you are pushing it too hard. Normally my baths are an hour and afterwards I feel invigorated and energized. I don't even feel the need to warm up afterwards. One time after an hours bath I spot iced and I really started to get cold. Obviously I was pushing it too hard. So I don't spot ice after a bath. The previous advice to go for a B or C instead of an A makes a lot of sense. Seems like to me that not warming up after CT makes the effects of the CT last longer.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-09T05:05:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12891>

BJK77, I started CT a week after Dr. K posted the protocol, just enough time to get a HS-CRP test. I went slow, but I'm now up to 45-60 minutes in a 50-55F bath. However, sometimes I don't feel like rolling over, so I don't. I do eat lots of fat.

I'm not sure about some people's aversion to the shivering afterwards, though. All I usually get is a gentle trembling, usually in my backbone area. It doesn't affect my speech, and usually isn't noticeable to anyone else. It doesn't really bother me.

Today, for the first time, the water actually started to feel warm, at times. I was so proud of myself! Then when I was getting out, I noticed my full glass of icewater. Oops, forgot!

### Glamorama — 2012-04-09T05:06:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12892>

I'm struggling with CT. I have no problems doing spot-CT daily but I struggle with the baths. I psych myself out of it if I don't do it daily. I've been sick with a nasty cough for a week so I hardly have the willpower till I feel better.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-09T05:30:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12903>

If it's any help, I started off with 65F water, which was gaspingly cold for me. I didn't do a second bath for almost a week, I just continued with my face (and arm) dunks. Then I felt like trying it again. I found that the same temp water was much easier. So I waited a couple days, and did a third bath, still at the same temp, just added 5 minutes or so each time. I found myself enjoying the baths, so it became an everyday thing. It was about 2-3 weeks before I lowered the temp down to 60F. Each time I lowered the temp, I reduced the time in the tub a bit, at least at first. Sometimes I skip a day for various reasons, but I really miss it.

### Guest — 2012-04-09T05:39:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12905>

It's me getting in my way. I need to take the self out of this and focus on what can be.

### agatha — 2012-04-09T14:36:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12940>

Getting into the bath is the worst part (although way easier than at first). Before getting in I say to myself in a loud voice 'JUST GET ON WITH IT'

### nonchalant — 2012-04-09T15:26:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-12947>

When I step into the bath, I think "Oooh, swimming pool on a sweltering day!" It always makes me smile.

### Mery — 2012-04-09T20:14:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-13049>

It has not been easy I’m on my 6th day of having cold baths and I think I’m going to fast, yesterday and today I did 40 minutes and I was shivering after 30 minutes so tomorrow I’m go back to 30 minutes. I didn’t put ice today and yesterday.

I prefer to do my bath at night because after my CB 60 F, I’m shivering so I go to bed fully clothed and at midnight I get hot, so I take everything as my body get warm.

I totally agree with Dr Jack to do CT one need determination and focus, some days i don’t feel like doing CB that side of me is the one who don’t like changes, but my positive side push me to do it and I’m glad I’ve done it.

I know if don’t try I’ll never know, so far I’ve seen good think like detox, my little finger was swollen for 2 days, i don’t get so bloated.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T20:22:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-13054>

I think to myself "this is way better than going to a 1.5 hr fitness bootcamp" like I used to do for health. Plus I can listen to podcasts or read this forum while there. Mucho better than the old days.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T20:41:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-13080>

I followed Cavemam's room heater advice yesterday and today, and also stopped getting into the bath whilst it was running, and it was surprisingly easy to get into a full tub and stay there for 25 minutes (could have stayed longer, but children needed attention). Warm-up time was shorter, too!

### Lyndra — 2012-04-09T22:24:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-13121>

I think I am finally starting to turn the corner on the cold baths after about 30 days now. I warm up sooner afterwards and I don't mind getting in. On some days, it actually feels good (maybe that's just my brain telling me it should feel good). I still can't put my arms in without big time shivering, so I still have a ways to go.

### nonchalant — 2013-11-22T16:43:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-101357>

So the difficulty some of us have in warming up after CT must be related to the difficulties triathlon participants experience after their swim, right? We are somewhat cold adapted, however, so the cold water doesn't present much of a problem. But it is after we get out, when our skin no longer senses the cold water, that we need to transition to a new energy source, and we aren't doing it efficiently. Ketosis must be the answer. Is insulin not working in temps under 62F the problem?

### Shijin13 — 2013-11-22T16:56:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-101360>

great insight Sally. I've found its harder for me to warm up when the water is above 50 deg. the closer to 40 deg it is the easier it is for me. I've been keeping the house at a cool 60 deg - and I'm cool but comfortable all day. I've had a hard time making the time while working from home this past month - btw work, my blog and such - that some days I look up and its time to pick the kids up and make dinner.... I'm going to go get in shortly. I'm looking fwd to the cold b/c its going to be a nice crisp 40 deg or so....

### nonchalant — 2013-11-22T17:15:51+0200

### SeaHorse — 2013-11-23T01:07:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-101411>

It's just been the last week or so that I've felt good with my CT...it's much colder now and I'm warming up well, all fall it was often miserable to get in the water and much harder to warm up. I was getting worried and a bit discouraged (not that I let on ) but now that we have ice on the pond everything is good again. I usually have a cold shower afterwards too...until the pond bottom clears up a little more I need a good wash after getting out!

### Danco3636 — 2013-11-23T01:24:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-101412>

Getting in the cold is ok... walking outside in the cold no shirt is ok... When I get into my now cold pool I am ok and go numb pretty quick and it becomes easy to stay in for a while. It is afterwards..... It just takes me a long while to completely warm up.... At some times I have had to take a warm shower or bath just so I can get back to focusing on work and things...... Does that go away after while.... I have been doing CT for some time now and eat fully 100% nutritional epi-paleo ketosis....

### Danco3636 — 2013-11-23T01:26:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-101413>

One thing I noticed is that often I do my CT in the am while in a fasted state..... It makes it tougher to warm up.... Now if I CT after a good size meal CT isn't as bad.

### caroline — 2013-11-23T01:36:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-101416>

JK has always said to eat lots of fat before C/T and drink 32oz ice water.......

### Josh — 2013-11-23T19:19:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-101479>

I just jumped into my now 48 F tub this morning on the deck and got out. My body immediately heated up and steam started rising off of me in the 45 degree rainy weather. So I thought why not get in again. When I got in, I felt good for about 30 seconds and then began shivering a lot. I am 5' 10", 150lbs without much fat. I am still learning what this all means. I will go back the book and the blog to see how to interpret this. I took a brief warm shower and have been just a little chilled since.

### Butters — 2013-12-13T14:30:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-103311>

The hardest part for me it to get under the shower/in the water. This is the greatest barrier, after that I'm mostly perfectly fine.

Questions:
1\. Jack said, that skin temperature must not fall under 50 degrees, but if my water only has 52 degrees this won't never happen won't it?

2\. Is it possible for very lean humans to cold adapt in the same way like humans with a higher body fat percentage? Is shivering normal in these lean individuals, because they can't form so much BAT? I don't know if I should continue despite shivering (often I start to shiver very soon but feel fine).

### Danco3636 — 2013-12-13T14:43:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-103312>

Good question. #2 I wonder as well. Does BAT form in one that is lean and perhaps even getting leaner?

### SeaHorse — 2013-12-13T15:17:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-103316>

Kevin Cotrell's guest post talked about this a bit...or Dr. Kruse commented on it...I can't remember....basically the better your body comp the more shivering and difficulty warming up you will have when you start....

My weight has been good for the last few years...I leaned out a bit more last winter, and I had a hard time warming up. This winter, after a not so great fall, I'm not having any issues warming up which makes me think my BAT has increased....and that there is a sweet spot for me with temperature. I'm not experiencing any Reynaud's with my hands or feet either as long as they are protected. That wasn't the case last winter, when they would go white after I got out of the pond using the same level of protection, I would shiver for hours afterwards and dealt with a lot of core temperature dropping...(I still loved it though)

Yesterday, the air was around 0 degrees and the pond was 33 degrees..it was sunny and calm so I stayed in for five minutes and had a cold shower afterwards....no shivering, no trouble warming up....THIS IS A BIG CHANGE, cold adaptation is an ongoing process clearly and in my case it might take another winter or two before I hit any kind of plateau. I still wear more clothes in the winter than a lot of you CTers....we do have a cold and drafty house, so that's nice

Anyway....my main point is that I don't think any of us are going to adapt in exactly the same way or time. Just keep going......

### Danco3636 — 2013-12-13T15:24:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-103317>

Thanks for your insights. ^^^^^

### wardog0351 — 2014-09-05T18:26:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-hardest-part-for-you.730/post-140009>

Quick NK question...I've been in NK for over a month with a macro ration of 30grams carbs/ 70 grams protein/ fat to satiety. My fasted morning BHB was 4.5 and CBG in the 70's(outside the therapeutic range), but I feel GREAT! I show gains in everything (gym, sleep, mental clarity). I raised my protein to 90 grams (just to see the effects) and noticed a decline in my BH to 2.0.

I understand whats happening, just looking for some additional insight.

# Thread: Hello from RI
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ri.733/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Charley — 2012-04-08T18:16:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ri.733/post-12708>

Hello,

New forum member here, coming out of lurkdom  I came across Dr. K's website while researching Multiple Sclerosis. I was diagnosed with MS Summer 2011. Worst summer of my life!!

I recently reviewed the lab tests my Neuro ran just prior to diagnosing me, and some things raise red flags for me that she didn't address...I am hoping to learn more here. There are many more labs that I would like to have done, but it's going to take me some time.

I am intrigued with CT as a tool to reverse neolithic disease. I believe it can help me stay in remission. I am trying to do everything I possibly can to prevent a relapse! I don't have any issues regarding weight. I have been following the protocol with some ups and downs....On my way to becoming OPTIMAL!

Charley

### Jim — 2012-04-08T18:24:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ri.733/post-12714>

Hi Charley!

Welcome. Glad to meet you.

Have you checked out Dr Terry Wahl's TEDx presentation yet? Pretty impressive. I understand that she is starting a research trial also. Here are links to her presentation and the trial.

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KLjgBLwH3Wc\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;KLjgBLwH3Wc\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KLjgBLwH3Wc\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;KLjgBLwH3Wc)

The breakdown of the Wahls trial is available for those with an interest in MS or paleo research.

[http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/results?term=Paleolithic+Diet](http://http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/results?term=Paleolithic+Diet)

It's number 8.

Good luck!

Jim

### Charley — 2012-04-09T07:59:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ri.733/post-12923>

Hi Jim.

Yes, I have been following Dr. Wahls since my dx. Her dietary recommendations make intuitive sense to me. I have been trying to incorporate her protocol into my diet as best I can. Although, I admit that it did take me several months to take the dietary changes seriously. I haven't experienced anywhere near the level of disability that she has. I plan on keeping it that way!

Thanks,

Charley

### Chrisgraff — 2012-04-09T18:54:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ri.733/post-13025>

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-11T23:50:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ri.733/post-13928>

# Thread: Have I had success? Question for experienced CTers.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-i-had-success-question-for-experienced-cters.737/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-09T05:00:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-i-had-success-question-for-experienced-cters.737/post-12889>

Hi Friends,

I have been doing spot ice regularly since March 22, 2012 and can easily do 50 minutes without going below 55F on the spot. I decided it was time to start the baths. I have taken three baths so far. My internal temperature dropped to 95.3F after 20 minutes in 71F water. I know kind of sad performance!

I had my third bath in 68F water and I raise the temperature of the tub water to 70F after 30 minutes. My internal temperature went from 96.8F to 98.6F! Normal body temperature is not "normal" for me. This is the first time my internal temperature has gone up during a cold treatment.

Is this what others have experienced? Should I expect to see more increases in body temperature after cold treatment?

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-09T05:07:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-i-had-success-question-for-experienced-cters.737/post-12893>

I started super slow at 80 deg. and went down one degree a day, although by accident it often ended up being a few degrees down. I found that each day even though the water was colder I felt warmer and more comfortable. Finally I just jumped down to 55 and then 50 and feel almost the same.

Just go slow and safe and know that it will all happen sooner than you might think and more comfortably too. I think your core temp going up is a great sign, and don't feel bad about not just diving into 50 deg. water at the start.

### shilohman — 2012-04-09T05:09:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-i-had-success-question-for-experienced-cters.737/post-12895>

>
>
>
> Is this what others have experienced? Should I expect to see more increases in body temperature after cold treatment?

How are you measuring your core temp? Did you take your temp several times to verify the 98.6?

I am taking my temperature orally. I use a basal thermometer which is an ultra sensitive thermometer that tracks the body's minutest temperature shifts.

### quelsen — 2012-04-09T16:43:19+0300

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-09T16:44:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-i-had-success-question-for-experienced-cters.737/post-12969>

> **PaleoMom;12962 wrote:** I started super slow at 80 deg. and went down one degree a day, although by accident it often ended up being a few degrees down. I found that each day even though the water was colder I felt warmer and more comfortable. Finally I just jumped down to 55 and then 50 and feel almost the same.
>
>
>
> Just go slow and safe and know that it will all happen sooner than you might think and more comfortably too. I think your core temp going up is a great sign, and don't feel bad about not just diving into 50 deg. water at the start.

Yes, I have noticed that too. I am finding that my cold tolerance is increasing surprising quickly. I am still feel "cold" and I am not radiating heat yet. I have a little talk with myself as I watch my internal temperature drop to borderline hypothermia. I say to myself: "cold is my friend" and "I am safe". It seems counter intuitive to get "cold" to get "warm" but at the same time makes sense to my right-brain kind of thinking.

In the last few days I have also increased my coconut cream and coconut oil intake to try to stoke my internal fire without the downsides of stimulants like coffee.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-09T16:47:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-i-had-success-question-for-experienced-cters.737/post-12971>

> **quelsen;13037 wrote:** mine raised then fell and has never come back up. have no idea why that is

Have you tried large doses of coconut cream or coconut oil? When I look back at my log this is the one thing I have changed in the last week. (Of course, I am very new to all this.)

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-09T17:04:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-i-had-success-question-for-experienced-cters.737/post-12982>

> **Jack Kruse;13036 wrote:** When cold adaptatipon begins for real you will see that you can raise your core temperature and you can directly alter your cortisol levels if you test for them as I do.......

Hi Dr Kruse,

I would like to do testing. Unfortunately, my family doctor(Dr Kevin Worry) has moved far up north. I have been thinking about the Life Extension's testing protocols, but I live in Canada. (I have been meaning to contact them about blood pulling services. I will put it on my list for today's actions.)

I am sure that there is a way through this problem of testing. I want to learn more about testing protocols too. I have gone way past the point of being a passive patient letting the doctor do all the thinking. (Of course, this will make it difficult to find another doctor.) I truly want to understand rather than blindly following directions without understanding - at least partly - why.

If you have any suggestions I would greatly appreciate it.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-09T19:02:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-i-had-success-question-for-experienced-cters.737/post-13030>

Hi Dr Kruse,

I just checked out Life Extension and the only test they can do for Canadians is the omega score because it is a prick test:

[http://www.lef.org/Vitamins-Supplements/ItemLCOMEGA/Omega-Score-Blood-Test.html?source=search&key=omega%20blood%20testing](http://http://www.lef.org/Vitamins-Supplements/ItemLCOMEGA/Omega-Score-Blood-Test.html?source=search&key=omega%20blood%20testing)

All the other tests require I come down to the states and set up a US address for results.

I have looked at the Life Extension list of doctors for Canada:

[http://www.lef.org/Health-Wellness/InnovativeDoctorsInternational/directoryofdoctorsint.html#CANADA](http://http://www.lef.org/Health-Wellness/InnovativeDoctorsInternational/directoryofdoctorsint.html#CANADA)

In general, my experience "interviewing" doctors to find out if our methods will mesh hasn't been a positive experience. Just attempting to find doctors that will accept my SCD/GAPS/modified paleo diet will be a challenge. I wonder how a ketogenic diet combined with cold thermogenesis will go over! I want to work with a doctor that will send me home with my test results so I can learn more about the results in my own time and have intelligent questions for the next consultation.

I really want a doctor for twice a year testing and check-in. I don't go to doctors for little problems. I want a doctor that can see the big picture. I want a doctor that can look at the test results and give me guidance for lifestyle changes. I don't want medication.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T19:55:33+0300

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-10T04:11:02+0300

Holy, it happened again. My temperature went up from 36.6C(97.8F) to 37.0C(98.6F) while I was in the cold tub for 30 minutes at 20C(68F). Wow. When something works it works fast.

### Sherry — 2012-04-12T16:37:18+0300

Lately I'm also experiencing no problem with the temperature for the first 30 minutes. I do seem almost warm and then when it gets to about 30 minutes I start shivering and keep myself submerged by pure willpower alone. ### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-13T16:06:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-i-had-success-question-for-experienced-cters.737/post-14672>

Hi Friends,

I was in my 21C(70F) tub yesterday and my nine year old daughter decided to come in. She is naturally very cold tolerant and spends much of her day outside. She partly got in but raised the tub temperature to 23C(74F) within about five minutes. She got out and had a hot bath. My temperature was 36.9C before and dropped in the tub to 36.5C. Within 15 minutes of leaving the tub my temperature was 35.5C.

I am working of increasing coconut products in my diet. I feel stuffed this week with two meals. I am working on a recipe using coconut cream. I'm thinking of calling it Dr Kruse's Creamy Coconut Candy, since he inspired this experiment. I will post a link next week.

# Thread: T3 and reverse T3?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ineedanLR — 2012-04-09T14:50:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12941>

Hi all,

Years ago (1989), I had an overactive thyroid. My doctor offered 2 treatments. One was to use radioactive iodine to kill of the entire thyroid, making me forever dependent on pills to get my T3. The other was a partial thyroid kill off, using limited amounts of radioactive iodine to 'normalize' the T3 levels by not killing off the entire thyroid.

Naturally, I wanted to selectively kill off the thyroid, so that I wouldn't need pills to have a normal thyroid activity. But, the doctor said this was not really a good solution as I'd have to test for T3 levels constantly and would probably have to take some small amount of synthetic T3 pills anyway.

So, I caved in and allowed the entire thyroid to be killed off, and have taken 175 mcg of synthroid (synthetic T3) ever since 1990.

I understand the basic operation of the thyroid system (T3, TSH (also called T4)). I find it interesting that D. J's leptin reset addresses an abnormal hypothalamus response, because the hypothalamus is part of the thyroid system.

But, **_what is reverse T3???? I read some online reverse T3 websites, but I'm still not getting it.....why is reverse T3 so important_** and why do lab work discussions on this list seem to downplay the importance of T3 levels and accentuate the significance of reverse T3 levels????

TY.

Art

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-09T16:19:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12958>

Hi Art, the hypothalamus is not part of the thyroid system, but they are both part of the endocrine system. Minor point. Normally, the body will convert T4 into the bio active hormone T3. T3 is the one that is used at the cell level. When the body is under stress, physical or mental, some of the T4 is converted to reverseT3 (rT3). To your cells, the rT3 looks like T3, and the rT3 acts like a placeholder taking up a position and that doesn't doesn't allow the real T3 to fit into the slot. So the rT3 is taking up room but not doing any of the work, and it's blocking the real deal from doing the work too.

That's a bit simplified, but I hope it makes sense.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T16:23:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12960>

hi, you have some pretty basic info backwards here

TSH = Thyroid stimulating hormone this is issued by the pituitary in response to signals from the body. TSH causes the thyroid to issue T4 hormone, T4 hormone is further down regulated into T3 (active hormone) or reverse T3 (RT3, inactive, supposedly) hormone.

synthroid is synthetic **T4 the storage hormone

cytomel, cynomel, Liothyronine are all versions of synthetic T3** the active hormone

taking synthroid requires the ability to convert T4 to T3, conversion happens in the liver amongst other places, if you can\`t convert T4 to T3 you will continue to feel hypo on synthetic T4 medication a better choice is natural dessicated thyroid which is a combo of T4, T3, T2 and T1 and mimics the ratios that are naturally by the human body (this is also brand named armour, erfa, naturethyroid amongst others)

when you body wants to slow your metabolism down it will tell the T4 to produce more RT3 instead of T3 thus the greater amount of RT3 you produce the more likely it is you are Leptin Resistant and thyroid resistant

Please read [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/reverse-t3/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/reverse-t3/) to learn more about RT3 and thyroid in general

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T17:17:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12991>

> **LisaAPB;13027 wrote:** So the rT3 is taking up room but not doing any of the work, and it's blocking the real deal from doing the work too.

This is also why a blood test of free T3 could indicate you have plenty of bio-available thyroid hormone, but you could still be hypothyroid at the cellular level.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-09T17:29:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12998>

> **ColleenM;13029 wrote:**
>
>
>
> taking synthroid requires the ability to convert T4 to T3, conversion happens in the liver amongst other places, if you can\`t convert T4 to T3 you will continue to feel hypo on synthetic T4 medication a better choice is natural dessicated thyroid which is a combo of T4, T3, T2 and T1 and mimics the ratios that are naturally by the human body (this is also brand named armour, erfa, naturethyroid amongst others)
>

>
>

> Please read [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/reverse-t3/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/reverse-t3/) to learn more about RT3 and thyroid in general

Thanks to the both of you for the enlightenment.

It seems my reverse T3 is even more significant than the T3 levels, I'll make sure my doctor tests me for it when I need another synthroid test-my T3 was low at last test, and I'm now taking more synthroid, so another test will be needed soon.

I am absolutely LR and I'm sure my liver is clogged beyond being functional....especially since I have no gallbladder and I haven't been taking ox bile (have some on order). I'm of the opinion that I need to begin burning fat and that i desperately need to eliminate all sugar as it is toxic, in any amount. So, liver detox should help me immensely.

I'm doing LR now, and am moving towards Paleo-it's gonna be a bumpy uphill ride for me. But, I have begun the journey.

Again, ty to you both for replying, it helps allot!!!!

Art

# Thread: zero sugar or just low carb?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ineedanLR — 2012-04-09T15:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-12948>

I understand our bodies need some sugar.

But, does the body make enough sugar to meet its needs if we don't consume any sugar at all...consuming only carbs (which the bodies own metabolism converts to sugar internally)?

I'm diabetic and have tracked the diabetic diets official recommendations for years. It hasn't changed. Currently, the official diabetic diet recommends 45 to 60 (net) carbs per meal, 3 meals a day (no skipping meals) and allows an additional 30 carbs per day in snacks-snacks are encouraged to fight hunger).

But, Paleo and Dr. J seem to think 30 to 60 g of carbs per day is ok-a radical shift from the (current) official diabetes diet protocol.

I've been on 60 carbs per day since starting the leptin reset, and I think my body tolerates the lower carb consumption just fine. I think 60 carbs per day might be enough carbs for the human body unless large amounts of physical activity demand more fuel. But, should these carbs come from sugar free foods or is fruit (that contains actual sugar) ok as long as the carbs are limited to 60 g per day?

In other words.....**_if we consume 60 g of carbs per day without consuming any sugar(s), will the body make enough sugar to satisfy its needs?_**

TY.

Art

### Adria — 2012-04-09T16:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-12957>

They'll keep recommending carbs for diabetics and the drug companies will continue to make their profits.

I've been keto diet for 20+yrs, meaning less than 20carbs total daily, no sugars or starches.

### ealachan — 2012-04-09T16:48:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-12972>

> In other words.....if we consume 60 g of carbs per day without consuming any sugar(s), will the body make enough sugar to satisfy its needs?

Part of what the reset is doing is forcing your body to figure out that yes, it can run just fine on lower carbs. Until your body learns how to handle it, it can feel kind of crap-tastic (ie: the dreaded "carb flu")...but once your system catches on, it's great!

### shilohman — 2012-04-09T16:55:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-12974>

We have all been taught that we need carbs. Resist the brain washing.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-09T22:53:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-13131>

> **shilohman;13043 wrote: We have all been taught that we need carbs. Resist the brain washing.**

**

I'm here because I have an open mind. Those who don't will enjoy their pasta and wheat bread because it's easier to remain the same than it is to CHANGE.......

But, don't we need a small amount of carbs because we need some glucose for proper cell function??? I'm not brainwashed now, _I'm trying to understand whether we need a small amount of carbs to make small amounts of blood glucose._**

And, if we need some glucose, can we get all the glucose we need from carbs, or do we need to eat small amounts of sugar (like we get in fruits)? I do understand that the fiber in fruit and the antioxidants are benefits and that it might not be wise to eliminate fruits entirely.

If Dr. J is monitoring, I'd appreciate having his input on this.

Art

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-10T00:59:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-13152>

> **ineedanLR;13200 wrote:
>
> But, don't we need a small amount of carbs because we need some glucose for proper cell function??? I'm not brainwashed now, _I'm trying to understand whether we need a small amount of carbs to make small amounts of blood glucose._**

Short answer, no. If your liver is functioning properly, it can make all the glucose you need through Gluconeogenesis.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-10T02:52:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-13192>

The liver makes the needed carbohydrates from proteins, powered by fats for the conversion. The liver, however, needs to be in shape for this, and if it is constantly repairing itself from onslaughts of fructose, alcohol, or acetaminophen, it's abilities are hindered.

Your understanding is a product of mis-information. You do not need ANY sugar. Your gut uses some of the carbohydrates from vegetables to support the good bacteria and supress the bad, so some veggie carbs are good for you. Other carbs (fruit, sugar, starchy vegetables) are not needed at all. Depending on your level of health, you may be able to tolerate them fine. The low carbs approach is for T2D.

There is a ton of info on here about Diabetes, I think you can search the blog posts for a bunch of reading on it. Also, Jack has been able to rid people of the diabetes with his protocol.

TY Caveman. My formal diabetes education is almost 20 years old. I haven't gotten a formal refresher....so I'm bound to be a little 'off' with regard to the latest diabetes diet and treatment(s).

However, last week I paid for a session with a dietitian associated with the diabetes clinic at the local hospital. She is also a certified diabetes educator. But, the only diabetes diet she knew about was the standard diet, the one I learned about 20 years ago when I was first diagnosed with diabetes.

She know nothing of Paleo, Dr J and his work, leaky gut, Leptin/Leptin Reset. I didn't even menton the gaps diet protocol to her (out of fear of overload). She also was skeptical when I tried to explain the importance of minimizing our intake of foods known to cause inflamatory and autoimmune responses.

I am a techie (electrical engineer, retired), so I understand science and the roll it plays in our lives.

My guess is that this forum will explode with new members when the news of Dr. J's work becomes more mainstream...and, as other professional medical type people come on board. I hope the management is already working on expansion plans.

GL to all.

Art

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-10T16:50:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-13331>
