APow,

is it evidence enough for you when I tell you my own mom (60 yo) got healed a few years ago from severe asthma (with medication) - only with CT (and accidentially!)?

NO other treatments, or changes in nutrition or lifestyle whatsoever. She lived in a moldy house by then and still lives in the same moldy house. Asthma is gone, totally gone. She is real healthy and full of energy. We new nothing about CT but mom just started to go swim in the lake by the house every morning year around (Finland) cause she thought it felt so refreshing. Soon thereafter the asthma disappeared. This is evidence enough for me. I know CT works. My mom now know someone here keeping his arthritis in shape with CT, so that he do not need any medication. There are many.

Have you not read Kruses blog? He tells about his results and experimentations all the time! First hand. Is it not evidence?

You should travel to Scandinavia. Here no one would ever doubt, it has real benefits. It lies so deep in our culture. 2% of the population does CT. How cool is that.

### Glamazon — 2012-04-12T20:19:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14316>

> **APow;14364 wrote:** Interesting points, Cron23. I've acknowledged before that CT may do no harm (unless you're doing an extreme version of it). But does it do any good (especially in the face of all the toxins you just listed)? Is it something a doctor should recommend as a treatment for a serious illness if it does absolutely no good, and works less well than "conventional" treatments?
>
>
>
> I'm not saying Dr. Kruse should sit quietly. He's free to say whatever he wants within the ethical limits of his profession. But should he be promoting his theories as if they are unassailable facts. No.

This is interesting, APow. Maybe it is just a matter of POV.

Since I have been reading Dr. K I don't get the impression that he is an absolutist or promoting his theories as unassailable facts. They are theories not facts. I think he has before even invited people to prove him wrong in the interest of good science. When we start talking about "shoulds" they can start becoming hobbles to positive action. I get you are being conservative and an advocate because of making sure people get good care and no one is making people do Dr. K's protocols. They have a personal choice. People are ultimately responsible for their own health care choices and so their results. There is a saying that was popular in the 70s, "we judge by results, often harsh, always fair." If people don't do due diligence that is on their shoulders. We can't be their nannies.

We can't make decisions for everyone on what they should do for health care. Personally, I don't think he is being irresponsible or premature or being reckless. He definately exhibits a surgeon mentality...take charge, full speed ahead, get it done now, results oriented but he lacks the old school close mindedness of many of his peers. He does take risks but those are calculated as far as I can tell based on a high degree of confidence in his research.It's refreshing to see a MD march to a different drummer and still be uncompromising in his service to his patients.

I think part of a doctor's job is to give his patients hope. So much that passes for hope generating in medicine is warmed over platitudes and maybes. Dr. K's theories give me personal hope. I am personally invested in hoping they work and are proveable over and over again for most people. I wouldn't be on this forum or invested in being a member if I didn't have some serious confidence in the over all project.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-12T23:38:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14408>

> **Cavemam;13468 wrote:** hehe, our community just started doing permits for Backyard Chickens - but HOA trumps that. I should make an effort to remove the "no livestock" addendum. Perhaps after I get all "who gives a shit what they think" after CTing for a few years like Jack.

Well, move out of a subdivision run by little nazis --- but if you can't, you might start a ballot initiative making it illegal to forbid growing food or keeping chickens, by any HOA. I believe that some places have put in local ordinances saying that HOA's cannot forbid clotheslines, for instance.

When I got my place in Oregon, I was very careful to investigate the covenant situation before closing. There's only one, and it's from the 19th century. I may not raise mink. It seems that across the main road not far away someone in the 19th century had a mink farm, with all the noise, smell, etc. that entails. So, I shall not raise mink.

There is a town ordinance -- people may keep hens but only if they get permission from all their immediate neighbors. But no roosters. My neighbors have a few hens and anxiously asked if I minded, when I moved in. They would have been okay even if I had, grandfathered in. I said I loved the sound of them, and I don't have a dog to harass them. I'm wondering what the town would think if I started keeping ducks or guinea fowl, both sexes. I assume I could. Some troubles protecting them from predators like coyotes, no doubt.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-13T00:42:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14448>

> **Birdy;14505 wrote:** no mink!!!!!! Gosh, that was my next factor X guess. The trouble with this plan is, entire towns and counties have set up these ordinances against basic living. It can't be unwound by a simple change, at least around here.

Change is on the way, thanks to Peak Oil and climate change and the depletion of the Ogalalla Aquifer. The natural response is to buy local and to grow one's own food as much as possible. Some places (like my state, thank heavens) allow ballot initiatives if enough signatures can be gathered. Regaining some long-lost sensible basic rights, like growing food gardens, or cold bathing in one's own back yard and using the "gray water" from it on the garden, may get a lot of strength if conventional food becomes any more denatured, any scarcer, and any more expensive. I don't see how matters can continue down the present agribusiness path much longer. Almost everything in the supermarket requires an incredible amount of diesel fuel to be grown, processed, shipped, and sold. We're eating oil. No wonder it makes us sick.

Just had a perverse little notion: call Paleo-Hacks "The Mink Farm".

### Cron23 — 2012-04-13T02:40:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14500>

Someone said I shouldn't have called APow a Troll... I want to clarify that trolling is a term used to describe people on the internet who frequently cause discourse. This is quite common in scientific sites. You can often tell the same person will come back under different names and try to create another persona. APow was a blatant troll. That is that! Wanted to make it clear I wasn't name calling

Here is the wiki definition:

In Internet slang, a troll is someone who posts inflammatory,\[2\] extraneous, or off-topic messages in an online community, such as an online discussion forum, chat room, or blog, with the primary intent of provoking readers into an emotional response\[3\] or of otherwise disrupting normal on-topic discussion.\[4\] The noun troll may refer to the provocative message itself, as in: "That was an excellent troll you posted".

While the word troll and its associated verb trolling are associated with Internet discourse, media attention in recent years has made such labels subjective, with trolling describing intentionally provocative actions and harassment outside of an online context. For example, mass media has used troll to describe "a person who defaces Internet tribute sites with the aim of causing grief to families."\[5\]\[6\]

# Thread: Hypothalamic Dysfunction - temperature sensitivity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothalamic-dysfunction-temperature-sensitivity.779/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-10T20:31:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothalamic-dysfunction-temperature-sensitivity.779/post-13418>

I'm finding I have a major sensitivity to both hot and cold environments. It's definitely worse in what I consider to be hot environments, which seems to be anything above 70 degrees these days! I get nauseous, extremely fatigued, my cheeks flush and I look like Santa Claus!

The only real problem I have in cold environments (other than the obvious) is that my feet freeze and you can literally feel the cold coming off of my skin if you hold your hand an inch away from them.

I've been doing some research on this and see it can be related to Hashi's and Hypothalamic dysfunction. I do have hypothyroidism, but antibody tests have always been negative. I'm going to have a repeat thyroid panel run next week and am anxious to see what that shows. Plus, most of what I'm reading seems to say hypo causes cold intolerance whereas hyper causes heat intolerance. I've got both, so I don't know that Hashi's would explain it anyway.

I found a few references to hypothalamic disorders (tumors, etc) causing both hot/cold sensitivities. I still have this lingering brain fog that surfaced about 2 years ago, so I can't always make sense of all the scientific jargon I'm reading. Just wondering if anyone else has come across something like this.

I've been doing CT for 6 weeks now and I've been on the leptin Rx for 10 weeks . . . and I feel like this is getting worse, which makes me very nervous with summer on its way. It was 66 degrees here yesterday and I truly felt like I was going to vomit in the car. I was roasting even with the a/c on full blast! My limited understanding of the leptin rx is that it should pretty much re-start the hypothalamus, correct? Is it possible this won't work in some individuals?

I know my hormones are all low. I just started supplementing with DHEA and pregnenolone. I also take NT for the hypo.

Is there anything anyone else would suggest I consider doing? I've had most of the tests Dr. K suggests done (hsCRP was good, vit D good, thyroid definitely off - still working on that, hormones low as I said, insulin good, cholesterol good).

### tweety — 2012-04-10T23:05:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothalamic-dysfunction-temperature-sensitivity.779/post-13472>

Maybe the CT is resetting you after all. If you are feeling warmer in 66 degrees, maybe your body is still in a state of upheavel? Feeling warmer is one of the signs of Leptin sensitivity. Hormones and deficiencies can make your journey bumpy (lots of forward progress followed by interruptions and setbacks), and it can make it take longer too.

I have been doing CT as long as you and feel like it is helping, but I have no hard evidence that I can pinpoint. I just "feel" better, like its working. I say stay the course and your body will work it out. Give it time, it may have alot to overcome.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-11T03:08:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothalamic-dysfunction-temperature-sensitivity.779/post-13541>

Maybe it's part of the reset. I guess I just assumed there would be more positive reactions to it. The heat intolerance is extremely uncomfortable and I nearly pass out in my office where the temps are 72-74 degrees . . . I'm really struggling to get through the work day.

### agatha — 2012-04-11T14:47:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothalamic-dysfunction-temperature-sensitivity.779/post-13697>

I don't have answers about the underlying issues but could you wear a cooling vest at work - or would that make you look too mad?

### BJK77 — 2012-04-11T16:23:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothalamic-dysfunction-temperature-sensitivity.779/post-13719>

Well, I'm sitting on a "cooling pad" right now with a gel pack of ice under each cheek and between my legs! Thankfully no one can see it as I'm behind a desk . . . the cooling vest probably wouldn't work since I'm in an office environment and need to look a bit dressier. Plus, it probably would make me look quite mad. They already think I'm a total nutcase for avoiding the candy dish and all the various treats that are brought in every day. I mean, really, who can truly enjoy life without a browning/cake/donut, etc every day?!?!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-12T02:00:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glossary-of-terms.787/post-13990>

"

> **Dan in Utah;14051 wrote:** Coconut oil is composed of approximately 66% medium-chain triglycerides aka MCT.

"

Thank you - I never could understand why coconut oil was abbreviated as MCT. Now it makes better sense. (This is from someone who took two weeks to figure out the SW, CW and GW.

By the way, my SW (last May) was 245-250, my CW is 175 and my GW is 150. I used hCG for 3 rounds and have stabilized using the LR. I have begun the CT a couple of weeks ago. 5 years ago, I had 66% of my pancreas removed (along with my spleen) because of a very fast growing tumor. It tripled in size in a month. It was still completely encased, but was within 4 mm of reaching the distal wall of the pancreas. The head of the pancreas was saved. Then, last Nov. I had to have half my thyroid removed because of a large calcified, hot nodule. The surgeon went back in to remove the rest of the thyroid 2 weeks later because the nodule had 2 different spots of carcinoma. Both very small, papillary with variants of fibulary. So, I am needing the ct for both diabetes and hypothyroidism, as well as those last 25 pounds.

### Martin — 2012-04-21T15:30:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glossary-of-terms.787/post-17356>

Here's a list I've been compiling for a while.

AI auto-immune

ACTH Adrenocorticotropic hormone

VEGF Vascular endothelial growth factor

TNF-alpha tumor necrosis factor

MMP-9 Matrix metallopeptidase 9

TGF-b1 transforming growth factor beta-1

VIP Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide

VGEF Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor

alpha MSH Melanocyte-stimulating hormone

ADH anti-diuretic hormone

PCOS Polycystic ovary syndrome

BCP Bombyx acid cysteine protease

CRH Corticotropin-releasing hormone

C.R.O.N. Caloric restriction with optimal nutrition

AA arachadonic acid

ALS Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis aka - Lou Gehrig's

ME myalgic encephalomyelitis

ECCG green tea antioxidant

MDA malondialdehyde

PPAR peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor

ALE advanced lipoxidation end-products

HRV Heart Rate Variability Test

IL-6 Interleukin-6

ROS Reactive Oxygen Species generation

IF intermittent fasting

CW conventional wisdom

CLA Conjugated Linoleic Acid

HIIT high intensity interval training

HCG, or human chorionic gonadotropin is the hormone that Simeons discovered in pregnant women.

VAP vertical auto profile (lipid profile test)

SAD=Standard American Diet

 RPD = Raw Paleolithic Diet

 RVAF diet= Raw Animal and Vegetable Diet.

 HC= High carb, also hydrocortisone

 AGEs= Advanced Glycation End-Products.

 ALEs= Advanced Lipoxidation End-Products.

 PAHs= Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons.

 NSAs= Nitrosamines. HCAs= Heterocyclic Amines.

 AV= Aajonus Vonderplanitz.

 PD= Primal Diet. 

GCB= Guy-Claude Burger. 

WAPF= Weston.A Price Foundation.

 SF= Sally Fallon.

 LC=Loren Cordain 

WP=Weston-Price.

NPY Neuropeptide Y

MCT Medium-chain triglycerides

VDR= Vitamin D Receptor

BCAA branched chain amino acid

ROS Reactive oxygen species

### Optimalbound — 2012-07-27T20:06:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glossary-of-terms.787/post-41548>

BG-6 and my recent educational email consult made me look up these:

SCFA = [short chain fatty acids](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scfa)

SHBG = [sex hormone-binding globulin](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sex_hormone-binding_globulin)

DDD = [degenerative disc disease](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Degenerative_disc_disease)

DJD = [degenerative joint disease](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Degenerative_Joint_Disease), aka. Osteoarthritis

OSA = [obstructive sleep apnea](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Obstructive_sleep_apnea)

ASI = [adrenal stress index](http://http://www.acupuncturehealth.net/the-adrenal-stress-index/)

PRL = [prolactin](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prolactin)

DPPIV = [Dipeptidyl peptidase-4](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dipeptidyl_peptidase-4)

BCM-7 = casein beta-casomophin-7, aka. "[the milk devil](http://http://www.faim.org/bookreviews/devilinmilkupdatefeb2010.html)", a peptide and an opioid that is released when digesting A1 dairy

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-28T23:14:57+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-13T23:06:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glossary-of-terms.787/post-58001>

> **Amber Tyrrell;59325 wrote:** What is DHA and LA?

DHA is docosahexaenoic acid, the omega 3 fatty acid that fish/seafood is loaded with. LA is (I think) linolenic acid, an Omega 6 fatty acid.

Most people have an altered ratio of O3/O6 and are too heavy on the O6's... Dr K has pointed out a lot that we need O6's in our diets, but for proper signalling and reduced inflammation we need that ratio closer to 1:1. So if you think your ratio is off (most of us are dealing with this) you need to seriously bump up the consumption of fish & seafood (with raw giving you the most DHA). Does that make sense?

I'm sure someone will correct me if I've got it wrong.

### Shellie — 2014-06-30T13:41:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glossary-of-terms.787/post-132722>

After reading all the acronyms, I put them all together in a new alphabetical list. Hope this helps others as much as it will help me. I will add to it as I continue reading.

AA - arachadonic acid

AI - auto-immune

ACTH - Adrenocorticotropic hormone

ADH - anti-diuretic hormone

AGEs - Advanced Glycation End-Products.

AI – Artificial Intelligence

AI – Auto-immune

ALEs - Advanced Lipoxidation End-Products.

alpha MSH - Melanocyte-stimulating hormone

ALE - advanced lipoxidation end-products

ALS - Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis - Lou
Gehrig's

AV - Aajonus Vonderplanitz.

BAB – Big Ass Breakfast

BCAA - branched chain amino acid

BCP - Bombyx acid cysteine protease

BHRT – Bio-identical Hormone Replacement

CLA - Conjugated Linoleic Acid

CO – Coconut Oil

CRH - Corticotropin-releasing hormone

C.R.O.N. - Caloric restriction with optimal nutrition

CT – Cold Thermogenesis

CW - conventional wisdom

DH – Darling Husband

ECCG - green tea antioxidant

eNOS - endothelial Nitric Oxide Synthetase

ED - Estrogen Dominance

ED - Erectile Dysfunction

ED – Eating Disorder

GCB - Guy-Claude Burger.

HAB – Half-Assed Breakfast

HC - High carb

HC - hydrocortisone

HCAs - Heterocyclic Amines.

HCG - human chorionic gonadotropin

HIIT - high intensity interval training

IF- intermittent fasting

IL-6 - Interleukin-6

LC - Loren Cordain

LR – Leptin Reset

LS – Leptin Sensitivity

MCT - Medium-chain triglycerides

MDA- malondialdehyde

MDA – Mark’s Daily Apple

ME - myalgic encephalomyelitis

MMP - 9 Matrix metallopeptidase 9

alpha MSH - Melanocyte-stimulating hormone

NAD - Neolithic associated diseases

NPY - Neuropeptide Y

NSAs - Nitrosamines

PAHs- Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons.

PCOS - Polycystic ovary syndrome

PD - Primal Diet.

PPAR - peroxisome proliferator-activated
receptor

ROS - Reactive oxygen species

RPD - Raw Paleolithic Diet

RVAF diet - Raw Animal and Vegetable Diet.

SAD - Standard American Diet

SCN - Suprachiasmatic Nucleus

SF - Sally Fallon.

TGF - b1 transforming growth factor beta-1

TNF - tumor necrosis factor

VDR - Vitamin D Receptor

VIP - Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide

HRV - Heart Rate Variability Test

ROS - Reactive Oxygen Species generation

TNF - alpha tumor necrosis factor

VAP - vertical auto profile (lipid profile test)

VEGF – Vascular endothelial growth factor

VIP - Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide

WAPF - Weston.A Price Foundation.

WP - Weston-Price

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-05-14T04:32:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glossary-of-terms.787/post-164187>

This may sound dumb but I really don't know the real meaning of "n=1".
I know it has to do with your own personal "thunderstorm" but that's about it

### Peertje80 — 2015-05-14T10:13:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glossary-of-terms.787/post-164212>

> [Cpt.Tired said:](/goto/post?id=164187)
>
> This may sound dumb but I really don't know the real meaning of "n=1".
> I know it has to do with your own personal "thunderstorm" but that's about it

N stands for the number of participants in a clinical trail. so N=50, there were 50 subjects. N=1 is a trail of 1 person. Usually referring to his/hers own little private 'research' experiment.

# Thread: Trying ask the right questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-ask-the-right-questions.788/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-10T23:28:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-ask-the-right-questions.788/post-13481>

I started this thread over on Robb W's forum to help unravel the puzzle of my son.

[http://robbwolf.com/forum/viewtopic.php?f=39&t=4929](http://http://robbwolf.com/forum/viewtopic.php?f=39&t=4929)

Mostly what I need is questions. When you have a special needs kid, lots of practitioners roll in with their patented solution without really evaluating the patient- sort of a one size fits all approach or worse yet cart before the horse. Rather what I need is

-   the questions to ask,
-   the suggested tests to help answer the question
    and THEN the the nutritional/lifestyle interventions.

I have received some help on that forum and from a few PM's here that will summarize in another post below.

So if you have some time to read that thread and some insight, please share. If you have a child that may have similar issues, feel free to share how the practitioner evaluated the symptom, any tests done, and then what were the considered treatments.

I have appts scheduled for this child in the next month, so I will hopefully see where we can get within traditional medicine - for their benefit and this little guy's too.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-10T23:43:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-ask-the-right-questions.788/post-13483>

Vitamin/Mineral Levels

​

B's, D, E, K, Mg Selenium, Iron, Calcium

Leaky gut-

-   Gut permability tests/GI Effects Metametrix test
-   Progesterone
-   Pregnelone
    BDNF
    NGF

Sleep study

DHEA level

Salivary Cortisol

HS CRP

### chocolate — 2012-04-11T02:29:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-ask-the-right-questions.788/post-13534>

I was just reading about a compounding pharmacy that does oxytocin compounding and it is going very well for lots of kids. It helps with sleep and attentiveness. I was tripping over it looking for the cold water/oxytocin/neurogeneration. That's what happens. I dismissed it for our family's needs, because I think the cold water is gonna get us there. That explains the marvelous sleep, I think. Then the zonulin that the doctor was mentioning, one of the simple links showed that the basic probiotic for home fermenting makes the zonulin do its job.

[http://www.belmarpharmacy.com/oxytocin.htm](http://http://www.belmarpharmacy.com/oxytocin.htm)

[http://www.npr.org/2011/01/03/13247391Tha's3/scientists-test-trust-hormone-for-autism-fight](http://http://www.npr.org/2011/01/03/13247391Tha's3/scientists-test-trust-hormone-for-autism-fight)

[http://www.raysahelian.com/oxytocin.html](http://http://www.raysahelian.com/oxytocin.html)

Its probably all old news to you, but I thought that it was interesting that the kids are not making enough oxytocin, so supplementing could help. Also, when I was looking on the metal detox sites, a lot of folks give their kids the lithium orotate supplements. They are very mild, and lithium orotate is in Centrum. It works very quickly, 30 minutes. You would only give him a little tiny bit. It calms you right the heck down. My daughter is on Lithium and she took the orotate when she was off it and loved it as a quick quieter. Its non-prescription, mail order. It is an extract from a vegetable. You could try it yourself and see, its very helpful. It doesn't get you to the questions to ask the doctor. That's my two cents. I know in our house the rule is one change at a time, no more than one in a week. I think its good because it gets us through the entire weekend and work week to find rough spots.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-29T03:50:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-ask-the-right-questions.788/post-19714>

Just a quick update for anyone who is curious how this has gone down. I drafted a lengthy email to the pediatrician, developmental pediatrician, GI doc and the nutritionist on 4/17. The pediatrician deferred to the specialists. The dev. pediatrician suggested a new adhd medicine that might not mess with the weight problem. The nutritionist asked me to fill out yet another food log and asked me to cough up references for all the testing I was suggestion. No response from the GI doc.

I wanted to give you an update since it has been about 90 days since I was in to see \[nutritionist\]with \[son\].

When I met with \[nutritionist\]in January, while nothing was an emergency, it seemed everything was going in the wrong direction. Poor sleep, constipation, meltdowns at the end of the day, suppressed appetite, and what appeared to be a slight weight loss. \[nutritionist\] confirmed this as well as what appears to be the expected slow down in height that goes with stimulant medication. We decided at that time to stop meds and focus on restoring appetite and growth. To that end, no real change to the dietary plan - great food -( lots of meat and fish - going grass fed and wild caught where $$ allows), fat as much as we can - nuts and even shots of olive oil, + the Pediasure Peptide. Vegietable intake relative to fruit could be better, but he is willingly eating whatever is put in front of him.

On the positive side, appetite is back, even to the point of \[son\] verbalizing he is hungry and asking for food. He is getting 3 squares + a snack and 8 oz of the PP daily. We jumped in with 1.5 mg melatonin to ensure sleep which seems to be doing well. I have added coconut oil to his diet - usually by making a homemade chocolate bar with dark chocolate, coconut oil and some almonds. When I can get it to him, it seems to help not only get the fats in but also aid the constipation. He seems to go more often without getting too loose. For the most part, he is happy and even enjoying a cooking class after school. He is enjoying being outdoors and trying harder to play with kids in the neighborhood.

On the negative side, there has been no change in weight, if not a bit more loss. And to that end, I think we really need to investigate some more. He is about 52 lbs and that is down from a year ago. So while a year ago, \[pediatrician\]and \[nutritionist\]were thinking that he may just "be thin" and will continue to "just be him", I don't think we can make that assumption when we are dealing with a negative trend in weight.

While it is not documented science, you know I heavily rely on gut instinct and use that to steer me to research more and try to ask the right questions. While the majority of people who have interest in their work have obesity issues, I have continued to do lots of reading of the biochemistry of Matt LaLonde, Stephan Guynet and Robb Wolf. Through them and networking with other moms who have kids with failure to thrive issues, I have some more suggested areas that might be the source of \[son's\]issue.

Since I do not have records of all his labs, I don't know if we have current readings on these, and in some cases I don't know if we have ever tested them

B-6. B-12, Folate (He does take a children's multi vitamin\_

Vit E and Vit K (he does take 200 IU of Vit E daily)

Vit A, Vit D

Magnesium, Potassium, Calcium

Selenium

Iron

While I know also that his Flex Sig and EGD's have shown good results, and that he passes on the fecal fat test, I am still terribly puzzled that the best weight gain he had was when he was taking the MT-4 lipase. Is it possible that there is something else happening at the intestinal brush border that we haven't investigated? I know there is a term called "leaky gut" that has variable acceptance in the medical community, but would a gut permability test, such as a GI Effects Metametrix test be warranted?

Other items which have been suggested to me as possible indicators of problems - which I have no clue if we ever investigated are

DHEA level -

Salivary Cortisol

reverse T3

HS CRP a tougher standard to measure inflammation

Is there something on the adrenal level that is driving an energy disconnect down the line at the thyroid?

Or is there something going on at the liver that we've missed

Pregnelone - tested using a VAP lipids test - if this is low and it is possible that so is

Progesterone - which from what I read is also crucial to neuro functioning in kids with ADHD and why some see imnprovment at puberty

Also, I don't know that we have ever tested BDNF and NGF, which while not necessarily related to growth/weight, may well have an overall effect on how well his brain is wiring for the things it needs to do as it grows. IMHO, excepting his genetic issues that may be compounding, I would like to think better fed cells (through improved digestion ) would increase the cellular ability to generate these which would in turn create better signaling for the liver, etc to do its job.

BDNF

NGF

Lastly, I know that good sleep is crucial to both good growth and good neuro function. \[Son\] still trends toward sleep difficulties at the extremes of the solstices. I don't know yet if a sleep study is warranted, perhaps this summer if we don't find and improve precursors to good sleep through the tests above.

while I would like to consider another stimulant medication for \[son's\] attention, I don't know that we can until we come back amidships on the growth issues.

Please let me know what you each think on this, and if there is time in your schedule to bring \[son\] in for another visit. I had his annual IEP last month and will bring you the goals for next year.

Regards,

### chocolate — 2012-04-29T04:25:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-ask-the-right-questions.788/post-19720>

I was messing up with the sulfur. The fruit needs the acetylaldehyde dehydrogenase and sulfur slows it down. The sulfur is an essential and the fructose isn't. I think fructose malabsorption is the root of our family's evils. It causes the intestines to signal for zinc not to be absorbed. And you can't sneak it in the body anyway, it will just dump it out the urine.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-05-12T15:13:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-ask-the-right-questions.788/post-23366>

Just a quick update.

I was able to meet with the GI doctor yesterday and obtain a consult to endocrinology and allergy. Lab work that is more related to GI than endo was also done. HS CRP, Vit D, CMP, Mg, Sel, most of the vitamins. Also doing a fasting lipids and serum cortisol. Fecal fat test will be repeated too.

So now my question... where do I find optimal ranges for children?

### janagram — 2012-04-11T02:56:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omega-3-and-barry-sears.792/post-13539>

Barry Sears' Zone Diet has morphed slightly over the years to the point where he now seems to think soy is a great source of protein and has a great omega3/6 ration. In fact his new products have grain and whey and he claims you can just forget the meat if you want to because of the great omega ratio this gives.

Anyone still with me?

So how could he say that, biochemist that he is? It flies in the face of all Paleo teachings, yet he is a scientist. (I was once a huge fan.....)

what I'm asking is ...Can a great omega 3/6 ratio make up for eating grain/no meat?

Barry Sears is selling supplements. His motive is the dollar, not helping people. He is probably paid by the soy industry just like Dr. Dean Ornish. The Chinese and Japanese eat soy, but they only eat fermented soy like mismo. Go to this link [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/10-things-to-know-about-tofu/](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/10-things-to-know-about-tofu/) on MDA. Mark is too kind to soy, so let me tell you about my relatives who are vegetarian and eat a lot of soy. They all have osteoporosis, I am talking about people 30 yrs old and older. Male/female, they all have osteoporosis. There is research that shows that soy in women inhibits the absorption of calcium by interfering with estrogen, not sure how it does its dirty deed in men, but trust me it does the same in men. Whether you are male or female, eating soy interferes with testosterone in men and estrogen in women. I use to eat soy and my testosterone level was hammered into the ground. Only after stopping the soy consumption did my body recover over many months. Barry Sears is doesn't give a squat about your health he is only interested in getting into your wallet.

Do some searches on the internet for soy and estrogen absorption and see what you come up with and convince yourself that what I am saying is true.

So, when the monkeys have nothing else to do, have them go at it!

### Marie — 2012-04-12T05:51:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-marketing-monkeys.793/post-14087>

> **Jack Kruse;14070 wrote:** i hate pictures and I dont like graphs.....
>
> I like making my head hurt.

I'm not talking graphs. And this isn't the first time you've heard this from me.

How about pictures of molecules? Enzymatic pathways? That's what many of us need to anchor the entire page of information. It would also break up the pages, providing visual anchors to go back and re-read.

You forget that as an INTJ you are only ~2% of the population. Your communication style works great for you because your greatest "natural talent" is linking "things" that appear to have no connection. Being lost for a while is part of the natural process, as is spending +20 years gathering random facts and experiences. INTJs also have one the highest tolerance for ambiguity AND conflict, because both of these situations force more information to be put on the table to produce a better solution. (Sound familiar?) This is WHAT INTJs do, and they do it best in their 40s...But ignoring different learning styles means you'd leave 98% of the population in shades of darkness. Now we know you like the dark, LOL, but that doesn't help in teaching.

Besides, this is a job for the Marketing Monkey editors, not you.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13557>

Since using an iPad, computer, nook, kindle or watching TV after dark is a mismatch...what are y'all doing between sundown and sleep?

I'm using an iPad at 8:30 EST to post this...obviously a mismatch.

### shilohman — 2012-04-11T03:42:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13569>

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-11T03:47:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13573>

I wear glasses from lowbluelights.com. I have baseball season tickets and I WILL be going to the games but wearing these within an hour of sundown. Don't care if I look weird! I am trying to get dark after sundown but I have a job and support hubby's business when I get home (second job more or less!!). Within a year I hope to quit the day job and do my business stuff in daylight so I can get more optimal!

I have to say, the glasses make me feel sleepy within an hour of wearing them, all I wanna do is crawl into bed. And I'm a person who can't usually fall asleep easily. I do notice a difference if I don't wear them. Also have yellow nitelites and plan to get more yellow bulbs for after dark.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-11T03:52:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13579>

We have instituted a 'natural' darkness protocol at our house. We just never turn on any lights. So as the house gets darker, we light a few candles (no fire place) in whatever room we are in. Once it's dark outside (currently by 6:45pm as we are in autumn), no more electronic devices. We do whatever we normally do, minus TVs, computers and mobile phones. Our family of four has learned that you can play cards and charades by candle light (not to mention the obvious like talk, listen to music, read a book, etc)! And last night we were putting the final touches on our fish-head broth by candlelight, too. Kids are naturally going to bed earlier as they wind down with decreasing light, and we are too. It's really been a pretty easy transition.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-11T04:20:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13590>

cold dark and sleepy......and sexy time.

### Glamorama — 2012-04-11T04:26:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13592>

> **shilohman;13638 wrote:** I wear amber color glasses (they block blue light) and avoid the computer as much as possible. Oops, but not right now

I do this too. I normally do CT, watch a bit of TV, and go to bed at 8.30 pm or 9 pm... then fall asleep by 10 the latest. We don't use lights after dark, only one small lamp in our lounge-room. We haven't used a heater yet either, ven though its been very cold this week. Very little sexy time, the husband is just never up for it, and the last 2 weeks I haven't really either. Too tired once it gets dark! We are morning-people.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-11T04:48:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13605>

I don't get off work until 11:00 and blue blockers are not really an option at work. In addition to working full-time, I attend graduate school, and home-school my children. I ordered some blue blocking glasses last night and plan to wear them after work. Until I graduate and land a teaching job, things will be challenging in this area.

Flux is on my computer so that helps a little. We plan more fire place use and changing bedrooms to reduce ambient noise and temperature.

Not optimal yet but taking the high road . . . .

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-11T15:59:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13716>

I've been wearing the orange glasses w/blue light blockers. If I'm up reading - I try to read on my kindle touch instead of my iphone or ipad, using minimal light source. I've installed flux on both my mac and my pc. Didn't put it on the iphone or ipad b/c I didnt' want to jailbreak it.

I've been turning the lights off at night, keeping the light as minimal as possible. Also trying to eat earlier at dinner time - but work sometimes makes that a challenge...

its funny b/c the kids will bring me my orange glasses at sundown... I'm going to see if I can find them orange glasses in their size for them b/c they are always trying to wear mine!

working toward being better. trying to get things done during the day that involve the computer. ordering some glasses today because the dh and I do like to watch movies... Dinner is always targeted for 6 or as soon as the dh walks through the door. Wee ones go to bed by 8:30 (they start dinner with a rather large snack around 5 that blends into dinner while I am cooking). Bed is usually around 10 and dh and I usually work a crossword puzzle first.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-11T16:52:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13726>

I'd love some tips on candle use - where you've found either wide, stable ones, or good stable candleholders, and/or what kind you've found. I want to buy exclusively beeswax or tallow - no soy or petro stuff.

I've been looking around when we visit pioneer homes, and noticing the candleholders they used for carrying them around (always with a drip protector stable base) & mounting on the walls. No sense re-inventing the wheel!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-11T16:53:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13727>

When we went to Puerto Rico, ,we stayed out till sunset every day and then ate dinner outdoors at restaurants. We found that by the time we went to the room, we were ready for bed by 9. Every time. It was amazing.

I want to try to re-create that somewhat at home, and think we'll start sitting on the deck while I nurse DD to sleep instead of the couch watching TV.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-12T01:04:45+0300

We also use tea lights within glass holders (meant for them). The ones mounted on walls sound good, too.

### shilohman — 2012-04-12T01:32:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-13978>

> **ColdBren;14036 wrote:** We we have the blue blocking glasses....is there any harm in using computers, phones etc?

On nights when I don't use my glasses I notice I have a harder time going to sleep and my sleep isn't as sound. When I do use my glasses it doesn't seem to matter if I use the computer or phone, my sleep is rock solid.

### Birdy — 2012-04-12T03:32:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-you-doing-once-the-sun-goes-down.794/post-14042>

I have f.lux, but am usually off the computer by 8. I knit, spin, play and listen to music, with a small (15 watt) incandescent lamp. Sometimes I watch TV or go in rooms with fluorescent light with sunglasses. I live like I am camping. I get ready for bed, have the dishes done and anything needing lots of light I do in the daytime. The big adjustment is in knitting. I have several projects, and so pick an easy one for evening, and I never EVER choose colors at night. Spinning is easier, since it requires no power, and really not much eyesight. They always say learn to knit without seeing, because you'll need it when you are old. So, that is what I do now, hard stuff in the day, easy knitting at night, or ball-winding. It is easy to do a drum circle or most weaving in pitch darkness. Trouble is, I haven't convinced anyone else to do it with me.

BCAAs have been implicated in insulin resistance. All the talk now is about how serum levels of BCAAs is related to insulin secretion. Has there ever been studies or observatinos that BCAA supplementation (whey protein, etc.) resulted in diabetes? Because that's what these studies seem to suggest. However, it could be that the elevated BCAAs could be the result of something else, not caused by IR. Anyway, those who underwent gastric bypass surgery and reversed their diabetes had drastically lower BCAA levels. One wonders whether this is due to perhaps protein malabsoprtion.

There are probably confounding factors. My point is that the Duke study seems to implicate high fat and protein diets in obesity and insulin resistance. And then we see diabetics become non-diabetics when given bypass surgery, as their BCAAs become markedly lower. Only those with the surgery show low BCAAs, not those who lost weight via diet or exercise. What's the obvious conclusion: BCAAs are somehow involved in insulin resistance. However, there could be a confunding factor, just as a high fat diet will not make you fat but thin.

### mambo — 2012-04-17T07:47:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/methionine-deficient-diet-increases-lifespan-leucine-deprivation-increases-thermog.796/post-15827>

That's what I mean by confounding. It's like, if you wanna be thin, eat fat. If you wanna be fat, eat carbs. This is why the whole thing needs to be looked at from another angle: Why do people who're cured of their diabetes all have drastically low BCAA levels, including Leucine? Is it possible that diabetes is really caused by high acidity which impairs your pancreatic beta cells' ability to secrete insulin? You can attain high acidity by eating lots of processed carbs, grains and heavy meats; alkalinity is associated with vegetables and fruits. If so, it's easy to imagine that you may be cured of diabetes if your stomach acids become drastically alkaline, as it happens after underoing gastric bypass surgery. To solve the riddle of diabetes and insulin resistance, you need to look above the molecular level and focus less on what individually these molecules do. 8.5% of U.S. population is diagnosed as diabetic; something very common is happening in these people, and it could be something as simple as acid-alkaline balance.

[http://www.uptodate.com/contents/structure-and-function-of-the-insulin-receptor](http://http://www.uptodate.com/contents/structure-and-function-of-the-insulin-receptor)

Receptor gene and mRNA — The insulin receptor gene maps to human chromosome 19 and spans more than 150 kilobases (kb) \[8\]. The 22 exons of the receptor gene are transcribed into several mRNA species ranging from 4.2 to 9.5 kb in length due to variation in their 3'-untranslated regions \[9\]. Exon 11, which encodes a 12 amino acid segment localized to the C-terminus of the α-subunit, is subject to tissue-specific alternative splicing in a pattern conserved between humans and lower mammals (figure 1) \[10-12\].

**The abundance of receptor mRNA and protein are up-regulated by differentiation of adipocyte and muscle precursor cells as they acquire an insulin-sensitive phenotype** \[13\]. In some cells, exposure to insulin reduces receptor mRNA abundance, which may play a role in regulation of receptor number in vivo \[14,15\]. In rare cases of severe insulin resistance due to mutations in the receptor gene, extreme reduction in receptor abundance has been shown \[16\]. However, altered receptor abundance is not believed to play a major role in common forms of insulin resistance in obesity or type 2 diabetes.

The primary translation product of the insulin receptor mRNA is a linear α-ÃŸ sequence of the insulin receptor precursor (proreceptor). The signal sequence of 27 hydrophobic amino acids at the N-terminus of the α-subunit allows the receptor to enter the endoplasmic reticulum, during which process the signal peptide is cleaved. The proreceptor is further proteolytically-processed by furin in the Golgi apparatus into distinct α and ÃŸ-subunits at a cleavage site consisting of four basic amino acids (Arg-Lys-Arg-Arg) \[17-20\], apparently after disulfide linkage of two proreceptor molecules \[21\]

I think the highlighted section implies a BAT link with the receptors.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T03:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/methionine-deficient-diet-increases-lifespan-leucine-deprivation-increases-thermog.796/post-16434>

[http://care.diabetesjournals.org/content/34/7/1676](http://http://care.diabetesjournals.org/content/34/7/1676) Iron and Diabetes Revisited...My money's on the iron....junking the insulin receptors...just have to find out. I know it can be nasty in the liver.

Iron is the most abundant transitional metal in the body. It has long been recognized that iron overload can increase the risk of diabetes, particularly in iron-overload states such as hemochromatosis and recurrent transfusions in diseases like thalassemia. Furthermore, a large body of epidemiological evidence suggests that an increase in dietary iron (as heme, mainly from meat and meat products) is associated with an increased risk of diabetes (1). In contrast, iron deficiency (over time one of the most common nutritional deficiencies in the world) may lower the risk of diabetes. Indeed, it has been suggested that recurrent phlebotomy may protect against diabetes (2), although there are no large multicenter, randomized controlled trials to support this hypothesis. In addition, iron has been implicated in the pathogenesis of renal disease including diabetic nephropathy (1).

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T04:01:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/methionine-deficient-diet-increases-lifespan-leucine-deprivation-increases-thermog.796/post-16435>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16225476](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16225476) Iron and insulin resistance.

Abstract

BACKGROUND:

**Preliminary clinical and experimental results suggest that iron can modify hepatocytes' insulin sensitivity by interfering with insulin receptor and intracellular insulin signalling.

AIM:

To evaluate in vivo the influence of iron on insulin resistance and insulin release in patients with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and in vitro the interaction between iron and insulin sensitivity by analysing the effect of iron manipulation on insulin receptor expression in hepatoblastoma HepG2 cell line.

RESULTS:

Insulin resistance evaluated by homeostatis model assessment (HOMA)-insulin resistance significantly decreased after diet, and a further reduction was observed after phlebotomies. Iron depletion by desferrioxamine increased by twofold the 125I-insulin-specific binding, whereas iron addition reduced insulin binding, similarly to cells exposed to high glucose concentration.

CONCLUSION:

Iron status affects insulin sensitivity by modulating the transcription and membrane expression/affinity of insulin receptor expression in hepatocytes and influencing insulin-dependent gene expression suggesting that increased insulin clearance and decreased insulin resistance may contribute to the positive effect of iron depletion in patients with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease.**

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T18:30:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/methionine-deficient-diet-increases-lifespan-leucine-deprivation-increases-thermog.796/post-16610>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18061940](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18061940) Obesity, bariatric surgery, and iron deficiency: true, true, true and related.

Morbid obesity is a health problem that has been shown to be refractory to diet, exercise, and medical treatment. Surgeries designed to promote weight loss, termed bariatric surgery and typically involving a gastric bypass procedure, have recently been implemented to treat obesity with high success rates. However, long-term sequelae can result in micronutrient deficiencies. This review will focus on iron deficiency and its association with obesity and bariatric surgery. Iron deficiency develops after gastric bypass for several reasons including intolerance for red meat, diminished gastric acid secretion, and exclusion of the duodenum from the alimentary tract. Menstruating women, pregnant women, and adolescents may be particularly predisposed toward developing iron deficiency and microcytic anemias after bypass surgery. Preoperative assessment of patients should include a complete hematological work-up, including measurement of iron stores. Postoperatively, oral iron prophylaxis and vitamin C in addition to a multivitamin should be prescribed for bypass patients, especially for vulnerable populations. Once iron deficiency has developed, it may prove refractory to oral treatment, and require parenteral iron, blood transfusions, or surgical interventions. ****Bariatric surgery patients require lifelong follow-up of hematological and iron parameters since iron deficiency and anemia may develop years after surgery.****

### chocolate — 2012-04-25T14:19:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/methionine-deficient-diet-increases-lifespan-leucine-deprivation-increases-thermog.796/post-18660>

[http://www.pepck-and-the-ketogenic-diet.com/branched-chain-amino-acids-bcaa-epilepsy.html](http://http://www.pepck-and-the-ketogenic-diet.com/branched-chain-amino-acids-bcaa-epilepsy.html)

When considering human nutrition, the branched chain amino acids we are talking are leucine, isoleucine, and valine. These amino acids are considered essential to the human diet since they cannot be made from non-protein precursors. **BCAA’s rapidly cross the blood brain barrier, which is not true of all amino acids. And one of their important roles appears to be in the glumatame/glutamine shuttle between neurons and astrocytes. In addition, BCCA’s contribute to anaplerosis.** This is especially important under conditions when cellular pyruvate concentrations are low as would be expected during the ketogenic diet. One of the predictions from my model of the ketogenic diet is that increasing the availability of amino acids in the brain that contribute to anaplerosis would increase the flux through PEPCK and thus increase the efficacy of the diet

I'm thinking BCAA's gotta happen.

Genetic Regulation of Metabolomic Biomarkers: Paths to Cardiovascular Diseases and Type 2 Diabetes

ScienceDaily (Jan. 29, 2012) â€” The research group at the Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland (FIMM) has revealed eleven new genetic regions associated with the blood levels of the metabolites, including new loci affecting well-established risk markers for cardiovascular disease and potential biomarkers for type 2 diabetes.

### chocolate — 2012-05-12T05:31:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/methionine-deficient-diet-increases-lifespan-leucine-deprivation-increases-thermog.796/post-23340>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21899736](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21899736)

Leucine supplementation improves adiponectin and total cholesterol concentrations despite the lack of changes in adiposity or glucose homeostasis in rats previously exposed to a high-fat diet.

Abstract

ABSTRACT:

BACKGROUND:

Studies suggest that leucine supplementation (LS) has a therapeutic potential to prevent obesity and to promote glucose homeostasis. Furthermore, regular physical exercise is a widely accepted strategy for body weight maintenance and also for the prevention of obesity. The aim of this study was to determine the effect of chronic LS alone or combined with endurance training (ET) as potential approaches for reversing the insulin resistance and obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) in rats.

METHODS:

Forty-seven rats were randomly divided into two groups. Animals were fed a control diet-low fat (n = 10) or HFD (n = 37). After 15 weeks on HFD, all rats received the control diet-low fat and were randomly divided according to treatment: reference (REF), LS, ET, and LS+ET (n = 7-8 rats per group). After 6 weeks of treatment, the animals were sacrificed and body composition, fat cell volume, and serum concentrations of total cholesterol, HDL-cholesterol, triacylglycerol, glucose, adiponectin, leptin and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) were analyzed.

RESULTS:

At the end of the sixth week of treatment, there was no significant difference in body weight between the REF, LS, ET and LS+ET groups. However, ET increased lean body mass in rats (P = 0.019). In addition, ET was more effective than LS in reducing adiposity (P = 0.019), serum insulin (P = 0.022) and TNF-α (P = 0.044). Conversely, LS increased serum adiponectin (P = 0.021) levels and reduced serum total cholesterol concentration (P = 0.042).

CONCLUSIONS:

**The results showed that LS had no beneficial effects on insulin sensitivity or adiposity in previously obese rats. On the other hand, LS was effective in increasing adiponectin levels and in reducing total cholesterol concentration.**

### chocolate — 2012-07-15T01:59:47+0300

[http://webhome.idirect.com/~wolfnowl/thyroid13.htm](http://http://webhome.idirect.com/~wolfnowl/thyroid13.htm)

> BRANCHED CHAIN AMINO ACIDS (BCAAs)
>

>
> **Leucine, isoleucine, and valine.
>
> Compete with tyrosine for absorption, so increasing BCAAs may decrease tyrosine absorption and thereby decrease production of the thyroid and catecholamine hormones.)**
>
>
>
> HYPERS: Beneficial, especially for exercise, sports, and body building. Does not seem to cause hyper symptoms like other protein supplements.
>
>
>
> HYPOS: Unknown.

I'm searching for this.

### chocolate — 2012-07-15T02:07:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/methionine-deficient-diet-increases-lifespan-leucine-deprivation-increases-thermog.796/post-37885>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16788992](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16788992)

> A dual tyrosine-leucine motif mediates myelin protein P0 targeting in MDCK cells.
>
> Kidd GJ, Yadav VK, Huang P, Brand SL, Low SH, Weimbs T, Trapp BD.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Neurosciences, Lerner Research Institute, Cleveland Clinic Foundation, Cleveland, Ohio 44195, USA. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Differential targeting of myelin proteins to multiple, biochemically and functionally distinct Schwann cell plasma membrane domains is essential for myelin formation. In this study, we investigated whether the myelin protein P0 contains targeting signals using Madin-Darby canine kidney (MDCK) cells. By confocal microscopy, P0 was localized to MDCK cell basolateral membranes. C-terminal deletion resulted in apical accumulation, and stepwise deletions defined a 15-mer region that was required for basolateral targeting. **Alanine substitutions within this region identified the YAML sequence as a functional tyrosine-based targeting signal, with the ML sequence serving as a **secondary leucine-based signal. Replacement of the P0 ectodomain with green fluorescent protein altered the distribution of constructs lacking the YAML signal. Coexpression of the myelin-associated glycoprotein did not alter P0 distribution in MDCK cells. The results** indicate that P0 contains a hierarchy of targeting signals**, which may contribute to P0 localization in myelinating Schwann cells and the pathogenesis in human disease.
>
> Copyright 2006 Wiley-Liss, Inc

The highlight in box number twenty one isn't true in a dog's kidney.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-11T15:15:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-13706>

Okay, this is really simple.

This part of the forum is pretty much parents and mostly Mom's. I am detecting a lot of grief over the things we've fed our kids in the name of "health".

#1 We do the best we can with what we have

#2 Harboring bad feelings raises cortisol

#3 Bad feeling start to leak out on your kids

So leave them here.

Write it down and let it go.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-11T15:17:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-13707>

Gold fish crackers. I wish I could pluck back every single last one I ever gave a child. And remember I have FIVE... some who are now in college.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-11T18:25:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-13781>

Margarine, sunflower oil and soya products when we were told my OH and middle daughter were to avoid lactose. I so wish that I hadn't poisoned them with the stuff. I wonder whether some of my middle and youngest daughters' health problems started there, and whether we'll be able to reverse them.

On the other hand, I am truly thankful that my OH, my eldest, my youngest and myself are all non-coeliac gluten-intolerant, which means I have an excuse to never have grain-containing foods in the house!

### Sem — 2012-04-13T07:37:31+0300

I feel guilty about that but my older son was SO emotional when I told him we were cutting out gluten. He has nut allergies and is always stressed about whether food has nuts in it. I'm hoping to slowly cut out all grains but worry about him getting too anxious and not eating at all especially since the doctor told me after his eight year old check up last week that he grew two inches in a year but LOST half a pound. She wasn't thrilled about that and either am I...

I'm proud of the fact that my kids eat sushi as take-away and not McDonalds, and they ahve never had softd rinks, just filtered water. So thats one thing I got right!

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-13T17:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-14720>

oh the guilt. w/dd it goes back to not being able to nurse her directly, and having to pump for her, and not make enough, requiring supplementation w/ formula (didn't know about milk shares and the like w/her). Its the juice (I'm still trying to get both kids off of it - its watered down to 10% juice/90% water) Its not being able to control what the eat a dcp - b/c of the state/fed regulations that say they have to x of this, that and the other (w/in the usda food pyramid/plate - which is total crap)

its trying to fend of the grandparents - of its ok if they have x it won't kill them (well, maybe not initially but eventually)

its trying to not make them feel like they're so different from the other kids at school b/c we eat different.

Oh how I wish I could afford to home school, or work from home and have them w/me throughout the day....but neither is feasible...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-14T00:25:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-14885>

Guilt is something I've thought a lot about (mostly since becoming a mother!). The guilt over things I've gotten wrong extends far beyond food/healthy living. And it is daily. However, one of my spiritual teachers once spoke about the difference between guilt and remorse. Guilt, she said, was self-focussed; remorse was action-focussed... a resolution to not repeat the mistake and a full acceptance of responsibility or reality. That helped me.

I am never going to get everything right. And I'm not even sure there is a 'right'. We wake up to new information as we receive it and we do the best we can. (If you look at the attachment theory literature, for example, the key to healthy mother-child relationships is NOT the misattunements and when things go wrong, it's the capacity of the relationship to repair and come back to harmony/synchrony.)

Every day I wonder if I am doing the right thing vis-a-vis food and diet. Do I need to be 100% unflinching in my refusal to let my children have the occasional bit of grains? Do I have to tell them they cannot accept a cookie from their friend's mother while at a slumber party? I really don't know. I know what Dr Kruse says, but I am not Dr Kruse. My children still eat some very non-optimal things. And I am still a very non-optimal mother on many occasions. This is not the 80-20 rule; it's the reality of my imperfect life.

A friend's four-year old was hit by a car when they were walking home from the library. He was killed instantly. We don't know what life has in store and we are not in control of everything, as much as we'd like to me. I want a loving, happy, healthy relationship with my children. Even if it is full of mistakes.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-14T17:35:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15064>

I'm fortunate in that by the time I realized what I had done to my children (their cheekbones & arches & ears & tics & teeth (or lack of them) tell the story), I had already learned how to deal with my past.

I will have to face my mistakes every time I look at my children's close-together eyes or missing teeth in a toddler. I'm no longer able to try to avoid seeing these things. The pouring into them of gobs of "healthy whole grain cereal" for breakfast, lunch, & snacks; the obscene quantities of sugar, wheat, & PUFAs that went into me when gestating and nursing them ... But, in the words of I believe Maya Angelou, _**you do what you know. When you know better, you do better.**_

As long as we do that, there's no room for guilt, IMO. End of story. (Besides, they're a LOT better off than I was, a lot better off than they would be had I not done my best, and they're equipped with the information to change my grandchildren forever.)

> I want a loving, happy, healthy relationship with my children. Even if it is full of mistakes.

And yes.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-14T17:42:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15066>

1\. i have no guilt........I did what I did out of ignorance. I no longer tolerate any mediocrity. It in turn eliminates ignorance.

2\. I have no bad feelings because I find my greatest success in all my failures.

3\. My kids get worked by a Spartan father......I give them no quarter either. They want success they are learning Mother Natures rules too.......Modern parents beliefs are ruining kids. Let mother Nature toughen them by her standards and not your neolithic thoughts socialized by modern hominids.......

Why let it go........Mother Nature says evolve.......tomorrow I will have new challenges to face.

Bring the heat.......in it I get better.

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-14T19:18:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15080>

I am a mother, and grandmother. My children were born in the early 80's, and I cringe at what I fed them as a steady diet, ignoramous that I was. Lots of cereals, ramen noodles, pasta, and breads. I did the best I could with what I knew at the time. Now I know differently, raised those kids to think for themselves... I'm trying to share my new found knowledge but the best thing I am sharing is my success, setting an example. They see the changes in me and I hope it will trigger something in their minds, because I can't snap my fingers and change the mistakes I made with them in the past. When the kids or grandkids come to visit, they are served real food and so far, no complaints.

Yes my remorse is great, because I now know that I set certain rocks rolling even before my children were born, that affect even my grandchildren. However, my hope is also great, because I am living proof that change is possible.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-14T22:51:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15128>

Soy, is my biggest guilt trip, DS was born lactose and casein intolerance, nursed until 1 year and then onto soy milk for another year.

I cringe everytime I think of feeding a boy soy milk at that age fortunately he weaned himself from soy at about 2 or 3, but then it was water and juice . he\`s tall (6\`4\`\`), healthy, athletic and smart at 17 but I just hope with the changes we\`ve been making the past 4 years that some stuff has been ameliorated.

For DD it\`s been the carbs, ramen noodles is the worst. we used to do Rotten Ronnies but that stopped 18 months ago.

What I did good, 2 years ago tossed all PUFAs from the house brought in lard, butter and CO interestingly enough both my kids went from doing mediocre in school to honor roll for DS and much improved for DD.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-15T06:14:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15238>

One thing I did a few months ago when I was feeling guilty about what I fed them as small children, I went up to my now grown children and apologized for what I did. I told them that I though it was the right thing to do back then, but now I knew it wasn't.

They both looked at me as if I was nuts, and I said someday you will understand. I just want you to remember then that I am sorry for what I did to you.

After that, I haven't had any more guilt about it.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-15T07:17:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15246>

> **Merri;15307 wrote:** One thing I did a few months ago when I was feeling guilty about what I fed them as small children, I went up to my now grown children and apologized for what I did. I told them that I though it was the right thing to do back then, but now I knew it wasn't.
>
>
>
> They both looked at me as if I was nuts, and I said someday you will understand. I just want you to remember then that I am sorry for what I did to you.
>
>
>
> After that, I haven't had any more guilt about it.

I had completely forgotten, but we did the same when we went paleo (switching from a vegetarian diet! - neither of my children had ever eaten a bite of meat, yikes!). We told them we did what we thought was best but had no learned new info. And that we were really sorry we'd gotten it all wrong but we are going to make it right now. We explained to our youngest that it was like learning to spell a word wrong and then spelling it that way for the rest of your life, never realising you had it wrong. Then, once you're corrected, you can't go back and correct all the times you'd misspelled it, but you could start spelling it correctly. So, in one week, out went every can of beans/chickpeas etc along with all the bread and pasta and all the oils. In came the truckloads of meat and fish and CO and butter. Talk about culture shock.

My kids STILL look at me like I'm nuts. But they're eating our new crazy food and feeling the better for it!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-16T03:03:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15446>

I had a lifetime of wrong and endless stories of things pre-baby to feel guilty about, but now he's here (almost 6) - I look at mine every day and I think, HOLY \*\*\*\* HOW COULD THIS AWESOME THING BE A PRODUCT OF ME.

It made me realize nature will find a way. It's not the guilt I think about, but the hope that things can be changed because he's so awesome now after a very rocky first 18 months. (did I mention he (me) didn't sleep more than 2 hrs in a row till almost 1.5 yrs) and know that we are plastic. brains have been rewired. He's a rock-star now and the only thing wrong w him is a scrape or fall now and then.

On days like today - that understanding that THINGS CAN BE CHANGED is what I have to focus on. It was a rough day, Kid bday party with pizza and cake and juice. I drew the line for the 2nd party in a row, and many other parents were clearly scorning me and pitying him.

It brought back all those awful personal angst things from growing up the fat kid and not fitting in - but that's about me, not about him and I pushed it aside and soldiered forth. What I'm not sure about is if I want to keep doing kid bday parties. My instinct is to NOT engage in situations that suck. Is that ritual of childhood really going to be important to him and his well being? I don't know. I'm a total introvert - and he is a complete social animal, so probably what I should do is engineer social situations that support our choices.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-16T03:44:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15468>

never settle.......moderation is for mediocre.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-16T05:18:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15498>

> **Cavemam;15515 wrote:**
>
> Is that ritual of childhood really going to be important to him and his well being? I don't know. I'm a total introvert - and he is a complete social animal, so probably what I should do is engineer social situations that support our choices.

However... if he's the total social animal you say, maybe he can totally enjoy the rubbish ritual without eating cake etc. I tell my kids, just blame it on me, and they go to events (latest was the movies... I was the only mother not supplying cash for popcorn and candy... my daughter got exact change for her movie ticket) and enjoy them in spite of their mother! (It's just me who needs to avoid the awkward social situations ; they seem to cope fine. But maybe this is because I used to be the weird vegetarian mother who didn't let them eat the chicken nuggets and hotdogs.)

And well done for soldiering on. Those mother-cliques are excrutiating and it's really hard not to cave in an effort to be 'liked' (whatever that means.)

### janagram — 2012-04-16T05:28:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15502>

Ok, my kids were raised on a darn healthy diet (never had sodas...didn't like them) until I went vegetarian bringing in yrs of soy and grains and beans....I guess wknds at their dads (who knows what they ate but probably meat) helped a bit.

I feel so bad....tofu and more tofu! But as we are all feeling, its over now and we have learned from our mistakes....my son told me he saw an old hippie bumpersticker that said "Meat is Murder" with the M crossed out and WH written over it: "Wheat is Murder!" ### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-16T13:51:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guilt-thread.802/post-15535>

> **Cavemam;15515 wrote:**
>
>
>
> On days like today - that understanding that THINGS CAN BE CHANGED is what I have to focus on. It was a rough day, Kid bday party with pizza and cake and juice. I drew the line for the 2nd party in a row, and many other parents were clearly scorning me and pitying him.
>
>
>
> It brought back all those awful personal angst things from growing up the fat kid and not fitting in - but that's about me, not about him and I pushed it aside and soldiered forth. What I'm not sure about is if I want to keep doing kid bday parties. My instinct is to NOT engage in situations that suck. Is that ritual of childhood really going to be important to him and his well being? I don't know. I'm a total introvert - and he is a complete social animal, so probably what I should do is engineer social situations that support our choices.

I appreciate your angst. My little guy is socially challenged and so letting him get out there is really important. He gets invited to almost no birthday parties now - in 3rd grade. I totally let the Sunday is for family rule slide on Easter Sunday of all things because some neighborhood boys rang the bell asking him to come out and play. What a gift.... which fortunately didn't involve food. As bad as it is, my little guy has a genetic disorder, observable social and motor challenges, and a serious weight gain issue. So when they do include him, people are quick to ask me about how "special handling" procedures.

On the specifics of bday parties, develop your own rules. My now college age kids were allowed to go to 3-4 a year and they had to be for their BFF's. A bday party is a feast/celebration, but you can't have a continual party life. And kids need to learn that they can't do everything sooner or later. I would suggest that you really emphasize the playdates for the next couple of years - whether they be out of your homes where food is not an issue, or in your home where you carefully select who comes to play. Of course first it needs to be a child your child desires to have visit, but if you are not particularly comfortable with parents, that is not to be discounted either. Fruit doesn't fall far from the tree. So yes, I would do a little social engineering. If it is on your home court, you can be a Paleo witness, without coming across as a street corner evangelist proclaiming "the end is near if you eat grains" (never mind the truth of the matter) It is all about customizing our message to the audience. To borrow from St. Francis of Assisi - who obviously was into nature - "Preach always. If necessary, use words."

And don't worry either about the flooding memories of your childhood. This tall, lanky 4 eyed kid that was always picked last for dodgeball, had the same thoughts the first time my kid was "left out". You are just more sensitive to the situation. Use it to your advantage.

I have 3 college age kids right now who probably are a major source of my elevated cortisol over the last few years. They buck the system, tell you things like "there are more educated people out there who would disagree with you", and of course complain about what is served for dinner. But mark my words, the "thank you's" are prolific these days. My favorite to date was the one who had to stay for summer semester, and didn't have a meal plan. Thrust into the world of grocery shopping for herself. "Food is expensive, but you and Dad still buy the good stuff." Stay the course. Other ships in the harbor will follow...

### chocolate — 2012-04-11T19:58:39+0300

### chocolate — 2012-04-20T01:36:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/random-article.809/post-16785>

[http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn21720-move-over-dna-six-new-molecules-can-carry-genes.html](http://http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn21720-move-over-dna-six-new-molecules-can-carry-genes.html)

Move over DNA: Six new molecules can carry genes

All of a sudden, DNA has no reason to feel special. For decades it seemed that only a handful of molecules could store genetic information and pass it on. But now synthetic biologists have discovered that six others can pull off the same trick, and there may be many more to find.

The ability to copy information from one molecule to another is fundamental to all life. Organisms pass their genes to their descendants, often with small changes, and as a result life can evolve over the generations. Barring a few exceptions, all known organisms use DNA as the information carrier.

A host of alternative nucleic acids have been made in labs over the years, but no one has made them work like DNA.

This problem has now been cracked. "This unique ability of DNA and RNA to encode information can be implemented in other backbones," says Philipp Holliger of the MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology in Cambridge, UK.
